author: gsihaj5

title: Legacy classes cannot be extended
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Some classes were renamed or replaced by other classes in r138 and legacy wrapper functions were added for backward-compatibility. These functions print a deprecation warning, then use the new class to create an object and finally _return_ it. This is problematic because returning an object from a constructor function messes with the prototype chain and circumvents the `this` object. The result is that any other properties and methods defined in subclasses will be lost.

A lot of the legacy wrappers were added many years ago and can probably be left as-is. The one I'm struggling with now is `DataTexture3D`. The problem is that I can't just use the new `Data3DTexture` because I want my code to support r138 _and_ older versions of `three`.

For completeness' sake, I'll list all affected classes: [PointCloud, Particle, ParticleSystem, PointCloudMaterial, ParticleBasicMaterial, ParticleSystemMaterial, Vertex, DynamicBufferAttribute, Int8Attribute, Uint8Attribute, Uint8ClampedAttribute, Int16Attribute, Uint16Attribute, Int32Attribute, Uint32Attribute, Float32Attribute, Float64Attribute](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L113-L232), [AxisHelper, BoundingBoxHelper, EdgesHelper](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L259-L278), [WireframeHelper](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L292-L297), [WebGLRenderTargetCube](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L1671-L1676), [XHRLoader, BinaryTextureLoader](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L324-L336), [ParametricGeometry and TextGeometry](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L1959-L1971), [WebGLMultisampleRenderTarget, DataTexture2DArray, DataTexture3D](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/62c6fabc5e9c948ffd984192ed8588068f8253ee/src/Three.Legacy.js#L1991-L2012).

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Extend `DataTexture3D` and define a method `foo`
2. Use the subclass to create a `texture` and call `texture.foo()`.
3. See error `TypeError: texture.foo is not a function`.

**Code Example**

```js
function A() {

	return {};

}

class B extends A {

	foo() {}

}

const b = new B();
b.foo();
```

**Expected behavior**

The legacy classes should not break existing code. In this particular case, _subclassing_ is broken for the specific classes mentioned above. To make this more robust, the legacy classes could instead extend the new classes:

```js
// Current approach
export function DataTexture3D( data, width, height, depth ) {

	console.warn( 'THREE.DataTexture3D has been renamed to Data3DTexture.' );
	return new Data3DTexture( data, width, height, depth );

}

// Suggested approach
export class DataTexture3D extends Data3DTexture {

	constructor( data, width, height, depth ) {

		console.warn( 'THREE.DataTexture3D has been renamed to Data3DTexture.' );
		super( data, width, height, depth );

	}

}
```

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Linux
 - Browser: Firefox
 - Three.js version: r138
author: vanruesc

title: Roadmap for a color-managed workflow in three.js
body:
## Overview

Summary issue to organize and track progress toward offering a color-managed workflow in three.js. Loosely based on Maya's documentation, I'll define color management as follows:

> Color management involves applying the appropriate transforms to convert between color spaces as needed. These transforms are applied at specific points along the initialization, rendering, and display processes. For example:
> 
> - On input, a transform is applied to convert colors and textures from the color space in which they were saved into the working color space.
> - As you work, colors in the working color space may be converted to/from other color spaces in order to accommodate bit depth and precision considerations.
> - On output to an image or display, an output transform is applied to convert colors appropriately for the image format or browser and device capabilities.

## Roadmap

1. [x] **(1.1)** Add documentation of color management APIs (@donmccurdy)
    - [x] https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23430
2. [x] **(1.2)** Loaders identify and convert color spaces correctly (@gkjohnson)
    - [x] https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23283
3. [x] **(1.3)** Rename "encoding" properties more precisely as "colorSpace" (@donmccurdy + @Mugen87)
    - [x] `renderer.outputEncoding` → `renderer.outputColorSpace` 
        - https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23936
        - https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25756
    - [x] `texture.encoding` → `texture.colorSpace`
        - https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25771
4. [x] **(1.4)** Change output color space default from Linear-sRGB to sRGB (@donmccurdy)
    - [x] https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25783
5. [x] **(1.5)** Convert some inputs automatically from sRGB to Linear-sRGB (@donmccurdy)
    - [x] hexadecimal and CSS inputs: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23392
6. [x] **(1.6)** Change `texture.colorSpace` default to `THREE.NoColorSpace` (e.g. normal maps)
    - https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25771
7. [x] **(1.7)** ~~Ensure intermediate frame buffers have sufficient bit depth for their color space.~~ See https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23251#issuecomment-1014606909. No changes required.
8. [x] **(1.8)**  Let `CubeTextureLoader` return sRGB cube textures by default.
    - [x] #26162
 9. [x] **(1.9)** Change `renderer.useLegacyLights` default to `false`
    - [x] #26392

_**Timing considerations:** If we are doing (1.3), (1.4), and (1.5), it would cause less disruption to existing code if we can make these changes within the same release. Fewer changes, if any, will be required for end-user code._

## Future

_These changes are more speculative, and may be considered explorative steps toward support for wide-gamut color spaces in WebGPU and SVG renderers._

1. [ ] **(2.1)** Support changing `ColorManagement.workingColorSpace`
2. [ ] **(2.2)** Support color input/output in Display P3 color space
3. [ ] **(3.3)** Support vertex color workflow changes if necessary (`ColorBufferAttribute`?)

_Continued in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/26479_.

title: Value supplied instead of a key in WebGLShaderCache.remove
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

I think this line is wrong:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9b86245eafd2c71ace83688ad0f20de1750f2968/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLShaderCache.js#L48

`shaderCache.delete` should get a key (shader code), but it is called with a value (`WebGLShaderStage`) instead.

Evidence of the shaderCache type is at https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9b86245eafd2c71ace83688ad0f20de1750f2968/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLShaderCache.js#L91-L102:

Here you can see the map is indexed with code, while `WebGLShaderStage` are values.

I am afraid a proper fix is not trivial, as the key is not available at that point. A simple hotfix would be to search through the map values to get it. If such fix is acceptable, I can prepare a PR, if not, some more extensive restructuring would be probably required.
author: OndrejSpanel

title: Avoid bare imports other than 'three'
body:
Related:

- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23560

With r138 the following syntax was introduced:

```js
import { NormalNode } from 'three-nodes/Nodes.js';
```

I do not think we should be using bare imports (except `three`) unless we are publishing it to a separate NPM package. Bundlers cannot resolve `three-nodes` without custom resolution configuration, and we're seeing confusion about this in Discord. By contrast, anything under the `three` import is fine if we configure it in package.json exports:

```js
import { NormalNode } from 'three/nodes/Nodes.js';
```

/cc @sunag

author: elalish

title: Unify constructor() and set() in Matrix and Color classes
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently, the Matrix and Color classes have the constructor() and set() methods accepting different arguments.

It means that, for example, `new Vector3(x, y, z)` and `new Vector3().set(x, y, z)` will produce the same results, while Matrix and Color will not - Matrix constructor does not accept any arguments (set() accepts), while Color set() method does not support multiple arguments (constructor supports).

One of the problems arising from this is a problem in the ConvertType in the ShaderNode.js in the Nodes system - if it will always use set() method `new class().set( ...args )`, it would not always work with Color, and if it will always use constructor `new class( ...args )`, it will not work with Matrix classes.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I suggest allowing Matrix constructor to take arguments and allowing Color set() method to take multiple arguments.

title: WebGPUTextureRenderer: Get Depth buffer as TextureNode
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

For advanced post-processing with a fullscreen quad like in the webgpu_rtt.html example, it is also necessary to have access to the depth buffer of the Rendertarget from the WebGPUTextureRenderer. If I did not miss anything, it is currently not possible to access the depth buffer.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

It would be great to have something for the WebGPUTextureRenderer like:

`let depthTextureNode = new Nodes.TextureNode( textureRenderer.getDepthTexture() );`

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Similar as in the webgl_depth_texture.html example, I tried to get a `DepthTexture `from a RenderTarget, which didn´t work:

```
    //create a WebGPUTextureRenderer and set a DepthTexture
    textureRenderer = new WebGPUTextureRenderer(renderer);
    textureRenderer.setSize(window.innerWidth * window.devicePixelRatio, window.innerHeight * window.devicePixelRatio);

    let depthTexture = new THREE.DepthTexture();
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.texture.minFilter = THREE.NearestFilter;
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.texture.magFilter = THREE.NearestFilter;
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.stencilBuffer = false;
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.depthTexture = depthTexture;
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.depthTexture.format = THREE.DepthFormat;
    textureRenderer.renderTarget.depthTexture.type = THREE.UnsignedShortType;

    // set up FX
    cameraFX = new THREE.OrthographicCamera(-1, 1, 1, -1, 0, 1);
    sceneFX = new THREE.Scene();
    const geometryFX = new THREE.PlaneGeometry(2, 2);
    const materialFX = new Nodes.MeshBasicNodeMaterial({
        side: THREE.DoubleSide
    });

    // this returns an error: "Multiple aspects selected in [TextureView] While validating [BindGroupDescriptor] against [BindGroupLayout]"
    materialFX.colorNode = new Nodes.OperatorNode( '*', new Nodes.TextureNode( textureRenderer.renderTarget.depthTexture ), new Nodes.ColorNode( new THREE.Color("green") ) );
    
    const quad = new THREE.Mesh(geometryFX, materialFX);
    sceneFX.add(quad);
```

**Additional context**

@sunag , maybe you could have a look at this? Many thanks in advance!

title: Convert remaining nodes and examples that are using the Nodes classes directly to using the ShaderNode functions
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently some nodes and examples use the Nodes classes directly, while others use them through the ShaderNode functions. The ShaderNode approach is much simpler and easier to use than the direct - e.g. compare `new OperatorNode('*', new MathNode(MathNode.SIN, value1), new MathNode(MathNode.COS, value2))` and `mul(sin(value1), cos(value2))`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I suggest that the remaining nodes and examples that are using the Nodes classes directly will be converted to using the ShaderNode functions.
This will require https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23564.

author: lavond

title: Implement Bounding Boxes in Groups to improve the frustum culling performance.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I'm struggling with a poor performance when certain number of meshes are being rendered.

Doing some research about the inner working of the threejs pipeline, I found that in the render process is being performed a filtering with the purpose of renders only visible objects laying within the camera frustum. But despite to be nesting the meshes in groups, the process of filtering is performed only in Mesh, Line or Point objects.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Could be interesting to implement the possibility of computing bounding boxes in Groups to avoid to check their children if them are laying outside the camera frustum.

For example if you have a scene with 100 groups of 10 meshes each, with the actual way there are performed a 1000 of checks to know what meshes are laying within the camera frustum. Implementing bounding boxes in groups, only 100 checks would be needed to know what groups are laying within the camera frustum and then checks the children of groups which tested positive.

The bounding box of the group could be updated by the user or each time a children is added or removed, performing an union with the children's bounding box.

And the same could be applied in Raycaster

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I have structured the scene in chunks, so my idea is to perform a pre filter before call the render method.

**Additional context**

-

title: MathUtils.generateUUID() produces upper-case characters
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Three.js UUID generation logic outputs upper-case characters, while UUID spec ([RFC 4122](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4122)) requires output to be lower-case.

Quote from RFC:

> Each field is treated as an integer and has its value printed as a
> zero-filled hexadecimal digit string with the most significant
> digit first.  The hexadecimal values "a" through "f" are output as
> lower case characters and are case insensitive on input.

[This StackOverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/13833488/5082182) also points to "INTERNATIONAL STANDARD ISO/IEC 9834-8 RECOMMENDATION ITU-T X.667" which states (emphasis mine):

> 6.5.4 Software generating the hexadecimal representation of a UUID **shall not use upper case letters**. NOTE – It is recommended that the hexadecimal representation used in all human-readable formats be restricted to lower-case letters. Software processing this representation is, however, required to accept both upper and lower case letters as specified in 6.5.2.

Other sources:
- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universally_unique_identifier): "RFC 4122 Section 3 requires that the characters be generated in lower case, while being case-insensitive on input."

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Call `MathUtils.generateUUID()`
2. Observe output to contain upper-case letters

**Expected behavior**

The generated UUID should only have lower-case characters, as per RFC

**Platform:**

 - Three.js version: r138

title: VTP parsing fails for DataArray with multiple components
body:
**Describe the bug**

Using VTKLoader to parse a VTP file containing DataArrays with multiple components
fails with a parsing error

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Save `face.vtp` that it attached to the bug [face.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8213689/face.zip)
2. Try to load it with `VTKLoader`

***Code***

```js
import { VTKLoader} from 'three/examples/jsm/loaders/VTKLoader.js';
// ...
var loader1 = new VTKLoader();
    loader1.load( 'face.vtp',
    function ( geometry ) {
});
```

3. 
Loading fails with the following error
```
VTKLoader.js?e3d9:42 Error: Invalid string. Length must be a multiple of 4
    at Base64toByteArray (VTKLoader.js?e3d9:661:1)
    at parseDataArray (VTKLoader.js?e3d9:741:1)
    at parseXML (VTKLoader.js?e3d9:928:1)
    at VTKLoader.parse (VTKLoader.js?e3d9:1135:1)
    at Object.eval [as onLoad] (VTKLoader.js?e3d9:32:1)
    at eval (three.module.js?5a89:39645:1)
```

**Expected behavior**

VTP file is loaded

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Linux
 - Browser: Chrome, Firefox
 - Three.js version: r138

**PR**

The issue is that in `parseDataArray`, rather than the usual/expected string, the data is in form of an array, containing 1 element (the compressed string). If using this first element as string, parsing works as expected. 
I hope you don't mind, I started a PR to fix this issue.
author: eino

title: After calling WebGLRenderer.resetState() subsequent render does not setup VertexAttributes
body:
**Describe the bug**

After calling `renderer.resetState()`, the next three.js render does not call `enableVertexAttribArray`, or `vertexAttribPointer`.

**To Reproduce**

In a situation where three.js is being used in conjunction with other webgl shaders on the same webgl context, if another shader calls `gl.enableVertexAttribArray` or `gl.vertexAttribPointer`, three.js does not reset that state on subsequent render calls even if `renderer.resetState()` is being called.

See live demo of the issue on the jsfiddle below.  The webgl shader is not actually doing anything, it is just there to demonstrate the effect of calling `gl.enableVertexAttribArray`.

Comment/uncomment lines 93-94 to demonstrate the issue.

https://jsfiddle.net/tom3000/us0bh6v1/127/

One workaround is to clear and re-create the scene every render call but obviously that's terrible for performance, so hopefully I can find a way to get three.js to re-set the vertex array bindings.

**Expected behavior**

`WebGLRenderer.resetState()` should clear the cached state for these buffers and make the necessary webgl calls on subsequent render.

I opened PR #23689 in an attempt to fix this myself, but the e2e testing caught two issues so I've closed it for now.

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: MacOS
 - Browser: Firefox
 - Three.js version: 0.138.0

title: VTKLoader: Loading fails for VTP fils containing DataArray of type="Int32"
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

If loading with VTKLoader a VTP file containing a DataArray of type Int32 fails with an exception.  

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Save cow.vtp contained in the archive [cow.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8222930/cow.zip)
2. Try to load it with VTKLoader:

```js
import { VTKLoader} from 'three/examples/jsm/loaders/VTKLoader.js';
// ...
var loader1 = new VTKLoader();
    loader1.load( 'cow.vtp',
    function ( geometry ) {
    }
);
```
Loading fails with the following exception
```
VTKLoader.js?0dde:42 TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'length')
    at parseXML (VTKLoader.js?0dde:1054:1)
    at VTKLoader.parse (VTKLoader.js?0dde:1136:1)
    at Object.eval [as onLoad] (VTKLoader.js?0dde:32:1)
    at eval (three.module.js?8475:39424:1)
```

**Investigation**

The issue is due to type "Int32" not being parsed, thus the buffer being empty when trying to read.
I prepared a fix to take those fields into account and allow of loading of the sample file, will propose it in a PR.

title: Rendering of large bufferGeometries is broken in recent chrome releases
body: **Describe the bug** In recent chrome releases, meshes with relatively large bufferGeometries (>= 11k vertices) are distorted. This is not always reproducible. However, I found out that after I run an example of a GPUComputationRenderer, the bug is 100% reproducible even if I close the GPUComputationRenderer example. **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behavior, in Chrome 98 or 99: 1. Go to https://jsfiddle.net/5Lgurtc6/1/ it's supposed to show a red square. 2. Now open a GPUComputationRenderer example, for ex: https://threejs.org/examples/?q=prot#webgl_gpgpu_protoplanet 3. Go back to the red square fiddle https://jsfiddle.net/5Lgurtc6/1/ and re-run the code. 4. Now you see that the red square is not rendered correctly. I couldn't reproduce the issue in Firefox or Chrome 91. **Expected behavior** Rendering of the bufferGeometry meshes shouldn't be distorted. **Screenshots** Expected Output (after running a GPUComputationRenderer example) **Platform:** - Device: [Macbook] - OS: [MacOS Monterey, MacOs Big sur] - Browser: [Chrome 98, Chrome 99] - Three.js version: [r128] - GPU: [AMD Radeon Pro 5300M]

title: Three.js import map not working
body:
Firefox and some other browsers (details below) have problems resolving the importmap. Error:
```
Uncaught TypeError: Error resolving module specifier “three”. Relative module specifiers must start with “./”, “../” or “/”.
```
The line it refers to is

```
import { ... } from /*'three'*/'blob:https://[URL]/c945bbba-09f5-454f-b2a4-508d3d70f89a';
```
which is the Firefox translation of

```
import { ... } from 'three';
```
referring to the import map in the index.html file:

```
	<!-- Import maps polyfill -->
	<!-- Remove this when import maps will be widely supported -->
	<script async src="https://unpkg.com/es-module-shims@1.3.6/dist/es-module-shims.js"></script>

	<script type="importmap">
		{
			"imports": {
				"three": "./vendor/three/build/three.module.js"
			}
		}
	</script>
```
This code works perfectly fine in Chrome Canary.

I have tested the [online version](https://badde.media/threejs/fungi/index.html) in Chrome, Chrome Canary, Chrome Mobile Android, Opera, Opera Mobile Android, Edge, and it worked. I’ve tested it in Firefox, Firefox Mobile Android, Chrome Mobile iOS, Safari Mobile, and it didn’t work - although other projects of mine did, like [this one](https://badde.media/threejs/shapes/index.html)

**To Reproduce**
Try [this link](https://badde.media/threejs/fungi/index.html) in Firefox, Firefox Mobile Android, Chrome Mobile iOS or Safari Mobile.

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to [this link](https://badde.media/threejs/fungi/index.html) 
2. Page should load
3. but doesn't. Or maybe partially, with errors like the black background staying black instead of becoming colored .

***Code***

```html
	<!-- Import maps polyfill -->
	<!-- Remove this when import maps will be widely supported -->
	<!-- <script async src="https://unpkg.com/es-module-shims@1.3.6/dist/es-module-shims.js"></script> -->

	<script type="importmap">
		{
			"imports": {
				"three": "./vendor/three/build/three.module.js"
			}
		}
	</script>
```
```js
import { Vector3, Object3D, Mesh, TextureLoader } from 'three';
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/hyok6tvj/)
* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/c5m1kazu/)
* THESE ^ two examples actually show the same error.

**Expected behavior**
When the site has loaded, hundred small colored spheres should spread out from the center inside of a black sphere where the camera also is located in. When one of these small spheres hit the big one, the small ones should disappear, the bigsphere should take the color of the hitting sphere (console.logged), and an inner sphere with wireframe shader should appear with the inverted color. In the middle, a patch of grass should occur where a mushroom grows out (mushroom /growing are WIP).

**Screenshots**
![Screenshot_20220310-103929_Opera](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19553316/157726950-4b1aa463-a5ad-4fe1-be40-19ba16a36838.jpg)
This screenshot shows the expected behaviour.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop, Mobile]
 - OS: [Windows, Android, iOS]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge] Please see above for further information.
 - Three.js version: REVISION = '137' stable build
 
 TB, I don't even think that it's a three.js bug, but probably one in the es-module-shims.js module.

author: clicktodev

title: NodeMaterial : can't extend NodeMaterials classes
body:
**Describe the bug**

Extending any of the NodeMaterial based material classes creates a broken and unusable material since r138. It gives me this error : `Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set properties of undefined (setting 'value')`.

**Fix the bug**

This problem occurs because the [constructor.name is used to determine the value of type](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/98985923afe4fe4030fce256b0cc0d02dc68696f/examples/jsm/renderers/nodes/materials/NodeMaterial.js#L10). This value will equal the name of the top-most class in the inheritance chain.

I believe setting it manually would be better (ex: `PointsNodeMaterial.prototype.type = 'PointsNodeMaterial';`).
Or using a boolean (ex: `PointsNodeMaterial.prototype.isPointsNodeMaterial = true;`) (like many other things in threejs) !

***Live example***

* [codepen-latest-release](https://codepen.io/wmcmurray/pen/oNpvamN?editors=1010) (line 50 👀 )

**Expected behavior**

Extending any ThreeJs material class should work out of the box.

**Platform:**

 - Device: all
 - OS: all
 - Browser: all
 - Three.js version: r138
author: wmcmurray

title: Loft NURBS
body:
In most of the common 3d applications, there aare different types of NURBS object, one of the most useful ones are Loft NURBS where one can define the shape of an objects by curves. For now, three.js supports only very few of these NURBS objects, and neither of them accepts a curve as input - just arrays with vertices.

I'm always frustrated when I have an object that I cannot directly use as input. The way it is now, I'd have to extract vertices from a curve, put these into an array and feed this array into the NURBS object's constructor.

I'd like to see support for more types of NURBS objects (with proper documentation) than those that are already implemented,  like Loft NURBS. These should be able to directly get the defining curves as input, instead of vertex arrays.

I want to be able to define curves based on mathematic formulae and make smooth surfaces of 3d objects out of them (in this case, I'm working on procedurally generated mushrooms).

I have considered using other libraries, but in this case, my customer wants to keep the project as small as possible, ie no external libraries are allowed.

![fungus 1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19553316/157957034-0d050d0c-4fd9-4729-968c-930a96b3a32e.jpg)
Screenshot shows different types of NURBS in Cinema 4d.

title: VTKLoader: decompression error when parsing VTP files of large size
body:
**Describe the bug**

When trying to loading VTP files of large size, parsing fails with a zlib decompression error

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Save bunny2.vtp contained in the archive
[bunny2.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8246072/bunny2.zip)

2. Try to load it with VTKLoader:

```
import { VTKLoader} from 'three/examples/jsm/loaders/VTKLoader.js';
// ...
var loader1 = new VTKLoader();
    loader1.load( 'bunny2.vtp',
    function ( geometry ) {
    }
);
```
Loading fails with an error
```
invalid zlib data
eval	@	VTKLoader.js?0dde:42
eval	@	three.module.js?8475:39284
Promise.then (async)		
load	@	three.module.js?8475:39269
load	@	VTKLoader.js?0dde:28
```

**Investigation**

This is due to an invalid bitwise shift applied when parsing VTP header, in `VTKLoader`'s `parseDataArray`, making the parser splitting the data in wrong blocks. 
I could make a fix, I'll open a PR to propose it.

title: PCDLoader rgb broken for some ASCII files

**Describe the bug**

ASCII PCD files with the `rgb` field as an integer can be loaded fine by the `PCDLoader`.
However, ASCII PCD files with a float `rgb` field just result in a black point cloud.
Most tools such as PCL and Cloud Convert don't save the `rgb` field as an integer, but e.g. [Open3D](http://www.open3d.org/) saves it as a float.

I am using three js vector & matrix math. I got stuck for 2 hours because Matrix4::lookAt() is not doing what a function with this name usually does in CG (correct me if I'm wrong). 

Expected: 
As far as I know, lookAt() creates a Matrix that does rotation and translation in a way, as if looking from the eye coordinate towards the target coordinate keeping the up vector pointing upwards. 

What happened: 
This method creates only a rotation matrix. No translation is happening here. 

Proposed solution: 
Rename this method to "lookAtRotation" or so to prevent confusion.

author: braebo

title: Add missing blend function constants
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I have some custom blending logic that would greatly benefit from the missing blend function constants:

- gl.CONSTANT_COLOR
- gl.ONE_MINUS_CONSTANT_COLOR
- gl.CONSTANT_ALPHA
- gl.ONE_MINUS_CONSTANT_ALPHA

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add the missing blend function constants. Proposed as:

- `THREE.ConstantColor`
- `THREE.OneMinusConstantColor`
- `THREE.ConstantAlpha`
- `THREE.OneMinusConstantAlpha`

And add a complimenting field to `THREE.Material`, proposed as:
```
material.blendColor
```
or
```
material.setBlendColor(color)
```
that maps to the underlying WebGL `gl.blendColor` method.

We might also want to handle this case (from the spec):
```
"If a constant color and a constant alpha value are used together as source and destination factors, a gl.INVALID_ENUM error is thrown."
```

I can look into this if it is deemed by maintainers to be a good addition.

title: WEBGPU Custom Shader Example
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

It's not clear if Custom Shaders are supported with the current top of tree for WebGPU.  All examples use the node system which would be problematic at the least from a support perspective (alignment with other systems shaders).  Using RawShaderMaterial results in a null'd out shader that errors in compiling.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Allow GLSL legal custom shaders

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I've seen branches in other users repo's that make use of RawShaderMaterial so it's not clear if the support was deprecated.  Or if they hacked in support.

**Additional context**

Any pointers to allow custom shading support would be appreciated.  Otherwise, we have to drop testing/using the WebGPU experimental branch (unfortunate)

body:
**Describe the bug**

Seems that when only positional data is present, morph targets/blend shapes are incorrect.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Download [blendshape-bugs.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8325401/blendshape-bugs.zip)
2. Go to https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/ (also broken in model-viewer, three.js editor)
3. Drop unpacked GLB file into viewer
4. Adjust blend weights to see that three.js produces a garbage/noise result
5. Drop the same file into https://sandbox.babylonjs.com/
6. Select the root node
7. Adjust blend weights to see that here it works as expected

**Expected behavior**

_Correctly displays blended shape._

_Cool looking but incorrect result:_
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/159512084-9173739d-da32-495a-91f0-20fde6154e54.png)

**Platforms where this is broken**
- [x] Windows
   - broken in Chrome 99, Firefox 98, Edge 99
   - broken with at least Nvidia RTX 2070 Max-Q and RTX 3060 Ti
- [ ] Android
- [ ] iOS

**Additional Info**

The file doesn't pass glTF validation, Nomad bug report here: https://forum.nomadsculpt.com/t/exported-gltf-is-sometimes-invalid-doesnt-pass-gltf-validation/6287. The corrected file (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8325531/blendshape-bugs-v1.glb.zip) exhibits the same problem.

title: DataTexture should have needsUpdate = true when constructed with existing data
body:
As shown in[ the documentation](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/textures/DataTexture), when using DataTexture one needs to use `needsUpdate = true` explicitly.

This is kind of unexpected, see e.g. comment in https://codepen.io/alexpyzhianov/pen/vNjJWd:

```
dataTex.needsUpdate = true; // somehow this line is required for this demo to work. I have not figured that out yet.
```

Is there any reason why a new `DataTexure` does not have `needsUpdate = true` automatically on construction? Other texture types do not seem to need this.

title: RFC: Making Euler, Color, Quaternions iterable - and consequently spreadable - like Vector*
body:
Vectors are iterable, so they can be spread to an array like `[...vector]`. The same isn't true for Euler, and users ** cough cough me right now ** might expect consistent behaviour.

I was surprised to see this missing, so I'm wondering wether there is a reason we wouldn't make them behave in the same way. 
I know order might be one reason, but then again users could order before spreading.

The code itself would just be the few lines to add the feature and a test, shouldn't take a long time, I'm more interested in why this could be a bad idea.

Have a great weekend and hugs all around!

EDIT: 
- Quaternion
- Color

Are also candidates for this
author: gsimone

title: BufferGeometryUtils.computeTangents( geometry ) fails with non-indexed geometry
body:
The error occurs here:
```js
return geometry.copy( _geometry );
```
when `geometry === _geometry`.

The proper solution depends on how you intend the method to be used:
```js
geometry = BufferGeometryUtils.computeTangents( geometry ); // return a new geometry
```
vs.
```js
BufferGeometryUtils.computeTangents( geometry ); // modify the existing geometry
```
/ping @donmccurdy 

 - Three.js version: [r139]
author: WestLangley

title: Reconsider removal of inline sRGB decode
body:
Decided to start a new issue rather than adding comments on the closed ones, hope that's OK.

I'd like to request a reconsideration of the inline sRGB decode removal from #23129.

The WebGL1 fallback involves forcibly disabling mipmaps if EXT_sRGB is supported, or a costly CPU-side conversion if not - both of these are significant regressions in WebGL1 functionality.

As discussed over on #22689, it seems like WebGL1 support is still accepted as a current necessity. iOS is the main reason why this is still an important consideration - roughly [20% of iOS users](https://mixpanel.com/trends/#report/ios_15/from_date:-29,report_unit:day,to_date:0) are still not yet on iOS 15 when WebGL2 was first enabled by default (prior to that the implementation was so buggy to be pointless enabling anyway).

iOS 15 uses ANGLE for its WebGL2 implementation, but with the Metal backend which is pretty new - it is usable and works correctly for a fair amount of content, but there are known bugs and regressions even in the latest iOS 15.4. Therefore sticking with WebGL1, even on the latest iOS, is one practical way to avoid the worst implementation bugs whilst Apple's WebGL2 implementation settles down and becomes more reliable.

I understand that doing sRGB decode in the shader leads to incorrect interpolation, and without using the correct sRGB internal formats, mipmap filtering will be incorrect. I think the change in #23129 will lead to worse practical effects for WebGL1 content, especially the CPU-side conversion.

Here are some example values:

| 8-bit sRGB value | [0,1] linear value | 8-bit linear (floating point) | 8-bit linear (rounded) | Linear -> 8-bit sRGB |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 7 | 0.002125 | 0.54 | 1 | 13 |
| 12 | 0.003677 | 0.94 | 1 | 13 |
| 17 | 0.005605 | 1.43 | 1 | 13 |

Essentially any pixel with an sRGB value in the range [7, 17] will end up as 1 in the CPU-converted texture, and be rounded to 13 when the shader encodes as sRGB for output (NB: I've assumed rounding here, whereas it looks like the CPU conversion uses Math.floor - the logic still stands but exact numbers may vary).

In my view this is likely to cause much more severe artefacts than the mathematically incorrect interpolation.

A sampler half-way between two pixels with sRGB values `(7/255)` and `(17/255)` should be computed in the sampler after the conversion of each sample to linear space - ie it should be `0.002125 * 0.5 + 0.005605 * 0.5 = 0.003865`. "Incorrect" linear blending would compute it as `(7 * 0.5 + 17 * 0.5) / 255`, ie `(12/255)` and then do sRGB conversion on that result, giving 0.003677. It's not quite right but it's not *that* bad.

The problem is the linear values are just an intermediate stage (need to be encoded back to sRGB for output anyway) and 8-bits is just not enough precision for storing them. In the shader as long as the precision isn't `lowp` you'd be guaranteed more precision for those intermediate results.

The overall question is what to do about sRGB stuff in WebGL1 contexts. One option is just to not support them - Unity only allows you to use "linear" lighting if WebGL1 is disabled, but that means both webgl1 and webgl2 contexts end up running in "gamma" mode with the mathematically-incorrect lighting calculations.

I think three.js's previous behaviour of emulating in the shader post-sampling was reasonable, which gives mathematically-correct behaviour in WebGL2, plus a visually consistent fallback in WebGL1. What's more as the intermediate values use sufficient precision, it wouldn't introduce banding artefacts for a simple "passthrough" unlit shader, unlike the current CPU fallback.

WebGL1 + EXT_sRGB looks like a reasonable "correct on WebGL1" approach, but the lack of mipmapping is annoying. sRGB-correct mips could be computed on the CPU side but there'd be a performance cost. Perhaps the best balance is to only use EXT_sRGB in the case mipmaps are explicitly disabled, rather than forcing that choice on users.

title: Move GLTFLoader, OrbitControls, and BufferGeometryUtils to core
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The GLTFLoader, OrbitControls, and BufferGeometryUtils are the three most imported things from the `examples/jsm` (and the postprocessing-related things are the fourth, I think). I also think that they are stable enough to be moved to the core.

I suggest they would be moved to the core.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

They can remain in the examples and then users will still import them from `examples/jsm`.

title: WebGL1Renderer now faster than WebGLRenderer after browser update
body:
Hi!

I was using Three.js this month for testing and everything was fine.

After I update Brave, today, all my code was running really slow, from 59 fps to 14-15 fps
The issue happened in both of the video cards in the notebook:
  - Intel HD Graphics 630 1536 MB
  - Radeon Pro 560 4 GB

Tried a lot of tests:
  - Updated r138 to r139: still slow
  - Put just 4 objects in scene: still slow
  - Changed WebGLRenderer to WebGL1Renderer: got speed back!


The renderer init code before:
`const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({
    antialias: true,
    powerPreference: "high-performance"
});
`

This is what the renderer init code looks now:
`const renderer = new THREE.WebGL1Renderer({
    antialias: true,
    powerPreference: "high-performance"
});`

Maybe it's a Chromium 99 vs 100 issue?
Can try to test in real Chrome browser and supply a full source code with different models if needed

**To Reproduce**

**Brave macOS version with the WebGLRenderer slow and WebGL1Renderer fast:**
Version 1.37.109 **Chromium: 100.0.4896.60** (Official Build) (x86_64)
Download link: https://github.com/brave/brave-browser/releases/tag/v1.37.109

**Brave macOS version with both WebGLRenderer and WebGL1Renderer fast:**
Version 1.36.117 **Chromium: 99.0.4844.74** (Official Build) (x86_64)
Download link: https://github.com/brave/brave-browser/releases/tag/v1.36.117


**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: macOS (12.3 (21E230))
 - Browser: Chromium based
 - Three.js version: r138 and r139

title: USDZ exporter ignores axis translation
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

In trying to export a glTF model, it looks like the USDZ exporter ignores the previously applied translation. 

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Load your glTF
2. Set updateMatrixWorld to true (i.e. gltf.scene.updateMatrixWorld(true))
3. Apply translation to an axis (e.g. gltf.scene.translateY(some_value))
4. Export; the USDZ won't be translated.

***Code***

```js
gltf.scene.updateMatrixWorld(true);
const box = new THREE.Box3().setFromObject(gltf.scene);
const lowestPoint = box.min.y;

if (lowestPoint < 0) {
    gltf.scene.translateY(Math.abs(lowestPoint));
}

const exporter = new THREE.USDZExporter();
const arraybuffer = await exporter.parse(gltf.scene);
fsExtra.writeFileSync(fileName + ".usdz", arraybuffer);
```

**Expected behavior**

The USDZ would have its axis translated as well.

**Screenshots**

![USDZ](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/91926334/161681972-b4ec886f-f89e-48e7-916b-bb5e0b2ffa75.png)

**Platform:**

author: Chrisyss

title: GPURenderer fails to set correct transparent background
body:
In the case of `WebGLRenderer`, we can do this:

```js
renderer.setClearColor( 0xff0000, 0.5 );
```

But behind the scenes, the RGB values are premultiplied by alpha.

To achieve the same effect with `WebGPURenderer`, this is required:

```js
renderer.setClearColor( 0x7f0000, 0.5 ); // <== the app must premultiply by alpha
```

//

`WebGPURenderer` sets `compositingAlphaMode: 'premultiplied'`.  (This means that the compositor expects premultiplied alphas in the drawing buffer.)

I think it is sufficient to implement in `WebGPURenderer` similar clear-color logic to that found in `WebGLRenderer`,  `WebGLBackground` and `WebGLState`.

Also, as I suggested [here](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23776#issuecomment-1077562617), `WebGPURenderer._clearAlpha` should be initialized to zero.

/ping @sunag

 - Three.js version: [r139]
author: WestLangley

title: getWorldScale and getWorldQuaternion return incorrect values when object scale has three negative components
body:
**Describe the bug**

`mesh.getWorldScale` and `mesh.getWorldQuaternion` return incorrect values for a mesh with negative x, y, and z scale components.

If `mesh.scale` is (-1, -1, -1), `getWorldScale` returns (-1, 1, 1), losing two minus signs. If `mesh.quaternion` is (0, 0, 0, 1), `getWorldQuaternion` returns (1, 0, 0, 0).

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to https://codepen.io/bennlich/pen/NWXyLKm
2. Click anywhere on the canvas
3. Notice how the image texture flips, and look at the console to see how the values of mesh.scale differ before and after.
4. Go to https://codepen.io/bennlich/pen/MWrQPWE to see the analogous bug with getWorldQuaternion

**Expected behavior**

In the live example above, I would not expect the mesh to flip. I would expect `getWorldScale` to return the same value as the mesh's current local scale. Same with `getWorldQuaternion`.

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Linux
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: 0.139.2

author: marcofugaro

title: Feature request: Add premultiplied alpha control in `WebGLBackground`
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently `WebGLBackground` always uses the `premultipliedAlpha` setting of the back buffer. We'd like to enable `premultipliedAlpha` for the back buffer (because it's what the browser wants) and disable it for framebuffers (because srgb textures don't work with premultiplied alpha).

This setting renders `WebGLBackground` unusable becuase it always premultiplies the background color no matter what the current render target is.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

In `WebGLBackground.render`, check current render target. If it's null, apply the back buffer setting, otherwise apply the render target texture's setting.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

1. Add an option to `WebGLBackground` and let user change it before rendering to a framebuffer.
2. Let user implement their own `premultipliedAlpha`-aware background.

EDIT: A reference implementation https://github.com/oppenfuture/three.js/pull/97.

title: Changing `transparent` property doesn't have any effect on materials, if `true` is not provided in the constructor
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

It seems the since r137, the default value for `transparent` prop for materials need to be `true` (provided in the constructor) if you ever want to make the material transparent in its lifetime. If the default value is `false` (or is not provided), then changing it to `true` won't have any effect.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a mesh
2. Some time later (with `setTimeout`, or by a click event, or anything), attempt to make the material transparent, like this: `mesh.material.transparent = true; mesh.material.opacity = 0.5;`
3. Note how the material remains unchanged

***Live example***

title: Position and Rotation support for toJSON() and ObjectLoader()
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I'm trying to save my three.js meshes to the browser local storage and reload them. This is so that an exact scene can be restored when a user refreshes the page or closes and reopens the tab. It's working great with saving [`mesh.toJSON()`](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/core/Object3D.js#L629-L869) to local storage and then reloading it with [`THREE.ObjectLoader()`](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/loaders/ObjectLoader) except that the meshes position and rotation is ignored and thus gets reset to zero. I've developed a workaround for my use case but it would be great if these functions could have built in support for position and rotation.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

 - Have the `mesh.toJSON()` preserve the meshes position and rotation.
 - Have the `THREE.ObjectLoader()` restore the meshes position and rotation.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Users can build a workaround by saving mesh position and rotation elsewhere and then re-applying it after the ObjectLoader has loaded the mesh but this is far from ideal.

**Additional context**

I did a little research on StackOverflow before opening this issue and it seems like i'm [not alone here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48425819/three-js-mesh-position-tojson).

author: whatisor

title: RFC: RaycasterHelper
body:
Hey! 👋  
I made a very simple raycaster helper for myself, was wondering if it isn't worth adding this along the other existing helpers.


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1862172/162688071-4993e811-2fe0-4b8a-99c6-99c38214d78e.mov

Try it here https://6tc01w.csb.app/

It displays:
- a segment going from origin to near + near to far
- the two near/far points
- optionally displays any hit via instances - this could just be points tbh
- optionally displays wether or not any hit exists

The style is halfway between @gkjohnson 's bvh ray visualizations and three's directional light helper

API is:

```js
const raycaster = new Raycaster(origin, direction, near, far)
const helper = new RaycasterHelper( raycaster, 5 ) // raycaster + max number of hits to display

// in your loop
const hits = raycaster.intersectObjects( scene.children )
helper.hits = hits;
```   

Some boring nuance:
- all the elements created by the helper have their raycast set as null, otherwise they'd always be hit by the ray they are visualizing 😓 
- there's some inefficiency in the way lines are updated, but that should be easily fixed if it's worth doing
- all the components of the visualization are exposed as properties of the object, so they can be easily overridden, eg.
```js
const helper = new RaycasterHelper( raycaster )
// make the hits red instead of white
helper.hitsVisualization.material.color = "#ff005b"
```

title: Wrong documentation for WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding?
body:
The documentation page of [WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=cubeca#api/en/renderers/WebGLRenderer.outputEncoding) says:

> Defines the output encoding of the renderer. Default is [THREE.LinearEncoding]().
> If a render target has been set using [.setRenderTarget]() then renderTarget.texture.encoding will be used instead.

Looking at [WebGLRenderer source](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f0e2b3453f1412b53389beb04add414e3a80023c/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1440) it seems that `renderTarget.texture.encoding` is used only if `renderTarget.isXRRenderTarget` otherwise is used hardcoded `LinearEncoding`

title: ImageBitmapLoader (GLTFLoader) fails in Firefox ESR
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

GLTF models don´t load in Firefox ESR due to lack of support for ImageBitLoader. 

**To Reproduce**

1. Install Firefox ESR (version number 91) from https://www.mozilla.org/en-GB/firefox/all/#product-desktop-esr
2. Open https://www.geofront.eu/tmp/ironmen_bugreport_fail
3. GLTF model is not being loaded. See also error: THREE.GLTFLoader: Couldn´t load texture tex.webp 

***Code***

The problem lies in the removal of a check for Firefox to avoid usage of ImageBitmapLoader (compare r130). 
The most recent Firefox version works fine with this - but Firefox ESR (used internally in the museum that has published this webpage) is not yet capable of handling ImageBitmapLoader. 
We therefore suggest an additional check for Firefox and its ESR-channel version number 91 in this code snippet of GLTFLoader.js: 

```js
 if ( typeof createImageBitmap !== 'undefined' && /^((?!chrome|android).)*safari/i.test( navigator.userAgent ) === false  && /Firefox\/91/.test( navigator.userAgent ) === false) {
this.textureLoader = new ImageBitmapLoader( this.options.manager );
} else {
this.textureLoader = new TextureLoader( this.options.manager );
}

```

Note: This will have to be extended to later Firefox ESR versions (we did not track down the exact version where Firefox´ ImageBitmapLoader starts working). 

***Live example***

* Fail:  https://www.geofront.eu/tmp/ironmen_bugreport_fail
* Fixed: https://www.geofront.eu/tmp/ironmen_bugreport_fixed

**Expected behavior**

The GLTF should have loaded and displayed, just like in Firefox 99. 

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows
 - Browser: Firefox ESR release 91.8.0esr (64-bit) https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/firefox-esr-release-cycle
 - Three.js version: r139

author: makc

title: Model shadow error when `OES_texture_float` extension is unavailable
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Shadow is rendered incorrectly (while the model itself is rendered as normal) when `OES_texture_float` extension is unavailable on some outdated or mobile platform.
Root cause:
The value of maxBone of different skinnedMesh objects is not consistent, in the rendering process of each frame, the check of `needsProgramChange` does not include maxBone. As a result, during generation of  skinnedMesh's shadowMap, the program is created for the first time and reused for all other skinnedMeshes. If some skinnedMesh has larger number of bones than the first program, data in boneMatrices will not be correctly passed to skin's shader(src/renderer/shaders/ShaderChunk/skinning_pars_vertex.glsl.js).

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Replace the model in `example/webgl_animation_skinning_additive_blending.html` with animated gltf model of multiple parts with different bone number.
2. Open the example on platform that `OES_texture_float` is not supported, or manually disable floatFragmentTextures in `src/renderer/webgl/WebGLCapabilitites.js`
3. See the incorrect shadow.

***Code***

Code to disable floatFragmentTextures:

```js
const floatFragmentTextures = !(isWebGL2 || extensions.has( 'OES_texture_float' ));
```

***Live example***
Sorry I cannot provided the live example since the model is restricted to share publicly.

**Expected behavior**

Shadow is rendered correctly.

**Screenshots**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13135428/164214213-cc0c81ea-95a4-4f5d-a129-65409e7cd7cb.png)

**Platform:**

 - Browser: [Other]

title: Check exist image in texture before export
body:
Sometimes the image may not be loaded into the texture and then there will be an error during export due to the missing image.
That's why when exporting object or object.toJson(), we can check for the presence of an image in the texture to avoid errors.
example :
`if ( this.map && this.map.isTexture ) data.map = this.map.toJSON( meta ).uuid;`
to
`if ( this.map && this.map.image &&  this.map.isTexture ) data.map = this.map.toJSON( meta ).uuid;`

author: willstott101

title: Memory leak: Texture dispose() method doesn't close ImageBitmap
body:
**Describe the bug**

Currently, textures from `glb` files are loaded as `ImageBitmap` objects, which are not garbage collected by default - one needs to call [the `close()` method](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/ImageBitmap/close)

However, the `Texture.prototype.dispose()` method doesn't do it.

**To Reproduce**

1. Open https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/ in Chrome
2. Open the browser task manager (Shift+Esc or Menu -> More Tools -> Task Manager)
3. Find the tab in the task manager and see its memory consumption. It will be about 20-30MB.
4. Download this [`glb` model with big 4K textures](https://www.dropbox.com/s/qo0nmqxfokicft3/Cappuccino_cup.glb?dl=0)
5. Drag and drop the model on the viewer several times. You will see that the memory consumption rapidly increases, despite the fact all [textures are disposed of each time](https://github.com/donmccurdy/three-gltf-viewer/blob/890e4bc84d203058a6f2673e2b6f3ff19b6a3e5b/src/viewer.js#L724)

**Expected behavior**

The code `texture.source.data.close?.()` before `texture.dispose()` solves the problem.
But I would expect the `Texture.prototype.dispose()` method to do it automatically, especially given the fact, the textures are created by the gltf loader, and their type isn't obvious. Alternatively, a bool flag `deep=true` can be passed to the method to dispose of the inner resource.

If it's by design, it should be clearly written in the documentation.

**Platform:**

 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r139

title: Clone objects with userData that isn't JSON encodable?
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I cannot clone my scene (which I wanted to do for export purposes, see #23951) because my userData has got cyclic references in it. Now on the one hand I'd concede perhaps it shouldn't, but it does seem like an unnecessary limitation to me.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

A) An extra option to the clone/copy functions to toggle the copying of userData.
B) Use of [`structuredClone`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/structuredClone) in environments that support it over `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x))`

I think I should have the time to implement one of these if they're likely to get merged.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Traversing and removing my userData before cloning, and then re-adding it.

title: GLTFExporter: jpeg textures on export binary and embedImages=false cause validation error
body:
Heya,

when exporting a material with a jpeg texture as a .glb with these options:
```
{
    binary: true,
    embedImages: false
}
```

The resulting .glb has a validation error:

```
      {
        "code": "IMAGE_MIME_TYPE_INVALID",
        "message": "Recognized image format 'image/jpeg' does not match declared image format 'image/png'.",
        "severity": 0,
        "pointer": "/images/0"
      },
```

It seems three.js exports the texture with the `image/png` mime type, regardless of what type the image actually is: `...,"images":[{"mimeType":"image/png","uri":"data:image/jpeg;base64,...`.

I had a brief look around but couldn't find a way to set the mime type manually (but maybe I missed something...).

Working example: https://framer.tz1and.com/
Source: https://github.com/tz1and/image-framer/blob/main/src/index.js

Thanks!

title: WebXRManager throws error in onSessionEnd if getController is never called
body:
TLDR: Lack of null check for controllers in `onSessionEnd` causes errors if `getController` is never called

It seems controllers are only created when `getController` or `getControllerGrip` are called. if those functions are never called, we end up with null controllers in the inputSourcesMap

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/1238a324e081752d9f66219252361f05f73594df/src/renderers/webxr/WebXRManager.js#L337-L344

Once the session ends, there's no null check for the controllers when trying to disconnect them as seen below

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/1238a324e081752d9f66219252361f05f73594df/src/renderers/webxr/WebXRManager.js#L132-L138

title: GLTFExporter output file size regression
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Importing a glTF with jpeg textures and reexporting it is now causing a major bloat in file size compared to the original (~2.5x for DamagedHelmet). I'm pretty sure this is due to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23857, as we're now using the `convertToBlob` method of OffscreenCanvas instead of the `toBlob` method of Canvas. This feels like it might also be a Chrome bug? It is correctly outputting JPEGs of the proper dimensions, they are just much larger, like the quality setting is defaulting to something a lot higher than the usual 0.85. @donmccurdy @robertlong Thoughts? Our regression tests caught this in `<model-viewer>` when I tried to upgrade. 

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to the model-viewer [editor](https://modelviewer.dev/editor/) and select DamagedHelmet
2. Click on "Download Scene"
3. Open the zip and inspect DamagedHelmet.glb - note it is now 9.3MB

**Expected behavior**

The GLB should be ~4MB, similar to the 3.8MB original

**Screenshots**

author: ben4d85

title: Should skinning examples show skinning?
body:
I noticed none of skinning examples actually show skinning. At best maybe the stomach of the stormtrooper in [this example](https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_loader_collada_skinning.html) shows skinning. The rest might be using the skinning code but the models don't "skin". They're a collection of solid parts that would look exactly same without skinning code.

A good example that makes this clear is this example:

https://threejs.org/examples/webgpu_skinning_points.html

You can see, no points are being weight blending by multiple vertices. That's not "skinning"

The same is true for all of these examples: https://threejs.org/examples/webgpu_skinning_instancing.html, https://threejs.org/examples/webgpu_skinning.html, https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_animation_skinning_morph.html,
https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_animation_skinning_additive_blending.html

Even this one, https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_animation_skinning_blending.html, it's hard to see any actual "skinning". It's possible there's some skinning in the black parts of the joints but given the armor is stiff this example shows no actual skinning.

If it's not clear what I mean. This is skinning (computing vertex positions by summing the weighted influence of multiple matrices)

![skinning-lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/234804/166749100-9c3d1d5e-93aa-49f1-a258-5893e804816a.gif)
![skinning-solid](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/234804/166749108-a792338a-4c8c-47d1-b2e8-f56424dfbbb6.gif)

This is not skinning (moving independent meshes)

![not-skinning-lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/234804/166749128-be003b76-86a5-4c9b-bf29-7bfbf0efe041.gif)
![not-skinning-solid](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/234804/166749132-79cac73f-007b-43c9-a7e8-14fb814c78a3.gif)

All of the three.js "skinning" examples are not skinning or at least not good examples of skinning. Note: It's possible the examples are going through the skinning code. It's even possible they're using weights but if so the weights are all set such that there's only one matrix influencing each vertex which is not really a good demonstration of skinning.

Would it be ok to replace the example models with something else? A quick search, all of these CC-BY models are much better examples of skinning.

author: CraigglesO

title: Materials take care of needsUpdate by itself
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I have a question about the design of `needsUpdate` property in materials.
As far as I know, `needsUpdate` property aimed to recompile the shader before the next render.
However, my thought is that it shouldn't be the case for users to know when they need to use `needsUpdate`.
Because material internally should have knowledge itself about their shader and when needs to be recompiled.
BTW, I'm just curious why it needs to be designed this way.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

`needsUpdate` be managed internally by each type of material.

----

P.S. Please close the topic if this issue already exists or if the problem is actually not valid.

title: Creating a CubeTexture from 6 CompressedTextures doesn't work after r136
body: **Describe the bug** Prior to version `r136` of the library, creating a `CubeTexture` from 6 `CompressedTextures` used to work just fine. However, it seems like [this change](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/22951) introduced in r136 changed the behaviour of how textures are loaded into the GPU when WebGL 2 APIs are available, which broke this use-case. As a work-around, I have been able to get the cube texture to render by setting `texture.isVideoTexture = true` on the `CubeTexture`. According to [this line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L699), that forces Three to opt-out of using `texStorage2D` which seems to be causing the issue ***Code*** ```js const loader = new KTX2Loader() .setTranscoderPath( 'js/libs/basis/' ) .detectSupport( renderer ); const textures = await Promise.all([ loader.loadAsync( './textures/nx.ktx2' ), loader.loadAsync( './textures/px.ktx2' ), loader.loadAsync( './textures/ny.ktx2' ), loader.loadAsync( './textures/py.ktx2' ), loader.loadAsync( './textures/nz.ktx2' ), loader.loadAsync( './textures/pz.ktx2' ) ]); textures.forEach((texture) => { texture.generateMipmaps = false; texture.encoding = THREE.LinearEncoding; texture.needsUpdate = true; }); const cubeTexture = new THREE.CubeTexture(textures); cubeTexture.encoding = textures[0].encoding; cubeTexture.format = textures[0].format; cubeTexture.minFilter = textures[0].minFilter; cubeTexture.magFilter = textures[0].magFilter; cubeTexture.generateMipmaps = false; // HACK: Setting `isVideoTexture` opts-out of using `texStorage2D` cubeTexture.isVideoTexture = true; cubeTexture.needsUpdate = true; // Set scene background scene.background = cubeTexture ``` ***Live example*** * [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/vigilant-proskuriakova-5w6wf0?file=/src/Scene.jsx) (I'm using react-three-fiber, let me know if you want a vanilla Three.js version!) **Expected behavior** It should load the `CubeTexture` just fine as the scene background. Instead, the scene background is black and the following errors show in the console: ``` [.WebGL-0x700abe0a00] GL_INVALID_VALUE: Texture dimensions must all be greater than zero. ``` and ``` [.WebGL-0x700abe0a00] GL_INVALID_OPERATION: Level of detail outside of range. ``` Upgrading to r140.2, also shows this added error: ``` THREE.WebGLTextures: sRGB encoded textures have to use RGBAFormat and UnsignedByteType. ``` **Screenshots** https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8302959/168479550-cf0d681d-cd96-40a6-a816-18699047322b.mov **Platform:** - Device: Desktop - OS: MacOS - Browser: Chrome - Three.js version: r136

author: jjsolly

title: GLTFExporter uses wrong cache value `baseAttribute` instead of `relativeAttribute`
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

We have a model that is multiple cubes with the same POSITION attributes. One of the cubes has a morph target. When we export this model the mesh with the morph target came out correct, but the other meshes had their position data messed up.

I did some digging and realized that the POSITION attribute for the cubes w/o morph targets were using the attribute for the morph target. We're not creating/editing attributes at all, and the scene looked fine before export, so I started digging into the GLTFExporter code. I tracked the issue down to this code:

```
// Clones attribute not to override
const relativeAttribute = attribute.clone();

if ( ! geometry.morphTargetsRelative ) {

for ( let j = 0, jl = attribute.count; j < jl; j ++ ) {

	relativeAttribute.setXYZ(
		j,
		attribute.getX( j ) - baseAttribute.getX( j ),
		attribute.getY( j ) - baseAttribute.getY( j ),
		attribute.getZ( j ) - baseAttribute.getZ( j )
	);

}

}

target[ gltfAttributeName ] = this.processAccessor( relativeAttribute, geometry );
cache.attributes.set( this.getUID( baseAttribute ), target[ gltfAttributeName ] );
```

The last line is the culprit, but I included more for context. Basically the `baseAttribute` is used to update `cache.attributes` instead of `relativeAttribute` which caused subsequent meshes to processed incorrectly as the would use the morph attribute instead of the position one. 

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Load the following model using the GLTFLoader 
[model(13).zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/8703833/model.13.zip)

2. Export the model using GLTFExporter.parse using the following options:
```js
{
  binary: true,
}
```
3. If you inspect the exported model you should see that one of the cubes (the one with the morph target) is correct, while the others are using the accessor id for the morph target data.

**Expected behavior**

The exporter should cache the morph target attribute using `relativeAttribute` instead of `baseAttribute`.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop, Mobile]
 - OS: [Windows, MacOS, Linux, Android, iOS]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge]
 - Three.js version: [dev, r140]

title: [Suggestion] Replace EventDispatcher with native EventTarget and CustomEvents
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The current EventDispatcher is lacking a lot of functionality that the native EventTarget supports, such as `.stopPropagation`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I suggest that the custom EventDispatcher is replaced and having classes like Object3D extend the native EventTarget instead.

This would affect the event objects currently being dispatched, because they would have to be replaced with CustomEvent instances instead.

This would be a breaking change.

As for browser support, then it is the following for extending [EventTarget](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/EventTarget#browser_compatibility):

- Chrome: 64
- Edge: 79
- Firefox: 58
- Internet Explorer: None
- Safari: 14

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Alternatively I think the EventDispatcher should be further developed to support relevant APIs like `.stopPropagation`.

**Additional context**

It was previously mentioned in  #12854, that it would be able to be removed "soon".

title: Vector3.angleTo() should be from 0 to 2 * Math.PI
body:
This person explains this issue nicely:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62358681/three-js-vector-angleto-how-to-also-get-the-bigger-value

```
var v1 = new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 1);
var v2 = new THREE.Vector3(1, 0, 0); // Expected Math.PI / 2
var v3 = new THREE.Vector3(-1, 0, 0); // Expected Math.PI * 3 / 2

v1.angleTo(v2)
1.5707963267948966

v1.angleTo(v3)
1.5707963267948966
```

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/6e897f9a42d615403dfa812b45663149f2d2db3e/src/math/Vector3.js#L557

Another possibility is to have negative values from `new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, -1);` (exclusive, so it would be positive `Math.PI`) to `new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 1);` (this is equal to `0` so neither negative nor positive).

title: `ShaderMaterial` output Color Space behavior on main scene vs render target
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

I've hit a confusing case where ShaderMaterial is behaving differently depending on if it was rendered in the main canvas or in a render target. The issue seems to be related to how color space is managed inside Threejs. I've consulted the [Color Management's section](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#manual/en/introduction/Color-management)  in the docs but couldn't find information about how to handle output colors in a ShaderMaterial.

Here's a link to reproduce the issue

https://jsfiddle.net/yelouafi/36h8eusp/57/

In the example we render 4 cubes, for that we prepare an input color **that's converted to linear space**

- The 2 on top are rendered into the main scene:
    - Top left is using a `MeshBasicMaterial`
    - Top right is using a `ShaderMaterial`

- The 2 on the bottom are rendered inside a render target, the render target's texture is then mapped on to  a mesh

   - Bottom left is using a `MeshBasicMaterial`
   - Bottom right is using a `ShaderMaterial`


The basic material renders the same color in both situations, but the ShaderMaterial's behavior is a little confusing. It seems to render different colors depending on where is was rendered (main scene or render target). Below is a screenshot of the result: Seems top one is rendering the wrong color

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5453835/170996747-0bea035a-45f7-4e19-9850-97cb3fc59799.png)


if you uncomment the line 74 `// outputColor = LinearTosRGB(outputColor);` in the material's fragment shader the color is rendered correct on both the main scene and the render target.


Also seems render target's `encoding` param doesn't seem to have any impact on the outcome. 

So my question are

- how to handle color output in the ShaderMaterial's fragment shader? should we always encode to sRGB or take into account the output encoding of the destination (a canvas or a render target)

- In the case we're rendering into a render target, should we take into account the render target's encoding in the fragment shader: for example, if the render target's enconding is linear, should the fragement shader output a linear color in this case?

- One last question regarding the color supplied to `renderer.setClearColor`,  the docs seems to suggest that all input colors should be converted to linear but in the case of `setClearColor` it seems the color needs to be sRGB, is this correct?

title: GLTFExporter: buildMetalRoughTexture not usable
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

GLTF exporter's `buildMetalRoughTexture` uses the cached canvas to merge the metalness and roughness map.
After the maps are merged, the canvas is sent to `processImage` which uses the same cached canvas to resize/flip the image.
When `processImage` sets the canvas (=== image) width and height to match the max texture size given in options, the canvas context is cleared and the merged metallicRoughnessMap is lost.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a mesh with a MeshStandardMaterial using different metalness and roughness maps.
2. Export the mesh to GLTF.

**Expected behavior**

Metalness and roughness maps should be merged and exported.

**Platform:**

 - Device: all
 - Three.js version: all
author: matyasasynth

title: Rendering of Mesh with RawShaderMaterial should not depend on the presence of index or position attribute
body:
**Describe the bug** `BufferGeometry` with `RawShaderMaterial` requires index or position attribute to display anything. This seems like an unnecessary limitation. I am not terribly familiar with the internals of three.js, but if I had to guess, I would suppose it has something to do with how the draw range is computed on these lines: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/0c26bb4bb8220126447c8373154ac045588441de/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L763-L774 Specifically, without index or position attribute `dataCount` on this line is `0`: ```js const drawEnd = Math.min( dataCount, rangeStart + rangeCount, groupStart + groupCount ) - 1; ``` If it is safe to do so, the fix might be simply to remove `dataCount`, or alternatively if it needs to be there, change the way `dataCount` is computed a few lines earlier. **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behavior: 1. Create `BufferGeometry` without position attribute or index. 2. Create `RawShaderMaterial` which does not require position attribute. 3. Create `Mesh` and add to scene. ***Code*** ```js three.js vr - dragging
three.js vr/ar/screen - dragging
``` ***Live example*** * [glitch](https://glitch.com/~buffer-geometry-without-position-attribute) **Expected behavior** Rendering of a mesh should not depend on the presence of attributes which are not required by the shader. In fact, setting a draw range should be sufficient to render with `RawShaderMaterial`.

title: Multi-sample Anti-Aliasing cause scene crash on iOS15.4 & 15.5
body:
**Describe the bug**

When using SSAA and WebGLMultiSampleRenderTarget, in iOS15.4 & 15.5, the scene would show nothing. It was said to be the bug of WebKit that occurs in iOS15.4 and fixed in 15.5. But for my project, the bug remains in iOS15.5

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
Add SSAA pass from /examples

title: Making 'three' tree-shakeable
body:
The goal with tree-shaking here is I should be able to import a class from `'three'`, and only get the classes needed. In my test bundle importing only `Vector2` is producing a 295 KB (uncompressed) file still with lots of remaining side-effects even after `r141` and all the work done on #24006 (down from a 1 MB bundle in `r140`).

I'm opening this issue to resolve the remaining side-effects, which is do-able with some more work, and we have a couple ways of testing that.

Also to make the claim that `'three'` is finally fully tree-shakeable, we'll need to verify that in the most popular bundlers. I'll start with [Rollup](https://rollupjs.org/), [Webpack](https://webpack.js.org/), [Parcel](https://parceljs.org/) and [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io/). Open to suggestions of other bundlers and configurations, and will start with a simple test of importing only `Vector2`.

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

```js
import { Vector2 } from 'three';

console.log(new Vector2(0, 1));
```

And with [agadoo](https://github.com/rich-harris/agadoo):

```sh
npx agadoo
...
Failed to tree-shake build/three.module.js
```

It's worth noting that importing from the source files with `agadoo` also fails, and is something I can look into as well.

```sh
npx agadoo src/Three.js
...
Failed to tree-shake src/Three.js
```

The expected behavior, regardless of `agadoo`, is simply looking at the output bundle. If I import `Vector2`, I'm expecting only the `@license` header comment, and `Vector2`, nothing else.

References:
 - https://twitter.com/MarcoGomezGT/status/1520866649485754372
 - mrdoob/three.js#24006

title: Editor: Video renderer no longer available in Chrome
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

The editor's video renderer [relies on a global `SharedArrayBuffer` constructor.](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/commit/71ffae30b3c75407d87696f726392215885dd8f9) Previously, this was [compatible with Chrome and not Firefox](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/21603#issuecomment-814956064).

However [API changes](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/SharedArrayBuffer/Planned_changes#api_changes) have made this condition fail in Chrome too without certain headers present. AFAIK, the video renderer is not visible in any modern browser.


**To Reproduce**

In Chrome (currently v102.0.5005.61), run `'SharedArrayBuffer' in window`. It will evaluate to `false`, and the video renderer UI will not appear on https://threejs.org/editor

**Expected behavior**

The video renderer will be visible and functional in the editor on Chrome at least. I have to read more deeply about the new security restrictions, but hopefully this is an easy fix.


**Platform:**

 - Device: 2018 MacBook Pro
 - OS: MacOS 11.6
 - Browser: Chrome 102.0.5005.61
 - Three.js version: r141
