title: ColladaLoader object position/center swapped values
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->
_**Context**: Im programming a 3D Room Search PWA for my university_

**Describe the bug**

I was moving from OBJLoader to ColladaLoader and recognized, that my added Labels from CSS2DRenderer had wrong positions when they are correct in the OBJ file.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Load collada.dae file
2. get Position of element
3. get geometry.boundingBox.getCenter(matrix);
4. Value Y is swapped with actual value Z

***Code***
_Context: the function creates a tooltip to show the room number linked as a label to the position above the searched room in my scene._

```js

/**
 * Adds a tooltip above a room with the given room number if exists in model
 * @param roomNumber object.name value that is compared to the given room number
 */
export function addTooltip(roomNumber) {

    const roomCard_tooltip = createRoomCard(returnRoom(roomNumber), true) //some HTML content for the tooltip
    roomCard_tooltip.style.marginTop = '-10em'; //set center position up
    tooltip = new CSS2DObject( roomCard_tooltip ) //add as global instance of label
    const room = scene.getObjectByName(roomNumber); //return RoomID of room in my model
    if (room) {
        let offset = null;
        console.log(`Room:  ${room}`)
        console.log(`Room init pos:  ${room.position.x}, ${room.position.y}, ${room.position.z}`) //is always 0, 0, 0 because not set
        if (room.position.x === 0 && room.position.z === 0) { //if coordinates not already set
            room.geometry.computeBoundingBox();
            let matrix = new THREE.Vector3();
            offset = room.geometry.boundingBox.getCenter(matrix); //compute center point of room
            console.log(`Room center:  ${offset.x}, ${offset.y}, ${offset.z}`)
            room.geometry.applyMatrix4(new THREE.Matrix4().makeTranslation(-offset.x, -offset.y, -offset.z));
            room.position.copy(offset); //set position from offset data
        }

        console.log(`Room pos:  ${room.position.x}, ${room.position.y}, ${room.position.z}`)
        room.material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial( { color: 0xE31433}); //sets color of room highlighted
        tooltip.position.copy( room.position ); // ERROR!!!!!!!!!!!!!!, position is not correct when loaded with ColladaLoader()
        addLightPoint(room.position.x, room.position.y, room.position.z, scene) //highlight position of initial room point
        if (offset != null) tooltip.position.set( offset.x, offset.z, - offset.y); // SOLUTION: swapped Z and Y value
        scene.add(tooltip)
        console.log(`tooltip pos:  ${tooltip.position.x}, ${tooltip.position.y}, ${tooltip.position.z}`)

        addLightPoint(tooltip.position.x, tooltip.position.y, tooltip.position.z, scene) //highlight position of tooltip point

    } else {
        console.error("Sorry, can't find room in object!")
    }
    
/**
 * Adds a light point to the position given
 * @param $x X-coordinate
 * @param $y Y-coordinate
 * @param $z Z-coordinate
 * @param scene the scene/context
 */
export function addLightPoint($x, $y, $z, scene) {
    const pointLight = new THREE.PointLight( 0xff0000, 1, 200 );
    pointLight.position.set( $x, $y, $z );
    scene.add( pointLight );

    const sphereSize = 1;
    const pointLightHelper = new THREE.PointLightHelper( pointLight, sphereSize );
    scene.add( pointLightHelper );
}
```

**Simple Loader:**
```js
let daeModelHdM = "" + new URL("../assets/models/dae/HdM_campus.dae",
    import.meta.url);

const loader = new ColladaLoader(manager);
loader.load( daeModelHdM, function ( collada ) {

    const object = collada.scene;
    const animations = object.animations;

    object.traverse( function ( child ) {
        if (child.isMesh) { //later and if is room or building
            //TODO: sorting into storys and buildings 2D-array
            
           //child.name <=> roomNumber/building

            objects.push(child); //saved in global instance
        }
    } );

    scene.add( object );

    let scaleFactor = 1;
    object.scale.set(scaleFactor, scaleFactor, scaleFactor);
    //object.rotateX(11);

    }, (xhr) => {
        console.debug(`${((xhr.loaded / xhr.total) * 100).toFixed(2)}% of model loaded`)
    }, function (error) {
        console.error(`An error happened: ${error}`);
    }
);
```

***Live example***

* [limited available project showcase](https://horst.benedict.lang-familie.de/) might be not up to date, maybe on 30th of June 22
* [later if online](https://horst.mi.hdm-stuttgart.de/)

**Expected behavior**

Same position calculation with the other loaders (tested with OBJLoader and Rhino3dmLoader).
I havent seen an obvious issue in the position of ColladaLoader code on a quick look so I guess the swapped values are in the getCenter, boundingBox or geometry.

**Screenshots**

Big PointLightHelper is 0,0,0 (Ground Zero, initial position)    -    small  PointLightHelper is 21, 76, 10 (the collada room center -> wrong!)    -    tooltip + PointLightHelper is 21, 10 , 76 (if changed values correct position)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/76404140/176493573-08a9e3a1-2329-4094-af8d-18db319dc14a.png)

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop, Mobile
 - OS: Windows 11, MacOS, Android
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: "three": "^0.140.0"
 - ColladaLoader: "three/examples/jsm/loaders/ColladaLoader"

title: Bug - KTX2Loader does not load Basis Textures

**Version:** ThreeJS v142

**Relevant ThreeJS Documentation:** https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/wiki/Migration-Guide#136--137

I have noticed two things, both start with [this commit](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/commit/f12268fe65781d1fbe016039780146f595847daa#):

1st, it set domElement.style.pointerEvents to 'none', which makes sense, ~~but now domElement is actually two elements and pointer-events: none style is applied to inner element - which does not make sense~~. I have to manually set it to none on the outer element before OrbitControls attached to webgl canvas underneath can work.

2nd, setting pointer-events to 'auto' on all the elements overrides my own styles, and I had to check the source to know that I have to reset this style after new CSS3DObject( element )

title: Material display differences between the versions of 0.135.0 and the versions of 0.136.0
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**
I use the same  mesh ,which has a material that is not affected by light.The difference is that the color of the material below three@0.136.0 is closer
to the original texture,while the color of the material above three@0.135.0 is darker
**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. use any geometry and any UV texture
2. use my custom material 
3. use three@0.135.0 and three@0.136.0

***Code***

```js
export function createMaterial(texture, geometry) {
  let colormode = 0;
  if (geometry.getAttribute("uv") && texture) {
    colormode = 2;
  } else if (geometry.getAttribute("color")) {
    colormode = 1;
  }

  let ka = 0.95,
    kd = 0.05,
    ks = 0.15,
    shiniess = 256.0;
  if (colormode === 0 || colormode === 1) {
    ka = 0.95; 
    kd = 0.6; 
    ks = 0.25; 
    shiniess = 128.0;
  }

  const Material = new RawShaderMaterial({
    uniforms: {
      lightParams: { value: new Vector4(ka, kd, ks, shiniess) },
      colorParams: { value: new Vector4(1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0) },
      colorMode: { value: colormode },
      useColor: { value: true },
      modelColor: { value: new Vector3(0.85, 0.85, 0.85) },
      texImg: { value: texture },
    },
    vertexShader: `precision highp float;   
    precision highp int;
    
    uniform mat4 modelViewMatrix;   
    uniform mat4 projectionMatrix;  
    
    attribute vec3 position; 
    attribute vec3 normal;  
    attribute vec3 color;   
    attribute vec2 uv;   
    
    uniform int colorMode; //0. Uniform Color ; 1.Vertex Color; 2. Texture Color
    uniform vec3 modelColor;  
    uniform bool useColor;
    
    const vec3 defaultColor = vec3(0.85,0.85,0.85);

    varying vec3 vNormal;
    varying vec3 vColor;
    varying vec2 vTexCoord;
    
    void main()	{
      vNormal = (modelViewMatrix*vec4(normal,0.0)).xyz;
      if(useColor==true)
      {
          if(colorMode==0)
          {
            vColor = modelColor;
          }
          else if(colorMode==1)
          {
            vColor = color;
          }
          else if(colorMode==2)
          {
            vTexCoord = uv;
          }
      }
      else
      {
          vColor = defaultColor;
      }
      gl_Position = projectionMatrix * modelViewMatrix * vec4( position, 1.0 );
    }`,
    fragmentShader: `precision highp float;
    precision highp int;
    
    uniform vec4 lightParams;
    uniform vec4 colorParams;
    uniform int colorMode; //0. Uniform Color ; 1.Vertex Color; 2. Texture Color
    uniform bool useColor;
    uniform sampler2D texImg;
    
    varying vec3 vNormal;
    varying vec3 vColor;
    varying vec2 vTexCoord;
    
    const vec3 viewDir = vec3(0.0, 0.0, 1.0);
    
    vec3 CalculateLight(vec3 color, vec3 normal, float ka, float kd, float ks, float shiniess) {
      float diff = abs(dot(normal, viewDir));
      float spec = pow(diff, shiniess);
      return color * (ka + diff*kd) + spec*ks;
    }
    
    void main()	{
      vec3 color = vColor;
      if(colorMode==2 && useColor==true)
      {
        color = texture2D(texImg,vTexCoord).rgb;
        if (color.x < 0.01 && color.y < 0.01 && color.z < 0.01)
          discard;
      }
      if(useColor==false) color = vec3(0.85,0.85,0.85);
      float ka =  useColor==true? lightParams.x:0.5;
      float kd = useColor==true? lightParams.y:0.5;
      float ks = useColor==true? lightParams.z:0.2;
      float shiniess = useColor==true? lightParams.w:128.0;
      
      if(gl_FrontFacing)
        color = CalculateLight(color, vNormal, ka, kd, ks, shiniess);
      else 
        color = CalculateLight(vec3(0.698,0.718,0.855), vNormal, 0.5, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0);
        gl_FragColor = vec4(color*colorParams.xyz, colorParams.a);
    }`,
    side: DoubleSide,
  });
  return Material;
}
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/hbfpj2wv/2/)
* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/086yzvgc/)

**Expected behavior**

title: TAARenderPass: Pass over-brightens scene, shows occasional black frames
body:
**Describe the bug**

When using TAARenderPass, certain valid settings result in incorrect behavior:

- `scene.background` — any non-black background will cause the final scene to over-brighten during TAA accumulation
- `pass.sampleLevel` — any non-zero level will cause a single-frame flash of black when the accumulation phase begins

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Open `webgl_postprocessing_taa` locally
2. Add a background color like `scene.background = new THREE.Color( 0xEE5555 );`
3. Increase TAASampleLevel (any level > 0)

Observe (1) the scene brightens when it stops moving and begins accumulating, and (2) the scene flashes to black for a single frame at that time. It may be easier to see the flash when the background is kept black.

**Expected behavior**

TAARenderPass should be compatible with non-black scene backgrounds, and should not show a black frame while accumulating.

**Screenshots**

If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem (drag and drop the image).

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: macOS 12.4
 - Browser: Chrome 103.0.5060.53
 - Three.js version: r142

author: stefandouw

title: Ambient occlusion uses TEXCOORD_1 instead of TEXCOORD_0
body:
**Describe the bug**
When both TEXCOORD_0 and TEXCOORD_1 are present, three.js will use TEXCOORD_1 for ambient occlusion. 

However TEXCOORD_0 is expected behavior for content authors, because [0 is the default in the spec](https://www.khronos.org/registry/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#_material_occlusiontextureinfo_texcoord) when the author has not specified TEXCOORD_1 in the material via the texCoord property, nor via the [KHR_texture_transform](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_texture_transform/README.md) extension. 

Other common glTF renderers seem to use the default TEXCOORD_0 when rendering this example model.

**To Reproduce**
1. Download attached example ZIP file, and extract the GLB.
2. Load the GLB in https://threejs.org/editor/
3. Result: "Incorrect" text is shown for occlusion.

**Expected behavior**
"Correct" text should be shown for occlusion, if TEXCOORD_0 is being used as intended.

**Screenshots**
![screenshots](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40177036/177862770-aa3d7c14-3549-49cd-a0e9-4b0721b3bb28.jpg)
Screenshots from Visual Studio Code, with glTF Tools extension.

**Platform:**
 - Device: desktop
 - OS: Windows 10
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r142
 
***Example file***
[occlusiontest_gltf.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/9067008/occlusiontest_gltf.zip)

Thanks for listening!

author: WestLangley

title: Improvement to PropertyBinding track name parsing and/or documentation
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I am using a KeyframeTrack to control properties on a ShaderMaterial and had a handful of issues getting things working correctly.
PropertyBinding uses the KeyframeTrack name, but I was limited to the depth of properties I could access. Peeking the [PropertyBinding code](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/animation/PropertyBinding.js#L148) and parseTrackName, I can see the limit. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12730229/177956274-5b6a845f-6b28-42c5-9822-279c94982eae.png)

Setting a single float within a materials was fine, but I struggled with anything else. I couldn't directly access the X, Y, Z or W components and trying to set a value to a new Vector4 resulted in a NaN at runtime. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12730229/177956390-42b57958-a797-4b1c-ae9c-860ec1d1a0fe.png)

The workaround for this was to expose an X, Y, Z and W float, and combine them within the shader.

Additionally, getting to this point took some working out - the syntax (imo) isn't very intuitive, and the [documentation on parseTrackName](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/animation/PropertyBinding.parseTrackName) didn't really help until it clicked with me with the help of some errors.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I believe the limits to parseTrackName should be reviewed, at least for Material/s.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

As there is a workaround for this as I've described above, updating the documentation to make it a little more clear on how this works and what track names should adhere to would also be ideal.

body: **Describe the bug** The performance of the LineSegments object has suffered a 1000% deterioration between version 0.117.0 and version 0.118.0. The performace degradation is present in all versions after 0.117.0. Even in the current version 0.142.0. **To Reproduce** Create a LineSegments object with enough number of segments eg 400000, compare performance between version 0.117.0 and version 0.118.0. Repeat the experiment with version 0.142.0 Steps to reproduce the behavior: 1. Open chrome 2. Go to [release 0.118.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/three-js-start-zu1bf5?file=index.ts) 3. Open Inspector 4. Go to performance tab 5. Start registration 6. Drag and zoom on canvas 7. Stop registration 8. Focus on gpu time frame 9. Go to [release 0.117.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/three-js-start-vxs5qr?file=index.ts) 10. Open Inspector 11. Go to performance tab 12. Start registration 13. Drag and zoom on canvas 14. Stop registration 15. Focus on gpu time frame 16. Compare point 8 and point 15 ***Code*** ```js import * as THREE from 'three'; import { OrbitControls } from 'three/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls'; const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true, alpha: true, }); renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight); document.getElementById('app').appendChild(renderer.domElement); const scene = new THREE.Scene(); const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera( 75, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 1000000 ); camera.position.x = 40; camera.position.y = 25; camera.position.z = 50; const controls = new OrbitControls(camera, renderer.domElement); controls.screenSpacePanning = false; const geometry = new THREE.BufferGeometry(); geometry.setFromPoints(generateSegmment()); const material = new THREE.LineBasicMaterial({}); material.color = new THREE.Color(0xff0000); const object = new THREE.LineSegments(geometry, material); object.frustumCulled = false; object.matrixAutoUpdate = false; object.updateMatrixWorld(true); scene.add(object); animate(); function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); renderer.render(scene, camera); } function generateSegmment(): Array { const segments: Array = []; let k = 0; for (let i = 0; i < 50000; i++) { const pointA: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i, 0, i - k); const pointB: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i, 0, i + 10); const pointC: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i - 10 - k, 0, i - k); const pointD: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i - 10 - k, 0, i + 10); const pointE: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i, 0, i + 10); const pointF: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i - 10 - k, 0, i + 10); const pointG: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i, 0, i - k); const pointH: THREE.Vector3 = new THREE.Vector3(i - 10 - k, 0, i - k); k += 2; segments.push( pointA, pointB, pointC, pointD, pointE, pointF, pointG, pointH ); } return segments; } ``` ***Live example*** * [release 0.117.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/three-js-start-vxs5qr?file=index.ts) * [release 0.118.0](https://stackblitz.com/edit/three-js-start-zu1bf5?file=index.ts) **Expected behavior** Have the same performance as version 0.117.0 **Screenshots** Please see gpu time 0.117.0 0.118.0 **Platform:** - Device: [Desktop] - OS: [MacOS] - Browser: [Chrome] - Three.js version: [r118, r119,r120,r121,r122,r123,r124,r125,r126,r140,r141,r142]

author: doczoidberg

title: Configure WebXR camera layers
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

By default the WebXR cameras don't match the layers configured on your main scene camera. 

This poses an issue if you make heavy use of layers to show and hide certain objects dynamically, as people in VR will see different things to people outside of VR.

The WebXRManager exposes the `.getCamera()` method which returns an empty ArrayCamera, and once XR starts `ArrayCamera.cameras` is populated with the left and right eye cameras.

All three of these XR cameras need to have their layers updated to match the same functionality as the non-XR camera.

To complicate this more, care is needed when modifying the layers of the child cameras as the left eye is hardcoded to use layer 1 and the right eye layer 2, and these shouldn't be modified.

## Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally the WebXRManager would automatically inherit the layers used by the main camera, but this poses a problem when the xr cameras have a tight requirement/convention on layers 1 and 2 for the left and right eye. It might be more feasible to use layers 31 and 32 to reduce chance of end user issues.

A simpler solution might be to add something like `WebXRManager.setLayers(bitmask)` which updates all the cameras for you, and also checks to ensure you dont break the left and right eye layer convention.

An even simpler solution could be to just expose the left and right eye cameras, eg `WebXRManager.getLeftCamera()`. Ultimately this would provide maximum potential with the lowest effort ✅

## Describe alternatives you've considered

For now we're resorting to a hack where before we render each frame we run this function, and exit early once the cameras are configured.

```jsx
configureXRCamera() {
  /**
   * HACK
   * ----
   * The WebXRManager uses its own cameras and they don't match the layers
   * configured on our main scene camera.
   * This is worsened by the fact that we don't have an easy way to preconfigure
   * these camera layers as WebXRManager.getCamera() returns an empty ArrayCamera
   * that isn't populated until XR starts.
   * In addition, for some reason both the ArrayCamera and its sub-cameras need to
   * have layers all updated.
   */
  if (this.xrCameraConfigured) return
  if (this.xrCamera.cameras.length) {
    // configure main xr camera
    this.xrCamera.layers.enableAll()
    this.xrCamera.layers.disable(GraphicsLayers.GHOST)
    // configure left eye camera
    this.xrCamera.cameras[0].layers.enableAll()
    this.xrCamera.cameras[0].layers.disable(GraphicsLayers.XR_RIGHT_EYE)
    this.xrCamera.cameras[0].layers.disable(GraphicsLayers.GHOST)
    // configure right eye camera
    this.xrCamera.cameras[1].layers.enableAll()
    this.xrCamera.cameras[1].layers.disable(GraphicsLayers.XR_LEFT_EYE)
    this.xrCamera.cameras[1].layers.disable(GraphicsLayers.GHOST)
    // never run this again!
    this.xrCameraConfigured = true
  }
}
```

## Additional context

None

title: Fog color from the environment
body:
**Context**
I want to make a realistic scene where there's the terrain + sky box environment. I'm using a Sky shader from this example: https://threejs.org/examples/?q=water#webgl_shaders_ocean

I want to make the terrain kind of fade into the environment using something like fog, but the problem is that it doesn't look realistic if I try to match the colors manually. And also to make it look realistic it should depend on the time of the day.

**The solution I'd like**

I want to use the environment color for the fog and be able to define its density. That would be cool :)
Maybe you could just point me in the right direction on how to set it up and I'll create a shader by myself and/or a PR. I'm not very aware of places in the code where it could be done in the easiest way possible.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Solution 1: Use a separate shader and keep a standard`scene.fog` disabled.
Solution 2: changing the fog's shader (which probably isn't a good idea)

title: Half Edge Structure for Custom Models.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

As I was working on my bachelor thesis, I needed to create a Half Edge Structure for any given model. I had problems that I had no way to access already created `HalfEdge` Class in [/examples/jsm/math/ConvexHull.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/math/ConvexHull.js). Also the problem is that this structure is only used in `ConvexHull.js` however it could be used to create HalfEdge structure from any 3D Model. )
**Describe the solution you'd like**
- I suggest to move `HalfEdge` Class to new file `HalfEdge.js`.
- Create a new Class `HalfEdgeStructure` with this very simple structure 
```
this.mesh = mesh
this.halfEdgeMap = new **Map()**
```

This Issue is related to following Issue:  **OBJLoader loses information about Vertex ID.** #24361
author: T4G2

title: OBJLoader loses information about Vertex ID.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

While I was working on my bachelor thesis, I needed to create a Half Edge Structure for any 3D model that was loaded. For this I needed after Loading. as you can see in issue  **Half Edge Structure for Custom Models.** #24360 ,
I will need to create a Half Edge structure more effectively a way to extract original vertex id from *.obj* files.

The way to extract would be quite simple, I would have to only store Vertex ID information into new List, and then I would return so the user can do anything they want with given structure (For example they could feed it into HalfEdge Structure to create a Half Edge Map).


There is an alternative to generate HalfEdge Representation of Mesh without this step, but it would be very ineffective as we would have to manually test, if there are points with the same vertex position to match them.


This Issue is related to this issue:  **Half Edge Structure for Custom Models.**#24360

title: Reflector (WebGLRenderTarget): Fog Color problem in sRGB outputEncoding
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

From https://github.com/mozilla/hubs/issues/5572

**Describe the bug**

Fog color in `Reflector` (`WebGLRenderTarget`) can mismatch the one in the main scene.

I made an online example to show the problem.

https://raw.githack.com/takahirox/three.js/MirrorFogExample/examples/webgl_mirror_fog.html

**Screenshots**

sRGB output encoding + fog. Fog color in the reflector looks brighter than the one in the main scene. (See the green wall.)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7637832/179628595-d1d35be0-892e-4a6c-8bc4-87b8f9f00411.png)

sRGB output encoding + no fog. Color in the reflector looks the same as the one in the main scene.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7637832/179628919-9d9ba6fb-f1e9-4ab7-918b-ff678b49d146.png)

Linear output encoding + fog. Fog color in the reflector looks the same as the one in the main scene.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7637832/179629040-070f2049-842d-4154-92ea-7a3e906c711c.png)

***Live example***

https://raw.githack.com/takahirox/three.js/MirrorFogExample/examples/webgl_mirror_fog.html

**Expected behavior**

Fog color in the reflector looks same as the one in the main scene even in sRGB outputEncoding.

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows 10
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: dev

title: Windows Mixed Reality causes webgl context loss when requesting WebXR
body:
IT could be a Microsoft OpenXR bug. But I am stumped to what is going on. 

On each fresh page load, the first WebXR session request will flicker a black screen and trigger a webgl context loss. It seems the loss happens after session is opened. But when a context is restored, the webgl context changes internally but the session breaks and fails in the headset. Its trying to reference the old session inside the webxr manager as displayed in the console screenshot.

The only work around I've found is closing the session on context loss and telling the user to try again. Which would be on every single fresh page load.

Its very difficult to replicate and only on Mixed Reality headsets with Edge Chrome. 

Using any webxr example

author: 7ombie

title: three-nodes  cannot  use  in react?
WebGPURenderer is computing the camera frustum incorrectly.
body:
For example, in `webgpu_skinning.html`, add:

```js
const f = new THREE.Frustum().setFromProjectionMatrix( camera.projectionMatrix );

console.log( f.planes[ 5 ] ); // - 0.500250
```

The logged plane constant should be `- camera.near`, which in this example is - 1.

---

As per the WebGPU spec, the NDC z-coordinate range is [ 0, 1 ] -- as opposed to WebGL, where the range is [ - 1, 1 ].

Consequently, `WebGPURenderer` requires a custom version of `Frustum.setFromProjectionMatrix()`.


I'll file a PR to fix this.

three.js version: [r.142]
author: WestLangley

title: Reflector Color Space Issues
body:
I am opening this as a separate issue in the hope we can keep the discussion focused.

Note that "color space" and "encoding" have still not been decoupled in the shader, so I am using legacy terminology here. Hopefully that will end soon.

1. `Reflector` should render in scene-referred linear color space -- to a float or half-float render target. (Or to an RGBM-encoded `UInt8` render target.) This is true, regardless of the renderer's output encoding. Setting the Reflector's `renderTarget.texture.encoding` to `renderer.outputEncoding` is not the right thing to do.

2. Consequently, it is only correct to include `encodings_fragment` in the reflector's fragment shader if it is needed for something like RGBM encoding. But three.js no longer supports such encodings.

3. When encoding to `sRGB` color space, tone mapping must proceed encoding. This is to ensure the inputs to the encoding step are in LDR. `Reflector` includes the encoding fragment, but not tone mapping fragment. Ideally, it should not include either of them.

Granted, encoding to sRGB and then decoding to linear-sRGB when reading back should not make a vast visual difference, except for rounding due to the Uint8 conversion. But still, it is unnecessary.

Feedback welcome.

three.js version: [r.142]

author: AngyDev

title: NodeMaterials: Remove or clarify usage of inherited varyings/uniforms from base materials
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently the node materials inject default uniforms and varyings from their base material (ShaderMaterial) and from their main shader node (Physical, Standard, Basic, etc.). This ends up adding a bunch of uniforms and varyings that may or may not be used and no clear way on how we might use them>

From the generated code in the [webgl_nodes_materials_standard](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=nodes#webgl_nodes_materials_standard) example:

```glsl
// Three.js r143 - NodeMaterial System

// <node_builder>

// uniforms
uniform sampler2D nodeUniform0;
uniform vec3 nodeUniform1;
uniform sampler2D nodeUniform4;
uniform sampler2D nodeUniform5; 

// attributes



// varyings
varying vec2 nodeVarying0;
varying vec4 nodeVarying1;
varying vec3 nodeVarying2;
varying vec3 nodeVarying3;
varying vec3 nodeVarying4; 

// vars

vec3 nodeVar0;
vec2 nodeVar1;
vec3 nodeVar2;
vec2 nodeVar3;
vec3 nodeVar4;
vec4 nodeVar5;
vec3 nodeVar6;
float nodeVar7; 

// codes



// </node_builder>

#define STANDARD
#ifdef PHYSICAL
    #define IOR
    #define SPECULAR
#endif
uniform vec3 diffuse;
uniform vec3 emissive;
uniform float roughness;
uniform float metalness;
uniform float opacity;

// ...

#if defined( USE_COLOR_ALPHA )
    varying vec4 vColor;
    #elif defined( USE_COLOR )
    varying vec3 vColor;
#endif
#if ( defined( USE_UV ) && ! defined( UVS_VERTEX_ONLY ) )
    varying vec2 vUv;
#endif
#if defined( USE_LIGHTMAP ) || defined( USE_AOMAP )
    varying vec2 vUv2;
#endif
#ifdef USE_MAP
    uniform sampler2D map;
#endif
#ifdef USE_ALPHAMAP
    uniform sampler2D alphaMap;
#endif
#ifdef USE_ALPHATEST
    uniform float alphaTest;
#endif
#ifdef USE_AOMAP
    uniform sampler2D aoMap;
    uniform float aoMapIntensity;
#endif
#ifdef USE_LIGHTMAP
    uniform sampler2D lightMap;
    uniform float lightMapIntensity;
#endif
#ifdef USE_EMISSIVEMAP
    uniform sampler2D emissiveMap;
#endif
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I'd really like a way to be able to properly define and reference these uniforms/varyings. Perhaps nodes like `UniformNode` and `AttributeNode` can check to see if there is already a defined uniform/attribute and reuse those?

Alternately, these uniforms/varyings could only be user defined. And I think there's a decent case to be made that this is the better option.

```glsl
#if ( defined( USE_UV ) && ! defined( UVS_VERTEX_ONLY ) )
    varying vec2 vUv;
#endif
#if defined( USE_LIGHTMAP ) || defined( USE_AOMAP )
    varying vec2 vUv2;
#endif

// ...

#ifdef USE_AOMAP
    float ambientOcclusion = ( texture2D( aoMap, vUv2 ).r - 1.0 ) * aoMapIntensity + 1.0;
    reflectedLight.indirectDiffuse *= ambientOcclusion;
    #if defined( USE_ENVMAP ) && defined( STANDARD )
        float dotNV = saturate( dot( geometry.normal, geometry.viewDir ) );
        reflectedLight.indirectSpecular *= computeSpecularOcclusion( dotNV, ambientOcclusion, material.roughness );
    #endif
#endif
```

The issue I'm currently trying to solve with node materials is being able to dynamically assign which uv gets used for each texture so that i can properly support glTF's `texCoord` property (See #12608). In the above snippet, you can see that because the node material is based on the other material, it pulls in the same assumptions about which uvs to use for each texture. This means, even if I wanted to write a node shader that dynamically assigned which uv set to use per texture, I couldn't for these maps.

I think `NodeMaterial` may be better off extending `RawShaderMaterial` and using a simplified `ShaderLib` to get rid of these assumptions and allow for maximum developer flexibility.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Perhaps this is already possible for uniforms with the `MaterialNode`? I really can't tell how that works.

**Additional context**

None

author: lumebot

title: FBXLoader: Fbx Models containing .tif-format textures do not work
body:
hey
When I use an fbx model with a .tif-format texture, I find that the texture is not displayed on the browser. This raises some questions, and I hope to find answers.

1. Why wasn't support for .tif-format textures considered?
  After consulting some information and related issues(#436), I learned that many browsers do not natively support .tif-format textures. By observing the source code of `ImageLoader`, I found that three.js works by displaying images through `img` tags.
  Is it possible to make three.js support .tif-format by extending `ImageLoader`? In fact, I made the image display by modifying the `ImageLoader` component, using `tiff.js` to load the `tiff resource`, and using canvas to convert it to `png`.

2. Another problem is derived from the above problem. `FBXLoader` does not seem to provide any extensibility. Each subcomponent is directly `new` in the method. When I want to modify or extend the logic of some subcomponents, I have to put the entire `FBXLoader` Taking it out for modification alone makes the modification cost very large and is not conducive to subsequent maintenance. May I ask if this is the original intention of the original design (do not want others to modify the source code) or other reasons?

title: OrbitControls events strange behaviour
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

I am using "Orbitontrols" and these 3 events which are "start", "change" and "end".
When we click just on the screen without changing the camera position etc. the "start" and "end" events are hired, which is strange because we dont do any mouse "hold" click etc. And some times (randomly, difficult to reproduce) when we click the 2nd time on the screen, the "change" event is also fired..

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Use these 3 events
2. Click on the screen, only 1 click without any mouse hold etc.
3. See at the console, you will see the messages fired by events
4. Click a 2nd time and if you have a chance, "change" event will be also fired

***Code***

```js
	let controls = new OrbitControls(homeThreeJS.camera, homeThreeJS.renderer.domElement);
	controls.minPolarAngle = 0;
	controls.maxPolarAngle = Math.PI * 0.5;
	controls.enablePan = false;
	controls.target.set(0, 0, 0);
	controls.minDistance = 2.9;
	controls.maxDistance = 3;
	controls.update();

	controls.addEventListener("start", function () {
		console.log("Change started");
	});

	controls.addEventListener("change", function () {
		console.log("Changed!");
	});

	controls.addEventListener("end", function () {
		console.log("Change end!");
	});
```

***Live example***

* [https://codesandbox.io/s/03-repeat-texture-coordinates-forked-p1ljqy?file=/src/Gl.js](https://codesandbox.io/s/03-repeat-texture-coordinates-forked-p1ljqy?file=/src/Gl.js)

**Expected behavior**

"Start" and "end" events should be fired only if we click and hold the mouse to change the camera position

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r143

title: Color: Support HSL percentage values with decimals.
body:
**Describe the bug**

Using decimal values for `saturation` or `lightness` in an HSL colors lead to `Unknown color` warning

**To Reproduce**

see live example

***Code***

```js
new THREE.Color('hsl(209, 95.0%, 90.1%)')
```

***Live example***

https://jsfiddle.net/4v1rp8d0/9/

**Expected behavior**

The color should be properly parsed. It looks like [this regex is the issue](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/math/Color.js#L225)

[According to the MDN docs for `percentage`, decimals are supported for `number`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/number).
author: JackCA

title: merge method in BufferGeometry class should automatically compute correct value for the offset parameter, not default it to zero when it is not provided.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Yes, the .merge() method includes an optional "offset" parameter. If this is not provided, then merge defaults this offset to zero, which causes an overwrite instead of a merge. 

A clear and concise description of what the problem is. Ex. I'm always frustrated when [...]
Some discussions seem to suggest that the offset parameter is a matrix, which seems like a logical assumption to make. Unfortunately, this is not how the actual implementation works. Therefore the "literature" and "logic" are not consistent with the actual implementation, and this leads to confusion. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**
1) If the "offset" argument is not provided, then a merge should occur, not an overwrite.
2) I do not understand the point of having an optional offset argument that is an integer, so this optional argument should be removed.
3) I do understand the idea of an optional argument that applies a matrix to the merged geometry, but I would rather see an example in the documentation that simply shows users how to apply the matrix transform to the geometry that is being merged.

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.
Fix merge, remove the optional "offset" argument. Add documentation to the .merge method to show the correct procedure for transforming the geometry that is being merged.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Fix merge, change the second optional "offset" argument to be a matrix instead of an integer.

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

**Additional context**

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

author: kamildul

title: WebGLShaderCache poor performance
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

The WebGLShaderCache uses full vertex/fragment shader source code as keys in a `Map<string, WebGLShaderStage>` and performs several lookups per frame, causing noticeably poor performance.

**To Reproduce**

1. Create a RawShaderMaterial subclass with a non-trivial vertex and/or fragment shader (a few hundred lines of GLSL)
2. Render 10k object instances using this material
3. Record a performance trace in Chrome dev tools
4. Observe the time spent in `WebGLShaderCache#_getShaderStage()`

**Expected behavior**

* Avoid double-lookups in the common path: instead of `.has()` followed by `.get()`, use a single `.get()` when possible
* Allow materials to specify their own `vertexShaderKey` and `fragmentShaderKey` strings that will be used instead of the full shader source code

I have made both of these changes in a patched version of three.js we use in Foxglove Studio (see https://github.com/foxglove/studio/blob/main/patches/three.patch). I'd be happy to turn this into a PR for three.js if this seems like the right approach.

**Platform:**

 - Device: All
 - OS: All
 - Browser: All
 - Three.js version: dev
author: jhurliman

title: Raycasting: Wrong intersection point when scene rotated
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

`THREE.Raycaster.intersectsObject()` returns no or wrong intersection coordinates when the `THREE.scene` is rotated using `scene.rotation.x = 270 * (Math.PI / 180)`.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior (or checkout live example):
1. Create a scenario where a Raycaster is set to go clearly through an object
2. Test intersection results => `Raycaster.intersectsObject()` should return the correct intersection coordiates
3. Add `scene.rotation.x = 270 * (Math.PI / 180)` after creating scene or anywhere before calculating intersections
4. Test intersection results => `Raycaster.intersectsObject()` should return no intersections or intersections with wrong coordinates

**Demonstration**
red cube: raycaster starting point  
blue cube:   raycaster destination point  
green point: calculated intersection  
yellow arrow: raycaster direction vector  

***Code***

```js
scene.rotation.x = 270 * (Math.PI / 180)

const raycaster = new THREE.Raycaster(position, p2, 0.1, 10)

const arrowHelper = new THREE.ArrowHelper(raycaster.ray.direction, raycaster.ray.origin, 4, 0xffff00)
scene.add(arrowHelper)

renderer.render(scene, camera)

const intersects = raycaster.intersectObject(cube, false)

// mark each intersection with a little cube
intersects.forEach((intersection) => createCube(intersection.point, 0x00ff00, 0.1))

```

***Live example***

Comment out line 46 (scene rotation) to see the effect
* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/6jhpk5vd/)

**Expected behavior**

The cube should have the same coordinates even if the scene is rotated.

**Screenshots**
See demonstration for a legend of the colored cubes
normal scene: intersection works:  
![SceneWithoutRotation](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33429771/183129070-0c8f621c-68ec-4396-8e08-2b7230b62f53.png)

rotated scene: intersection is behind intersection:  
![SceneWithRotation](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33429771/183129056-1459b9c0-d420-4071-ad50-9054a6fc443e.png)

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Linux
 - Browser: Chrome, Firefox
 - Three.js version: r143, dev

title: CMYK images don't work as textures on iPhone
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

When an image has CMYK color mode and is used as texture, the texture won't show on iPhone IOS 15.5. Tested with Safari and iPhone Chrome. They work just fine on PC browsers. 

I explicitly tested with images converted to CMYK color mode using Photoshop (from PSD main menu >> image >> mode >> CMYK color).


**To Reproduce**

```js
// CMYK img - doesn't work in iphone
const geometry = new THREE.BoxGeometry(1, 1, 1);
const texture = new THREE.TextureLoader().load("img-cmyk.jpg");
const material = new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ map: texture });
const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
```


***Live example***

* https://ritwaldev.github.io/testThree/

title: The preview FBX file reports an error of cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'UUID')
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

I failed to preview the FBX file with threejs, and the console gave an 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'uuid')' error prompt

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Upload files to the server
2. Get the preview address from the server and start the plug-in initialization
3. See error ‘TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'uuid')’

***Code***

```js
// code goes here
ThreeDModule.prototype.loadFile = function (self) {
    console.log("self", self);
    var filePath = self.source;
    var fillColor = self.params.threeDModuleFillColor;
    var inputMode = self.params.threeDModuleInputMode;
    var $wrapper = self.$wrapper;
    var wrapperWidth = $wrapper.width();
    var wrapperHegiht = $wrapper.height();

    var camera, scene, renderer;
    var clock = new THREE.Clock();
    var mixer;

    init();
    animate();

    function init() {
      const container = document.createElement("div");
      container.setAttribute("class", "file-container");
      $wrapper.append(container);

      // 设置透视投影相机, 参数：视场、长宽比、近面、远面
      camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(
        45,
        wrapperWidth / wrapperHegiht,
        1,
        2000
      );
      // 设置相机位置
      camera.position.set(100, 30, 80);

      // 设置场景
      scene = new THREE.Scene();
      scene.background = new THREE.Color("#dcdcdc");
      // 设置场景雾化
      // scene.fog = new THREE.Fog(0xa0a0a0, 200, 1000);

      //给场景增加环境光
      let Ambient = new THREE.AmbientLight(0x404040, 2);
      scene.add(Ambient);

      // 设置光源
      // const hemiLight = new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xffffff, 0x444444);
      // hemiLight.position.set(0, 200, 0);
      // 场景中添加光源
      // scene.add(hemiLight);

      // 设置方向光
      const dirLight = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 1);
      dirLight.position.set(20, 200, 100);
      // 阴影
      dirLight.castShadow = true;
      //设置相机渲染面积
      dirLight.shadow.camera.top = 180;
      dirLight.shadow.camera.bottom = -100;
      dirLight.shadow.camera.left = -120;
      dirLight.shadow.camera.right = 120;
      // 向场景中添加方向光
      scene.add(dirLight);

      // ground
      // Mesh：设置网格对象，PlaneGeometry: 二维平面几何体接收宽高参数, MeshPhongMaterial: 材质
      const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(
        new THREE.PlaneGeometry(2000, 2000),
        new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial({ color: 0x999999, depthWrite: false })
      );
      mesh.rotation.x = -Math.PI / 2;
      mesh.receiveShadow = true;
      scene.add(mesh);

      // 设置网格辅助线, 接收参数：网格宽度;等分数;中心线颜色;网格线颜色
      const grid = new THREE.GridHelper(2000, 20, 0x000000, 0x000000); //
      grid.material.opacity = 0.2;
      grid.material.transparent = true;
      scene.add(grid);

      // 设置fbx文件加载器
      const loader = new THREE.FBXLoader();

      loader.load(filePath, function (object) {
        // 模型缩放系数
        object.scale.multiplyScalar(4);

        // 设置变形动画
        mixer = new THREE.AnimationMixer(object);

        const action = mixer.clipAction(object.animations[0]);
        action.play();

        object.traverse(function (child) {
          if (child.isMesh) {
            child.castShadow = true;
            child.receiveShadow = true;
            // 把发光颜色 强度 贴图 都换掉，否则模型会变黑
            child.material.emissive = new THREE.Color(hexify(fillColor));
            child.material.emissiveIntensity = 1;
            child.material.emissiveMap = child.material.map;
          }
        });

        scene.add(object);
      });

      // 设置渲染器
      renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true, alpha: true });
      renderer.setPixelRatio(window.devicePixelRatio);
      // 渲染尺寸
      renderer.setSize(wrapperWidth, wrapperHegiht);
      renderer.shadowMap.enabled = true;
      container.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

      // 设置控制器
      const controls = new THREE.OrbitControls(camera, renderer.domElement);
      controls.target.set(0, 20, 15); // 设置控制器位置
      controls.update();
      self.on("resize", onWrapperResize, $wrapper);
    }

    function animate() {
      requestAnimationFrame(animate);

      const delta = clock.getDelta();

      if (mixer) mixer.update(delta);

      renderer.render(scene, camera);
    }
    function onWrapperResize() {
      wrapperWidth = this.width();
      wrapperHegiht = this.height();
      camera.aspect = wrapperWidth / wrapperHegiht;
      camera.updateProjectionMatrix();
      renderer.setSize(wrapperWidth, wrapperHegiht);
    }
    /**
     * rgba转16进制
     * @param color
     * @returns {string}
     */
    function hexify(color) {
      var values = color
        .replace(/rgba?\(/, "")
        .replace(/\)/, "")
        .replace(/[\s+]/g, "")
        .split(",");
      var a = parseFloat(values[3] || 1),
        r = Math.floor(a * parseInt(values[0]) + (1 - a) * 255),
        g = Math.floor(a * parseInt(values[1]) + (1 - a) * 255),
        b = Math.floor(a * parseInt(values[2]) + (1 - a) * 255);
      return (
        "#" +
        ("0" + r.toString(16)).slice(-2) +
        ("0" + g.toString(16)).slice(-2) +
        ("0" + b.toString(16)).slice(-2)
      );
    }
  };
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/hbfpj2wv/2/)
* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/086yzvgc/)

**Expected behavior**

Preview FBX file normally

**Screenshots**

If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem (drag and drop the image).
![微信截图_20220808094301](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/39852145/183324485-2655c3c9-9c23-4171-ba41-e9d393d19793.png)

title: ArcballControls - Extra rotation after recreating the controls
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

If a camera rotation is performed using ArcballControls _before_ recreating the controls, an extra rotation  happens after releasing the mouse on subsequent rotations. If no camera rotation is performed before recreating the controls, everything works fine.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a basic Three.js setup 
2. Add ArcballControls to it
3. Make some function / logic to recreate the controls on demand
4. Launch the page and rotate camera around
5. Trigger the controls' re-creation function / logic
6. Try to rotate camera around again
7. Notice the extra rotation after releasing the mouse button

***Code***

```js
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <style>
      body {margin: 0;}
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <script type="importmap">
      {
        "imports":
        {
          "three": "http://localhost:8080/three.module.js",
          "ArcballControls": "http://localhost:8080/ArcballControls.js"
        }
      }
    </script>
    <script type="module">
      import * as THREE from "three";
      import {ArcballControls} from "ArcballControls";
      var scene, camera, light, renderer, earth, controls, reqid, paused = false;
      function createcontrols()
      {
        controls = new ArcballControls(camera, renderer.domElement, scene);
        controls.addEventListener("change", function(e) {renderer.render(scene, camera); controls.update();});
      };
      function create()
      {
        renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({antialias: true, alpha: true});
        renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
        document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);
        camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(70, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 100);
        camera.position.set(0, 0, 1.75);
        light = new THREE.AmbientLight("rgb(255, 255, 255)");
        light.position.copy(camera.position);
        var geometry = new THREE.SphereGeometry(1, 360, 180);
        var material = new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial();
        material.map = new THREE.TextureLoader().load("http://localhost:8080/Earth.png");
        material.displacementMap = new THREE.TextureLoader().load("http://localhost:8080/Earth - Height.png");
        material.displacementScale = 0.002 * 99;
        earth = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
        scene = new THREE.Scene();
        scene.add(camera);
        scene.add(light);
        scene.add(earth);
        createcontrols();
        document.addEventListener("keyup", function(e)
        {
          switch (e.code)
          {
            case "KeyP": console.log("P"); paused = !paused; if (!paused) {animate();} else {stagnate();}; break;
            case "KeyR": console.log("R"); controls.dispose(); controls = undefined; createcontrols(); renderer.render(scene, camera); controls.update(); break;
          };
        }, {passive: false});
      };
      function animate()
      {
        if (paused) {return;};
        requestAnimationFrame(animate);
        earth.rotation.y += 0.01;
        renderer.render(scene, camera);
      };
      function stagnate()
      {
        if (!paused) {return;};
        cancelAnimationFrame(reqid);
      };
      create();
      animate();
    </script>
  </body>
</html>
```

***Live example***

None

**Expected behavior**

For the simpleton code above, drag to rotate the globe, press the R key to recreate ArcballControls, then rotate again to see how it "jumps" to a new rotation after releasing the mouse. I expect that the jump does not happen (like it doesn't for the other controls). My actual usage scenario is switching from one control to another (all 8 included in my code) on some mouse scroll, by the way - I chose to illustrate recreating the controls in the interest of simplicity. Let me know if this can be replicated, or if by any chance there is something obvious missing from the code above (like updating some matrix and such) that fixes the issue.

title: Raycaster ignores displacement maps and their effct on geometries
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

I'm not sure if this is a bug or the expected behavior, but according to the [Three.js manual](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/materials/MeshPhongMaterial.displacementMap), "a displacement map affects the position of the mesh's vertices" and "the displaced vertices can cast shadows, block other objects, and otherwise act as real geometry", so based on that, they should affect the results produced by a Raycaster. However, they do not.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a basic Three.js setup
2. Rotate the camera around an object that is using a displacement map
3. Use Raycaster to cast a ray towards the object
4. Notice that the logged distance to the intersect point with the object doesn't change

***Code***

```js
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <style>
      body {margin: 0;}
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <script type="importmap">
      {
        "imports":
        {
          "three": "http://localhost:8080/three.module.js",
          "ArcballControls": "http://localhost:8080/ArcballControls.js"
        }
      }
    </script>
    <script type="module">
      import * as THREE from "three";
      import {ArcballControls} from "ArcballControls";
      var scene, camera, light, renderer, earth, controls, reqid, paused = false;
      function createcontrols()
      {
        controls = new ArcballControls(camera, renderer.domElement, scene);
        controls.addEventListener("change", function(e) {renderer.render(scene, camera); controls.update();});
      };
      function create()
      {
        renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({antialias: true, alpha: true});
        renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
        document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);
        camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(70, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 100);
        camera.position.set(0, 0, 1.75);
        light = new THREE.AmbientLight("rgb(255, 255, 255)");
        light.position.copy(camera.position);
        var geometry = new THREE.SphereGeometry(1, 360, 180);
        var material = new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial();
        material.map = new THREE.TextureLoader().load("http://localhost:8080/Earth.png");
        material.displacementMap = new THREE.TextureLoader().load("http://localhost:8080/Earth - Height.png");
        material.displacementScale = 0.002 * 99;
        earth = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
        scene = new THREE.Scene();
        scene.add(camera);
        scene.add(light);
        scene.add(earth);
        createcontrols();
        document.addEventListener("keyup", function(e)
        {
          switch (e.code)
          {
            case "KeyP": console.log("P"); paused = !paused; if (!paused) {animate();} else {stagnate();}; break;
          };
        }, {passive: false});
      };
      function raycast()
      {
        var cameradirection = new THREE.Vector3();
        camera.getWorldDirection(cameradirection);
        cameradirection.normalize();
        var cameraorigin = new THREE.Vector3();
        camera.getWorldPosition(cameraorigin);
        var raycaster = new THREE.Raycaster(cameraorigin, cameradirection);
        var intersects = raycaster.intersectObjects([earth], false);
        if (intersects.length) {console.log(intersects[0].distance);};
      };
      function animate()
      {
        if (paused) {return;};
        requestAnimationFrame(animate);
        earth.rotation.y += 0.01;
        renderer.render(scene, camera);
        raycast();
      };
      function stagnate()
      {
        if (!paused) {return;};
        cancelAnimationFrame(reqid);
      };
      create();
      animate();
    </script>
  </body>
</html>
```

***Live example***

None

**Expected behavior**

I expect Raycaster to react to the effects that the displacement map "should" have on the geometry of the object (and not only on its material, according to the manual), and log different distances to the intersection point with the object, based on the "height of the terrain" being displaced. I intentionally set a considerable displacement scale in the code above to make it even more obvious. My actual usage scenario is preventing the camera to approach closer than the minimum distance to an object, but according to the altitudes / terrain on the object's surface. Let me know if Raycaster ignoring displacement maps is a bug or it's something expected to happen due to the displacement maps being just as "fake" as the bump maps (i.e. not affecting the _real_ geometry of the object).

**Screenshots**

None

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows 10
 - Browser: Chrome, Edge WebView2 via the [WebView plugin](https://github.com/khanhas/PluginWebView) for [Rainmeter](https://www.rainmeter.net/)
 - Three.js version: r.140, r.143

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

**Additional context**
1. Using threejs to load a 2d dynamic image, like I use nrrd loader. the slice position can be modified by user or we can get one position all volume data, then we may use dataarraytexture to render it.
2. A Dicom image loader.

author: sdarpeng

title: FileLoader: Range request support
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently `FileLoader` doesn't support [HTTP Range Requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Range_requests). It can send the request with Range header, but it doesn't accept [HTTP status 206](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/206) now.

```
const loader = new FileLoader();
loader.setRequestHeader({ Range: 'bytes=0-12' });
loader.load(url, ...);
// File loader error due to unknown HTTP status 206
```

I hope it supports the range requests.

Usecase example: I'm thinking of letting `GLTFLoader` load partial buffer for a buffer view. With the range requests support, partial asset loading (eg. with glTF LOD) can save network usage even with bundle glb because it will no longer need to download the entire buffer at once.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

`FileLoader` supports HTTP Range Requests.

Changes:
* Accept HTTP status 206. (But according to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/13082#issuecomment-357242253, problematic if a server support `gzip`? I don't perfectly understand yet.)
* Workaround for servers that don't support range requets. [They ignore Range header and respond with HTTP status 200 (full content)](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Range_requests#partial_request_responses).

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Make another loader for range requests in the core, examples, or an external user repository.

title: Nrrd loader has axis issues when load 16-bits image
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

When we use nrrd loader to load 16-bits nrrd images, the result will lose some value of axisOder array. if we load the NRRD example file we can get this `axisOrder = ["x", "y", "z"];`, but when we load ourself nrrd image it will get this:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80797317/183823301-95a25e6b-dc7c-4564-afa2-e9af22a044c8.png)
this is because the data format is different. the example nrrd image is based on 32-bits, but the ITK software only can export 16-bits file.

To fix this issue we can manually reset the axisOrder, like this:
```js
new NRRDLoader().load(
    url,
    function (volume: any) {
      volume.axisOrder = ["x", "y", "z"];
      })
``` 

But if we want to use this data in Data3DTexture, we still need to convert 16-bits data to 32-bits manually.

Also, another suggestion is when we use nrrd loader, can we get position's all volume data? Is it possible? because if we want to do [DataArrayTexture example](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=2d#webgl2_rendertarget_texture2darray), we can use one position's volume data as a parameter into DataArrayTexture to create a dynamic 2d image and render in threejs with other models.

As for Nrrdloader, when users load the nrrd image, could threejs add an attribute in volume to return different positions volume data?
e.g, 
```js
volume:{
  volumeData_x,
 volumeData_y,
 volumeData_z,
}
```
Currently, we only can get the whole image volume data.

If we can get each position's volume data, we can do many things. For example, we can only use z-positions volume data in DataArrayTexture.

**Screenshot**
If we directly load nrrd image and use dataarraytexture to render it we can get this:

![a_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80797317/183826292-98acfb2b-5383-4fb9-bf97-65beef4ae711.gif)

three positions data are rendered together.

I also did this render nrrd image: 
![a_2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80797317/183826832-b039e19f-bc47-43f9-9103-74503f2de2ac.gif)

The basic solution is I use a setimeout function to repaint the slice in an ordered number(index).
But you can see the drawback is the position will move, this is because each slice has it's own position and can't be modified.

You can get 16-bits NRRD image [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1_fQ3rAsfu1XykAVZZuKSBKWLAHal-pdx/view?usp=sharing).


**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop]
 - OS: [ MacOS,]
 - Browser: [Chrome,]
 - Three.js version: [dev, r143]
author: LinkunGao

title: GLTFLoader: HTTP Range Requests support
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

`GLTFLoader` first loads the entire bundle glb file and then starts the parse. It is inefficient for network usage if users want to first partially load the assets and then lazily load the others on demand (eg. glTF LOD extension).

**Describe the solution you'd like**

[Support HTTP Range Requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Range_requests) in `GLTFLoader`.

Changes probably needed:
* `FileLoader` supports HTTP Range Requests #24485
* First load only the glb header
* Load partial buffer for a buffer view with HTTP Range Requests

**Additional context**

With the changes, the number of requests can be many. How will the response time be? Longer because of many requests? Or shorter because of the parallelism?

title: Memory Leak in LineGeometry
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Calling the [`setPositions`](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9bb4782c22d7913ab49510b6ed8f51760f916759/examples/jsm/lines/LineSegmentsGeometry.js#L66) function of LineGeometry or LineSegmentsGeometry multiple times results in a memory leak that can crash the application. This happens because every time its called it creates a new Float32Array, InstancedInterleavedBuffer and two InterleavedBufferAttribute, instead of reusing the old ones.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a scene with ~400 Line2s
2. During every frame, call `setPositions` on their geometry to update them
3. Let it run for some seconds
4. The entire browser freezes and the WebGL context gets lots

***Code***

```js
import * as THREE from "https://unpkg.com/three/build/three.module.js";
import { Line2 } from "https://unpkg.com/three/examples/jsm/lines/Line2.js";

var lines, renderer, scene, camera;

// Increase this number until it crashes (100 is enough for firefox)
const n = 5;

init();
animate();

function init() {
  renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer();
  renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
  renderer.setPixelRatio(window.devicePixelRatio);
  document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

  scene = new THREE.Scene();

  camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(
    40,
    window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight,
    0.1,
    10000
  );
  camera.position.set(0, 0, 4);

  lines = new Array(n).fill(0).map(() => new Line2());
  lines.forEach((line) => scene.add(line));
}

function animate(t) {
  requestAnimationFrame(animate);

  lines.forEach((line, i) => {
    line.material.resolution.set(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
    const x = Math.cos(t + i / 20) * 2;
    const y = Math.sin(t + i / 20) * 2;
    line.geometry.setPositions([x, y, 0, x * 2 * (i / n), y * 2 * (i / n), 0]);
  });
  renderer.render(scene, camera);
}
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/2hmrqtd6/13/)

**Expected behavior**

Not crash, reusing the same attributes.

**Screenshots**

Not necessary.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop]
 - OS: [MacOS]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox]
 - Three.js version: [dev, r143]

body: Regardless of which language you are targeting, if it doesn't work properly than you have a bug. Your docs here: https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/core/Object3D This tells me every function and property that should be on an Object3D - accept that isn't what I am seeing, when I am creating one. It isn't what I am seeing when I load a mesh from JSON into it either. For example - here I have added a box to the scene. I traverse the graph, get the object and save it to a JSON object in order to attempt to get some details from the object. Simple code - grab any mesh for this: ` //Convert to the object model var objectJSON = mesh.toJSON(); const newObj = new Object3D(); console.log("Generated Object =>",newObj); const loader = new ObjectLoader(); var newObjectMesh = loader.parse(objectJSON,()=>{}); console.warn("New Object from object =>",newObjectMesh);` I then load that JSON object in, using ObjectLoader=> parse. This is the object graph returned: You can see on this image, that the box is not in the centre of the screen, yet the loaded object has a zeroed out Position Vector 3. You can also see that the methods and properties are missing from that Vector3 as described in your docs: https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/math/Vector3 Ok, so I then take a look at the Matrix of the object. It has the data in it, but none of the methods or properties described in your docs are actually on the object at all: https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/math/Matrix4 Now if you tell me that's because of your stripped down JSON scenario - then I say, fantastic. But please do show me in the docs or anywhere else, where I can convert that Matrix4 to a proper Matrix4 object, or a Vector3 to a proper Vector3 - so I can then call the methods to get the correct position/rotation/scale of the object. As a reminder also - not everyone uses stackoverflow because of their system of points etc. Your forums are also a fantastic place to understand things, but not a place to raise issues that unless can be explained adequately, are a bug. I will happily raise as many as these as possible in the coming weeks, with the only goal of helping to make the project better for it, if possible. Thanks for your help.

title: FBX Loader produces object with different orientation on first file load
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

The first FBX file I load with the FBX loader appears in the scene with incorrect orientation. All subsequent files loaded by the FBX file - including the re-loading the first file - appear in the scene with the expected, correct orientation.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Extract the FBX in [playset_tower.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/9427336/playset_tower.zip)
2. Go to https://threejs.org/editor/
3. Drag the FBX file onto the editor
4. The tower will appear laying on its side
5. Drag the same FBX file onto the editor again
6. The 2nd tower will appear standing upright

If, on the editor, you then go to File >> New and drag the FBX file on again, it will still appear standing upright. But, if you refresh the page, then next time you load the FBX file the tower will be laying down again.

**Expected behavior**

Loading the same FBX file should get the same result regardless of whether or not it is the first file loaded.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop]
 - OS: [Windows]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox, Edge]
 - Three.js version: [r143]




While trying to identify what was happening, I found in the FBX Loader that it has an internal variable called `tempEuler`. On the first load of the tower, `tempEuler` has an order of XYZ, but on all subsequent loads of the tower, `tempEuler` has an order of ZYX. I suspect that this is the cause of the different orientation of the tower between the first load and subsequent loads.

author: fulopkovacs

title: requestAnimationFrame called on "self" might not work on multi-window environments
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

On a NW.js application which runs in separated context mode, the global window object corresponds to a background, invisible window where requestAnimationFrame never fires. Additional windows spawned using the NW.js API have their own window variable (different from global.window). This causes problems with this line: 
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/8de7680efd897a088efb443ad136b9379ccd5bef/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L951

Since "self" will be pointing to the global window (background, invisible), the animation callback will never be triggered.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

A mechanism for the WebGLRenderer to set a custom window object, rather than having the global self hard-coded. For instance:

- If a canvas is specified in the constructor of WebGLRenderer, use ownerDocument.defaultView, or ownerDocument.parentWindow to know where to call requestAnimationFrame.
- Perhaps have the WebGLRenderer take an additional parameter to use as window, which would default to "self", even though that would be somewhat redundant considering the previous option.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

1 -> Overwrite the window.requestAnimationFrame function, so that it's executed by another window: this is very hackish and only works with one window, so if my application spawns two, each with a three.js canvas, it would break.

2 -> Use NW.js in mixed context mode: this way each spawned window has its own global window object. However, this works by running each window on a different process, which makes it hard to pass information back and forth.

4 -> Thanks to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23686 I can do something like:

`window.self = nw.Window.get().window; //Get NW.js child window object into the self reference three.js will use`
`renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true });`
`window.self = window; //Set the original window back to the global self reference`

This seems to work fine as well, but it's also extremely hackish.

4 -> Directly use requestAnimationFrame on the child window object, as it used to be done.
As far as I understand, this is currently discouraged in favor of setAnimationLoop for WebXR compatibility, but so far it's the best I've come up with. I guess it's an acceptable solution if WebXR support is never to be considered, and if everything else works as expected (please confirm?).

**Additional context**

Nw.js is a development platform by Intel which allows application programmers to combine a nodejs back-end with a HTML/CSS/JS front-end into a standalone app (similar to Electron):

https://nwjs.io/
https://docs.nwjs.io/en/latest/For%20Users/Advanced/JavaScript%20Contexts%20in%20NW.js/

In Nw.js, visible HTML interfaces don't use the default, global "window". Rather, the global window corresponds to an invisible, background object. This is an useful design choice for headless environments.

Thanks and kind regards.

~Sky

title: PlaneGeometry with 65536 vertices is missing last triangle
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

When creating a new `PlaneGeometry` where `(widthSegments+1)*(heightSegments+1) === 65536`, the last triangle (at the +x, -y corner) is missing, making the corner clipped.

***Code***

```js
// Also broken for 127 x 511, 63 x 1023, etc.
const geometry = new THREE.PlaneGeometry(1, 1, 255, 255);
```

***Live example***

* [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/three-js-buffergeometry-uint16-uint32-off-by-one-hwzxlf)



**Expected behavior**

The plane should be square and not missing the last triangle, as seen with other values of `widthSegments` and `heightSegments`.

**Screenshots**

Expected behavior (as seen with 256x256)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18251131/187490931-a74b9de2-a050-40b6-b1ad-5ab8d89af07a.png)


Actual behavior (as seen with 255x255)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18251131/187490746-37c70286-c5cf-4f6c-ac07-7b2d676f39b1.png)

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r143

**Other Notes:**

The bug seems to have something to do with THREE.js using a `Uint16Array` instead of a `Uint32Array` for the `geometry.index` buffer in this case. If https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/utils.js#L39 in `arrayNeedsUint32` is changed from
```
if ( array[ i ] > 65535 ) return true;
```
to
```
if ( array[ i ] >= 65535 ) return true;
```
a `Uint32Array` is used and the corner renders as expected. I'm not sure why this would be, though, because the last few elements of the index array appear to be unchanged by using a `Uint32Array`.

title: TransformControls gizmo is rendered upside down with orthographic camera
body: **Description** I ran into an interesting behavior after I bumped the three.js version from r0.141 to r0.142. In the r0.142 version, the gizmo rendering with orthographic cameras was updated (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24242/commits/c35b2e6b280456fedd0c0fea64c17590b08aeb2b) that turned the rotation gizmo upside down. I checked the commits and found that in the case of an orthographic camera, the direction is no longer determined from the subtraction of the camera and world positions but is retrieved from the camera using the getWorldDirection method. Unfortunately, the normal vector (from the Camera#getWorldDirection) points to the opposite direction which turns the gizmo orientation upside down: I’m not sure if it is a feature that I don’t handle properly or if it is a bug. **Platform:** - Device: Desktop - OS: MacOS - Browser: Chrome - Three.js version: r0.142

author: anbello

title: `import` fails to resolve when using `esbuild` build tool
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

When using the esbuild build tool, the import statement reports an error that cannot be resolved.

E.g:

```js
import { OrbitControls } from 'three/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls';
```

This is because of the following configuration:

```json
"exports": {
  "./examples/jsm/*": "./examples/jsm/*"
}
```

Although tools like rollup, webpack, etc. may be normal, obviously esbuild respects [node specification standards](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#package-entry-points) more.

According to the node specification, in order for the esbuild tool to work properly, the file extension needs to be added:

```js
import { OrbitControls } from 'three/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js';
```

Obviously when this happens, many people need to spend as much time as I do to analyze the cause of the problem.

In fact, the following configuration can also solve the problem:

```json
"exports": {
  "./examples/jsm/*": "./examples/jsm/*.js" 
}
```

In order to improve development efficiency (compilation speed), `esbuild` should have been widely used, can this problem be solved?

_see also https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/2518_

**Describe the solution you'd like**

1. For files of the same type (such as `.js`), change the `exports` configuration in package.json, for example

```json
"exports": {
  "./examples/jsm/*": "./examples/jsm/*.js"
}
```

2. For different categories of files contained in a folder, multiple configurations can be written, or it is clearly mentioned in the documentation that developers should write extensions when importing?

```json
"exports": {
  "./examples/jsm/*.js": "./examples/jsm/*.js",
  "./examples/jsm/*.png": "./examples/jsm/*.png"
}
```

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

**Additional context**
