title: Roadmap for addons
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

We should make using addons less confusing for the users.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I suggest the following roadmap:
* [x] Make alias `three/addons/`, pointing to `three/examples/jsm/` (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23406),
* [x] Change the documentation to use word "addons" instead of "examples" and tell only about the alias (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24579),
* [x] Update manual (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24888),
* [ ] Possibly update documentation paths,
* [ ] Move the fonts from `examples/fonts`, on which addons are dependant (if there are such? Not sure whether it is still the case) to `examples/jsm`.

~~After import maps would be supported by Safari and Firefox~~ In the end of the year:
* [x] Finally drop support for `examples/js` (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25043),
* [ ] Move `examples/jsm/` folder to the root of the repo and rename it to `addons`, making it less confusing for users reading the source code of the addons.
author: LeviPesin

title: VideoTexture version problem with multiple WebGLRenderer's
body: **Describe the bug** On Chrome & Safari, with support for `HTMLVideoElement.requestVideoFrameCallback()` the texture version is updated with video progression. However when multiple `WebGLRenderer` instances are used, each fails to indepently track the version causing only the first instance to update. With three `WebGLRenderer` this causes only the first to update with video, however the first frame is rendered. Firefox presumably not with support for the callback renders correctly. Old versions of THREE.js render correctly as the callback was not supported. This may or may not be made worse with use of the `EffectComposer`. If I explicitly set `needsUpdate = true` when the texture version changes on the first render call, for each subsequent render, then the desired result is achieved. This issue was encountered after upgrading from r122 to r144. ***Code*** ```js render() { this.#composers.forEach(composer => { composer.render(); }); requestAnimationFrame(() => this.render()); } ``` Force texture update alternative: ```js render() { this.#composers.forEach(composer => { this.#mesh.material.uniforms.map1.value.needsUpdate = true; tcomposer.render(); }); requestAnimationFrame(() => this.render()); } ``` ***Live example*** The example starts with an image for a few seconds then transitions to a video which exhibits the problem as described. The transitions and images all render as expected under the same set of renderer instances. * https://miffy.dsbunny.com/M1/ (r144 - exhibiting new behaviour) * https://ahyoomee.miru.hk/canvas/video.html (r122 - functioning as desired) Please note clicking the mouse on the page will cause the 3 renderers to animate apart, highlighting the different renderer locations. On a small screen this may cause the second and third renderer to appear missing. simply resize the screen or scroll to the right. **Expected behavior** All renderers should update with the full video frame. **Screenshots** r144 with top renderer updating, but lower two frozen on first video frame: r122 with all renderers updating as desired: **Platform:** - Device: Desktop - OS: MacOS, Windows - Browser: Chrome, Safari, Edge - Three.js version: r144 Please comment if a simpler example, or archive of source is desired.

title: AnimationAction wrapping on time === duration
body:
**Describe the bug**

When using AnimationAction and setting the time = duration it loops around. Just wondering if this should be changed to test > than rather than >= than. 

**To Reproduce**
See code below.

***Code***

```js
animationMixer.setTime(clip.getDuration());
```

**Expected behavior**

It should not loop over until once it is passed duration.

**Additional Information**

In my use case each keyframe represents an exact frame and a different scripting engine puts the frames together in different orders according to the user script and calls `animationMixer.setTime`. 

For example, the artist has a keyframe at time 0, time X, Y and finally time Z which happens to equal clip.duration. I should be able to set the animation to any of these times without it looping.

My current work around:
```js
if (time === this.action.getClip().duration) {
      time = time - 0.000001;
}
```

title: HDRI not working with ShaderMaterial
body:
Hi Everyone,

I am having issues using an environment map with ShaderMaterial.

I am loading the envMap using `THREE.CubeTextureLoader`, which works perfectly with `MeshStandardMaterial` but has no effect if I use instead `ShaderMaterial`, as you can see in this fiddle example: [https://jsfiddle.net/3jo6Ldpf/2/](https://jsfiddle.net/3jo6Ldpf/2/) . 

The cube on the right has a ShaderMaterial applied and the HDRI is not showing up.
In the same example, I tried using the option: (line 51)

```
materialShader.defines.ENVMAP_TYPE_CUBE_UV = ''
```
and  I get the following error:

```
ERROR: 0:484: 'CUBEUV_MAX_MIP' : undeclared identifier
ERROR: 0:485: 'CUBEUV_TEXEL_WIDTH' : undeclared identifier
ERROR: 0:486: 'CUBEUV_TEXEL_HEIGHT' : undeclared identifier
ERROR: 0:523: 'CUBEUV_MAX_MIP' : undeclared identifier
```

There is a function in [WebGLProgram.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/59247fa177b9b3637bd64835c8b0ea4218a63010/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLProgram.js#L374) called `generateCubeUVSize` that is suppose to compute the values of CUBEUV_MAX_MIP, CUBEUV_TEXEL_WIDTH, and CUBEUV_TEXEL_HEIGHT, and then automatically generating the corresponding definitions(defines), so I was puzzled why it was not working in this case.

When I tried manually defining those options by adding in the same fiddle example these lines of code:
```
  materialShader.defines.CUBEUV_MAX_MIP = '4'
  materialShader.defines.CUBEUV_TEXEL_WIDTH = '0.03'
  materialShader.defines.CUBEUV_TEXEL_HEIGHT = '0.03'
```
I got this error:
```
'textureCubeUV' : no matching overloaded function found
```
regarding the shader chunk `envmap_physical_pars_fragment.glsl.js`.

This gave me the hint that `envMap` passed in the function `textureCubeUV` is not the sampler2D uniform expected by the function but something else. The type of envmap seems to derive from the shaderChunk `envmap_common_pars_fragment.glsl.js`, which define envmap as `uniform samplerCube envMap;` rather than `uniform sampler2D envMap;` if `ENVMAP_TYPE_CUBE` has been defined.

So I wanted to understand who defines `ENVMAP_TYPE_CUBE`, since in the fiddle code I defined `ENVMAP_TYPE_CUBE_UV`, and in [WebGLPrograms.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/59247fa177b9b3637bd64835c8b0ea4218a63010/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLPrograms.js) 
I found this piece of code:
```
const environment = material.isMeshStandardMaterial ? scene.environment : null;
const envMap = ( material.isMeshStandardMaterial ? cubeuvmaps : cubemaps ).get( material.envMap || environment );
const envMapCubeUVHeight = ( !! envMap ) && ( envMap.mapping === CubeUVReflectionMapping ) ? envMap.image.height : null;
```
where it seems that if you do not have a `MeshStandardMaterial` you cannot have cubeuvmaps.

This code  could also explain why I was getting the error about CUBEUV_MAX_MIP etc not being defined, because those defines are happening only if `envMapCubeUVHeight` is not null.

Is there a way to have an HDRI working with `ShaderMaterial`?

 I am not a threejs expert so I might be not considering something trivial.
 I just tried to debug as much as I could until I got stuck to avoid bothering you folks <3 

----------------------------------

 - Device: [PC]
 - OS: [Windows, MacOS]
 - Browser: [Chrome]
 - Three.js version: [r143] (but I am getting the same error even in previous versions, up to 132)

title: TransformControls: Event not passed through to nested functions (version update 122 => 144)
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

After updating the codebase of a medium-sized project from version 122 to 144 (yes, we were stuck for a long time), we have a problem with the TransformControlls class exported from your "examples". When using "addEventListener" for the change event, event targets that are passed through nested functions (for example when using a debounce function for the listener) are `null`. This is not an issue when directly passing the callback function into the `addEventListener` function. In version 122 it worked, and I could not find anything related to this in the changelog. Did I miss something?

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Add a TransformControll to any mesh
2. Add a `change` event listener
3. Try to pass in a function that wraps another function and then log `event.target` in that wrapped function

***Code***

```js
// Init transform controls
const transformControls = new TransformControls(camera, renderer.domElement);
  tansformControls.mode = 'translate';

transformControls.addEventListener('dragging-changed', event => {
  controls.enabled = !event.value;
});

renderer.domElement.addEventListener('click', e => {
  e.stopPropagation();
});
    
    
// THE IMPORTANT PART:
// works, target is an object
transformControls.addEventListener("change", e => {
  console.log(e.target)
})
    
// does not work! Target is undefined.
ransformControls.addEventListener("change", debounce(console.log, 100))
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/deq2m0r6/5/)

**Expected behavior**

The event target should presist when passed into nested functions
**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows 11
 - Browser: Firefox and Chrome (both latest stable and dev release)
 - Three.js version: 144 (latest)

title: isWebGL2 test failed unexpectedly
body:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/3a42fffb0635674a229249e6f8d1a7ffa50b7edf/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLCapabilities.js#L55
When the `WebGLRenderer` been created in a `iframe/window`, and its `domElement` appended to a parent DOM in another `iframe/window`, then `isWebGL2` test will failed, because the actual constructor is `window. WebGL2RenderingContext` where window refers to the original `window/iframe`, so `instanceof` always returning `false`

author: WestLangley

title: The `dispose()` method needs clarification
body:
In the docs, the `dispose()` method has various definitions:


- When a BufferGeometry is rendered, it allocates WebGL resources which can only be disposed of by calling this method.
- Disposes of the PMREMGenerator's internal memory.
- Disposes of the internally-created material and geometry used by this helper.
- Dispose of the directionalLightHelper.
- Dispose of the hemisphereLightHelper.
- Dispose of the pointLightHelper.
- Disposes of the light helper.
- Override of base class's dispose
- Abstract dispose method for lights; implemented by subclasses that have disposable resources.
- Disposes of this shadow's textures map and mapPass.
- This disposes the material. Textures of a material don't get disposed.
- Frees the internal resources of this instance.
- Can be used if an instance of [name] becomes obsolete in an application. The method will free internal resources.
- Dispose of the current rendering context.
- Dispatches a dispose event.
- Frees the GPU related resources allocated by a texture. Call this method whenever a texture is no longer used in your app.

---

Perhaps something similar to the last one would be appropriate?

- Frees the GPU-related resources allocated by this instance.

Or, perhaps

- Frees the GPU-related resources allocated by this instance. Call this method whenever this instance is no longer used in your app.

Suggestions, welcome. :-)

title: Favor using WeakRef
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
I just want to suggest that GC languages like Js always have something known as WeakRef that it's the best for resource-critical libraries to use it and makes the memory management easier and more performant. Consider [this answer ](https://stackoverflow.com/a/48048620/14855128) that describes how much useful a WeakRef can be.
author: Methylol

title: Fix `Uncaught TypeError: (intermediate value).now is not a function` on clock.getDelta()
body:
It seems like there is something wrong with the clock now.

On my FPS game, my `clock.getDelta()` works just fine on my animated character.

But on another page, running `clock.getDelta` returns:

> Uncaught TypeError: (intermediate value).now is not a function

I copied the exact code from the other page, still does it.

I event did a shift+reload on my working page to make sure any changes from the three.js library were synced.

It still works on that page.

I clicked the link on the error which brought me to the debug tab in Firefox.

It seems what you're doing though is:
```javascript
function jc(){return("undefined"==typeof performance?Date:performance).now()}
```
And the `clock.getDelta` function is calling that `jc` function.

What I see is right here:
```javascript
("undefined"==typeof performance?Date:performance).now()
```
This is where the error is coming from.

I recommend you fix it with this:
```javascript
("undefined"==typeof performance.now?Date.now():performance.now())
```
I made it check for `performance.now` instead of just `performance` because it seems `performance.now` does not exist anymore.

Running this code in my console runs smoothly and free of errors.

Until you fix this bug I will use the custom code I made.

title: Blender / GLTF: Add Scene.environment intensity
body: Playing around with [Blender HDRI workflows](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z8IGDM2f2SQ) - and it looks seems there's no reasonable way of lowering the environment map intensity, to use it only as a supporting light (not that it's a vital feature - but a tiny missing link between Three and Blendor / [glTF IBLs ref](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Vendor/EXT_lights_image_based/README.md#image-based-light-properties).) Since `scene.environment` is a "global" setting - would it make sense to add [scene.environmentIntensity](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1460) to `uniforms` - overriding `.envMapIntensity` if it is not defined? A bit ugly, but it'd be following the way [envMap is currently inherited](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1376) from the Scene. (A cleaner way may probably be to split `scene.environment` as ` = { map: [IBLTexture], intensity: [Number] }` and add backwards-compatibility in the setter?) (A way cleaner way would be probably to add `.intensity` directly as a new property of a Texture to generalise it - since there's already quite a few intensity-based textures in PBR materials - but that'll probably not be super backwards-compatible?)
author: mjurczyk

title: ShaderNode: MaterialX Nodes
body:
Document with the purpose of comparing Three.js `ShaderNode` with `MaterialX`.

https://materialx.org/assets/MaterialX.v1.38.Spec.pdf

## Geometric

### Position
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  Position Model | `positionGeometry`  |
| ✅ |  Position Object | `positionLocal`  |
| ✅ |  Position World | `positionWorld`  |

### Normal
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  Normal Model | `normalGeometry`  |
| ✅ |  Normal Object | `normalLocal`  |
| ✅ |  Normal World | `normalWorld`  |

### Tangent
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  Tangent Model | `tangentGeometry`  |
| ✅ |  Tangent Object | `tangentLocal`  |
| ✅ |  Tangent World | `tangentWorld`  |

### Bitangent
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  Bitangent Model | `bitangentGeometry`  |
| ✅ |  Bitangent Object | `bitangentLocal`  |
| ✅ |  Bitangent World | `bitangentWorld`  |

### Texture coordinate
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  texcoord | `uv( index )`  |

### Geometry attribute
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  geomcolor | `attribute( 'color', 'vec3' )`  |
| ✅ |  geompropvalue | `attribute( value, type )`  |

## Application
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  frame | `frameId`  |
| ✅ |  time | `timeLocal()`  |
| ⬜️ |  updirection |   |

## Math
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  add | `add( in1, in2 )` |
| ✅ |  subtract | `sub( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  multiply | `mul( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  divide | `div( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  modulo | `mod( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  absval | `abs( in )`  |
| ✅ |  sign | `sign( in )`  |
| ✅ |  floor | `floor( in )`  |
| ✅ |  ceil | `ceil( in )`  |
| ✅ |  round | `round( in )`  |
| ✅ |  power | `pow( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  sin | `sin( in )`  |
| ✅ |  cos | `cos( in )`  |
| ✅ |  tan | `tan( in )`  |
| ✅ |  asin | `asin( in )`  |
| ✅ |  acos | `acos( in )`  |
| ✅ |  atan2 | `atan2( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  sqrt | `sqrt( in )`  |
| ⬜️ |  ln |  |
| ✅ |  exp | `exp( in )`  |
| ✅ |  clamp | `clamp( in, low = 0, high = 1 )`  |
| ✅ |  min | `min( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  max | `max( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  normalize | `normalize( in )`  |
| ✅ |  magnitude | `length( in )`  |
| ✅ |  dotproduct | `dot( in1, in2 )`  |
| ✅ |  crossproduct | `cross( in )`  |
| ⬜️ |  transformpoint |  |
| ⬜️ |  transformvector |  |
| ⬜️ |  transformnormal |  |
| ⬜️ |  transformmatrix | `transformDirection( in, mat )`  |
| ✅ |  normalmap | `normalMap( in, ... )`  |
| ⬜️ |  transpose |  |
| ⬜️ |  determinant |  |
| ⬜️ |  invertmatrix |  |
| ✅ |  rotate2d | `rotateUV( in, degToRad( amount ) )`  |
| ⬜️ |  rotate3d |  |
| ⬜️ |  arrayappend |  |
| ⬜️ |  dot |  |

## Adjustment
| Supported | MaterialX | ShaderNode |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| ✅ |  remap | `remap( in, inlow, inHigh, outLow, outHigh )` |
| ✅ |  smoothstep | `smoothstep( in, low, high )`  |
| ⬜️ |  curveadjust |   |

title: Posibility to change zoom style for OrbitControls
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

There are some cases where it is useful to have a `PerspectiveCamera` that when you zoom in/out it will change the FOV of the camera, not the position (dolly) as it is now.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add a property called `.zoomStyle` on the `OrbitControls` instance, which will default on `'dolly'` and can be changed to `'zoom'`. This property will have effect only on a `PerspectiveCamera`

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Use an `OrthographicCamera`, but that doesn't work for us, as we have to match perspective from a generated object.

**Additional context**

I could make a PR for this, if this is something that would be desired

title: Option to disable two-pass rendering of transparent objects
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

There's a [hardcoded path](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/d081c5a3501d272d19375fab1b01fedf9df29b22/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1334) for rendering transparent objects with doublesided materials. However it's not particularly suitable for our use-case since our models are optimized for a single blended pass with backface fulling disabled. That leads to artifacts.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

An option for materials to override this behaviour.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Right now we're forced to patch by hand and commit into our repo a copy of threejs build.

author: AxiomeCG

title: Documentation: Add a warning when raycasting on the same frame than a change in position/scale/rotation.
body:
Hello,

I recently had a problem with the raycasting that I was not aware of. 
A lot of method rely on the prior that the world matrix is updated. In the default case, it is done on each frame by the renderer.

But when you are translating for example a Mesh on frame 1, and raycasting on the same frame, you will miss the target even if the ray should have intersect the Mesh. 

I added to the doc a note on the Raycaster setFromCamera and intersectObjects methods, and also in the Mesh and LOD raycast method, to make it clear for the user to be sure that when he uses one of these methods, he must be absolutely certain that the world matrix is up-to-date. Otherwise, the behaviour would be unpredictable. I will link this to my pull request.

In order to make 3D concepts even more accessible, I think it is a good warning to newcomers. It would have save me some time if I would have known that prerequisite from the start.

body:

Matrix3 has invalid `translate` implementation.

author: eXponenta

title: FBXLoader sometimes floods console with invalid material index warnings
body:
The following line of code ... 

console.warn( 'THREE.FBXLoader: Invalid material index:', materialIndex );

... is generating 10's of thousands of lines of console output on (at least) models produced by exporting from Blender and Fusion360 to FBX. Chrome's debugger also seems to have trouble coping with the amount of spew, leading to a need for frequent forced restarts.

Note: I believe that this problem started occurring after we upgraded from three.js version 0.133.1 to a more recent version.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Sync and run [this project](https://github.com/philipswan/TetheredRing) according to the instructions in the ReadMe.
2. Launch in Chrome, using localhost url.
3. Open a debug console by using <cntrl>+C to observe warning messages.

***Code***
The following code in TransitSystems.js is the only place where the fbxloader is called. Commenting out these lines of code will cause the problem to go away.
```js
    const fbxloader = new FBXLoader()
    fbxloader.load('models/RingTerminus.fbx', addRingTerminuses, progressFunction, errorFunction )
    fbxloader.load('models/GroundTerminus.fbx', addGroundTerminuses, progressFunction, errorFunction )
    fbxloader.load('models/RingTerminus.fbx', addHabitats, progressFunction, errorFunction )  // This is hacky - borrowed RingTerminus model and modified it to make a habitat
    fbxloader.load('models/Elevator.fbx', addElevatorCars, progressFunction, errorFunction )
```
Note: RingTerminus.fbx was exported from Fusion360. GroundTerminus.fbx and Elevator.fbx were exported from Blender.

**Expected behavior**

Possibly there is no "real problem" with the models and thus no warning error messages actually need to be printed out. Alternatively, there was always a real problem with these models (note: they seemed to work fine before), in which case perhaps we likely need to file bugs with both the Blender Team and Autodesk teams. Possibly there is yet another explanation for the observed behavior. Documenting instruction of how to avoid and/or resolve this issue (added to this bug report or official docs) may help others.

**Screenshots**

If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem (drag and drop the image).

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop and Mobile
 - OS: Windows 10 (others unknown)
 - Browser: Chrome and Edge (other unknown)
 - Three.js version: 0.144.0 (but not on 0.133.1)
author: philipswan

title: Raycasting: Ensure that world matrices are up-to-date
body:
**Describe the bug**

Relates to #24727
Analog to #21427

There is two parts for the bug:
- We are using the world matrix of the camera in the [Raycaster.setFromCamera ](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=rayca#api/en/core/Raycaster.setFromCamera)
- We are using world matrices for LOD, Mesh [raycast](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=Object3D#api/en/core/Object3D.raycast) method inherited from Object3D.

We have no warranty that the world matrices are up-to-date when executing these methods. We need to check if [matrixWorldNeedsUpdate](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=Object3D#api/en/core/Object3D.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate) is set to true before attempting raycasting.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Change the position/rotation/scale of a mesh 
2. Attempt to raycast on the same frame against the mesh, without updating the world matrix, in a place that you would expect to have hits.
3. intersectObject will return an empty array of intersections, even if it is obvious that we hit the mesh.

Or the opposite case
1. Change the position/rotation/scale of a mesh
2. Attempt to raycast on the same frame against the mesh, without updating the world matrix, with a ray that is traversing the old position of the mesh
3. You should expect the raycaster to tell that there is no intersection
4. There is one intersection.


For the setFromCamera, you can do the same by moving the camera and using the setFromCamera. It is more difficult to visualize though, but in the codebase there is no test if the camera's world matrix is up-to-date from what I saw.

***Code***

```ts
const geometry = new THREE.BoxBufferGeometry(1, 1, 1);
const material = new THREE.MeshNormalMaterial();
const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);

mesh.position.setY(2);

const rayOrigin = new Vector3(-3, 0.5, 0);
const rayDirection = new Vector3(1, 0, 0);
const raycaster = new Raycaster(rayOrigin, rayDirection);

const arrowHelper = new ArrowHelper(rayDirection, rayOrigin, 5);

console.log(raycaster.intersectObject(mesh));

const scene = new THREE.Scene();
scene.add(mesh);
scene.add(arrowHelper);
```

***Live example***
- [Codesandbox link to reproduce](https://codesandbox.io/s/three-js-template-forked-5owt8k?file=/index.js)
I'm proving the behaviour via a console.log. The raycasting is giving a non existing intersection after the mesh has moved.

**Expected behavior**

The raycasting should check if the objects that are required to raycast are up-to-date.

**Screenshots**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18304870/193545947-61d27880-f9f5-4ad8-b63e-029a50cccb29.png)

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop, Mobile]
 - OS: [Windows, MacOS, Linux, Android, iOS]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge]
 - Three.js version: [dev, r145]

author: kylebakerio

title: LOD documentation incorrect for raycast return type
body:
**Describe the bug**

The [LOD documentation](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=LOD#api/en/objects/LOD) reports that [`raycast`](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=LOD#api/en/objects/LOD.raycast) returns an array. However, there is no `return` statement in the [source](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/d081c5a3501d272d19375fab1b01fedf9df29b22/src/objects/LOD.js#L109).

IMO, either the documentation should be updated to reflect the source (ie returning `undefined`) or, alternatively, if the documentation is correct, then the source should be updated to reflect the documentation. Unfortunately, I'm too distant from the project to know which is the better option. Thanks in advance to the three team for checking this out!

author: dev-newvisibility

title: VideoTexture hold reference to video source
body:
**Describe the bug**

Follow source:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/textures/VideoTexture.js#L19

VideoTexture hold reference to video in closure, that is get bugs when you change a source like:
```
const texture = new VideoTexture(video);

texture.image = otherVideo;

```

In that case new video never be update from `requestVideoFrameCallback` and will wait update from older video instance, that never be removed by GC - memory leak.

Possible solution: forgeting about closure usages and check that listener runned in `update` method, this will garaneed that current watched source and texture source is same.

whey not use a `set image()` ? Becuase texture has `source` propery, that can be changed indirect and we again will lost reference.


Whay not use a `new VideoTexture`? We will loose a reference, because it can be cross shared between different materials.
author: eXponenta

title: Custom build failing as of r137
body:
For the last two years I have been producing a custom `three.min.js` bundled with `OrbitControls` by adding one line to `Three.js`:

`export { OrbitControls } from '../examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js';`

As of r137 this no longer works because the build process cannot automatically locate the module `three`. One gets errors like

```
(!) Unresolved dependencies
https://rollupjs.org/guide/en/#warning-treating-module-as-external-dependency
three (imported by examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js)
(!) Missing global variable name
Use output.globals to specify browser global variable names corresponding to external modules
three (guessing 'three')
```

While I'm sure there's an easy workaround (and I would appreciate if someone could point me in the right direction), this seems to me like an error in the build process. Since one gets an output build file, which unfortunately doesn't function, this is basically a silent failure.

I can see how to resolve this in the context of example files, but shouldn't there be a way to have this happen automatically when using features from example modules?

author: NSGUF

title: Editor Local Mode: File-open/save and Folder-browser-tab
body:
Editor Local Mode: File-open/save and Folder-browser-tab

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The current editor is difficult to use for local project editing, lacking a **folder browser**, **file open/save** capabilities, and isn't oriented towards 'path-based' scenes that link to other content/sub-scenes via file paths. This is in contrast to other major editors (such as Unity/Unreal/Godot) which are wholly project oriented and provide excellent tools for browsing/editing large collections of models and resources that are then combined/built into shippable packages. For example the editor is not even particularly good at exploring/editing the example collection of content packaged within the three.js git itself.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Demo of solution: https://leweyg.github.io/lewcid_editor/   (contains folder view of three.js examples, opened by URL).

Couple main features:
- **Path from URL** - This fundamental feature allows us to open specific assets by path, and also lays the foundation for the editor to have a "current path" that both saves to, and loads resources relative to. 
- **Import from URL as child/sibling/callback** - Currently each importer spawns their assets in different ways, mostly as children of the root note placed at the origin, this makes it very hard to create "composed" scenes of assets (where one scene imports other sub-scenes, ideally with shared resources when instancing), and specifically when adding elements to a scene it is much more convenient to add any new group as a sibling with same transform as the selected object to allow rapid instancing.
- **'Folder' tab** to quick browse/search/open/instance/manage large collections of files, including for the example three.js content itself. A default viewer-scene (with a light if not present) should provided for objects, materials, images, sounds, etc.
- **Local System App** for File Open/Save, ability to launch local file-explorer or code/image/asset editor, system level "open in three.js editor" support, basic git commands, etc. URL-based path selector to allow different content per-tab etc.
- **Path-based Scenes** for scenes whose content is referenced via file paths (to models, materials, sub-scenes, scripts, etc.) rather than within the file itself. Also builds into project-wide optimization tools like material unification, dependancy-based asset builds, and possibly support for importing or editing-in-place whole projects from Unity/Unreal/Godot.
- **Fully WASD Camera** for improved navigation through complex scenes at different scales. Having orbit camera is very limiting, although may be useful as an option.
- **Snapping** of translation/rotation/scale should be user customizable, there is infrastructure for this, just need a UI. Expandable context menus would probably be the easiest approach.
- **Basic Animation Timeline Editor** for setting up automatic or scripted animation timelines.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Two main alternatives:
1. Use a traditional editor that does support project/git oriented editing, and effectively export to three.js for runtime only with a custom build pipeline. Seems un-neccasary considering the small additions required.
2. Import and review individual assets in the three.js editor in a somewhat clunky fashion and just avoid trying to review features in the editor that require cross file collaboration or iteration (using a mixture of vscode and different tools to work around these). 

**Additional context**

As a long time professional 3D app/game/open-source developer, while I love the three.js runtime, the Editor is really not in a place where I feel it is ready for large-scale project editing or passing to traditional art teams to work with it in an effective manner that is team/git oriented. Yet it wouldn't take too many additions to get it into that place.

To prototype (or develop this concept for my own purposes), I've made a branch and am working on these features here: https://github.com/leweyg/lewcid_editor 

Thanks for your consideration,

-Lewey Geselowitz
http://lewcid.com/lg/aboutme.html

title: GLTFLoader: Incorrect bind pose for newly loaded armatures
body: **Describe the bug** `Skeleton.pose()` relies on the initial world matrix of each bone to reset bind pose. However, the world matrices of bones loaded in a glTF asset are not updated when the model first loads, causing `.pose()` to squash the bones into the origin rather than the position they initially appear in. If I render the scene (or manually call `.updateMatrixWorld()` on the asset) prior to creating the the skeleton, then it sets the correct bind pose. This issue of non-updated bone matrices also affects the result of calling `.clone()` on newly loaded glTF models. **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behavior: 1. Load a glTF model with an armature 2. Create a skeleton from its bones 3. Call `.pose()` on the skeleton 4. Render the scene OR 1. Load a glTF model with an armature 2. Clone the glTF scene root and add it to the scene 3. Render the scene ***Code*** ```js const loader = new GLTFLoader() loader.load("/character.glb", (gltf) => { scene.add(gltf.scene) const bones = [] gltf.scene.traverse((o) => (o instanceof THREE.Bone) && bones.push(o)) const skeleton = new THREE.Skeleton(bones) skeleton.pose() }) ``` OR ```js const loader = new GLTFLoader() loader.load("/character.glb", (gltf) => { const cloned = gltf.scene.clone() scene.add(cloned) }) ``` ***Live example*** * [example with `.pose()`](https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/wa5bz7r8/7/) * [example with `.clone()`](https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/n84t3e5j/1/) **Expected behavior** The skeleton should reset to the initial pose as stored in the glTF asset. While the workaround (manually calling `.updateMatrixWorld()` prior to these operations) is simple, I don't think users should have to know the internals of how `Skeleton.pose()` works to get the expected output. I suppose the fix is to update the world matrices within `GLTFLoader`. IIUC these matrices are already available in the loader [here](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L4256), they just aren't filled into the bone `.matrixWorld` properties. **Screenshots** Incorrect bind pose: Correct bind pose: **Platform:** - Device: [Desktop] - OS: [MacOS] - Browser: [Chrome] - Three.js version: [r145]
author: mattrossman

title: Getting `navigator` undefined when using headless-gl
body: Hi! 👋 Firstly, thanks for your work on this project! 🙂 Today I used [patch-package](https://github.com/ds300/patch-package) to patch `three@0.145.0` for the project I'm working on. For context: I'm running `three` on a nodejs server using `headless-gl` as my renderer context. In doing so, I was getting an error that said `navigator` was undefined. I patched it locally as follows to get it to work, but just wanted to raise this issue in case its helpful to others / project maintainers. Here is the diff that solved my problem: ```diff diff --git a/node_modules/three/build/three.cjs b/node_modules/three/build/three.cjs index ccf5e09..a3009c7 100644 --- a/node_modules/three/build/three.cjs +++ b/node_modules/three/build/three.cjs @@ -16515,7 +16515,7 @@ function WebGLTextures(_gl, extensions, state, properties, capabilities, utils, const maxTextureSize = capabilities.maxTextureSize; const maxSamples = capabilities.maxSamples; const multisampledRTTExt = extensions.has('WEBGL_multisampled_render_to_texture') ? extensions.get('WEBGL_multisampled_render_to_texture') : null; - const supportsInvalidateFramebuffer = /OculusBrowser/g.test(navigator.userAgent); + const supportsInvalidateFramebuffer = true const _videoTextures = new WeakMap(); ``` This issue body was [partially generated by patch-package](https://github.com/ds300/patch-package/issues/296).

title: SVGLoader: Exception in `isHoleTo` when loading an SVG file
body:
**Describe the bug**

When using `SVGLoader` to load a particular SVG file ([file attached](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1017304/195216421-5677c5d5-85fd-44a5-9721-3abc176fec05.svg)), an exception is thrown in the internal `isHoleTo` function, at [this line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/jsm/loaders/SVGLoader.js#L2019):

`TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'isCW')`

Full stack trace:
```
SVGLoader.js:2019 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'isCW')
    at isHoleTo (SVGLoader.js:2019:57)
    at SVGLoader.js:2110:41
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SVGLoader.createShapes (SVGLoader.js:2110:31)
    at main.js?t=1665531910670:16:26
```

**To Reproduce**

See code and JS Fiddles below.

The SVG file is [available here](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1017304/195216421-5677c5d5-85fd-44a5-9721-3abc176fec05.svg). It's also embedded in the example below.

(the specific path that's creating the exception was extracted from a larger SVG -- this is why the .svg file in itself doesn't look that "great"..) :-)

***Code***

```js
import { SVGLoader } from "three/addons/loaders/SVGLoader.js";

const loader = new SVGLoader();

let loadedSvg = loader.parse(`
  <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
    <g transform="matrix(5.0000,0.0000,0.0000,5.0000,100,100)">
        <path d="M 5.6 -9.9 L 5.4 -11.9 M 2 -7.3 L 1.7 -6.7 M 2 -7.3 L 2 -7.3 M 2 -7.2 L 2 -7.3 M 2 -7.2 L 2 -7.2 M 2 -7.5 L 2 -7.5 L 1.9 -9.9 L 2.2 -8 L 2.9 -9.6 M 2.4 -8.4 L 2.3 -7.7 L 2.0 -7.2 M 4.4 -6.3 L 4.4 -7.7 L 3.6 -5.4 L 2.7 -2.6 L 3.2 -3.4 L 3.8 -4.7" style="" transform=""/>
    </g>
  </svg>
`);

const path = loadedSvg.paths[0];

// the following line will raise an exception
const shapes = SVGLoader.createShapes(path);

```

***Live example***

Both fiddles below (latest & dev) show the issue:
* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/gregsadetsky/su710ykn/20/)
* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/gregsadetsky/pc2os8ut/1/)

**Expected behavior**

The SVG file should be loaded without any errors.

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: macOS
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: dev, 0.145.0

title: Points flicker on iOS if bokeh post processing is applied to points material
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Points Material if used with bokeh post processing, particles start flickering on each render call on Safari iOS 16. Create scene with at least 10 particles of points material and render them close to camera, then apply bokeh post processing. With rendering on each frame, particles will flicker on Safari iOS 16.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. View demo in Safari on iOS 16 or older version

***Code***

```js
// code goes here

var count = 10;
var w = window.innerWidth;
var h = window.innerHeight;
var postProcessing;

function createPoints(){
	camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(70, w/h, 1, 100);
	scene = new THREE.Scene();

	camera.position.z = 6;

	var geometry = new THREE.BufferGeometry();
	material = new THREE.PointsMaterial();

	var mesh = new THREE.Points(geometry, material);

	var points = new Float32Array(count * 3);
        var multiplier = 0.6;
	for(var x = 0; x < count; x++) points[x * 3] = (x - 5) * multiplier;
	geometry.setAttribute('position', new THREE.Float32BufferAttribute(points, 3));
	
	scene.add(mesh);

	renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({antialias: false});

	postProcessing = {};
	postProcessing.renderPass = new THREE.RenderPass(scene, camera);
	postProcessing.effectPass = new THREE.BokehPass(scene, camera,{maxblur: 0.2});
	postProcessing.composer = new THREE.EffectComposer(renderer);
	postProcessing.composer.addPass(postProcessing.renderPass);
	postProcessing.composer.addPass(postProcessing.effectPass);
  
	renderer.setSize(w, h);
	document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);
}

function render(){
	requestAnimationFrame(render.bind(this));

	postProcessing.composer.render();
}

createPoints();
render();

```

***Live example***

* [codepen-release](https://codepen.io/Sahil89/full/yLjQKMO)

**Expected behavior**

Particles should not flicker as they only flicker in Safari on iOS.

**Screenshots**

If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem (drag and drop the image).

**Platform:**

 - Device: Mobile
 - OS: [iOS]
 - Browser: [Safari]
 - Three.js version: [r145]

title: Are `GLTF`/`KHR_punctual_lights` `intensity`s and `.physicallyCorrectLights` handled correctly?
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

- [Docs](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=s#api/en/renderers/WebGLRenderer.physicallyCorrectLights) don't give clear light units, and [examples](https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_lights_physical.html) imply total lumens.
- [`KHR_punctual_lights`](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_lights_punctual/README.md#light-types) however specifies lumens divided by square radians for GLTF.
- Meanwhile `GLTFLoader` [allegedly](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Ce9XCC2Ub9eVjAQdYbYdLvlkDBJ_0GtAyRbSF0KJWzE/) and apparently just copies directly from GLTF values (lm/sr if spec-compliant) to Three.JS light nodes (plain lm, apparently).

Since these three things seem to be in contradiction, presumably one of them must be wrong.

`lights/physical` Example:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/034343d97134c55559aa4edbcce3d405eae308da/examples/webgl_lights_physical.html#L47-L56

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/034343d97134c55559aa4edbcce3d405eae308da/examples/webgl_lights_physical.html#L294

`GLTFLoader`:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/290f32bcf186e38e8f1de35ebe0f75491d3eda46/examples/js/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L467

title: Positional Audio Crackling Distortion on iOS and Safari on Mac
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

I added a couple positional audio sources to a scene where I walk around a space and the audiolistener's position is constantly updated, and it works fine on chrome on mac but has issues on my iphone as well as safari on mac. The audio begins crackling after some time and gets progressively worse until it is unbearable. 

Pretty sure it is related to this: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=227199

**To Reproduce**

In the linked post there are some demos of the issue. I think it happens more quickly when there is a drop in framerate.

I worked around the issue by using THREE.Audio objects and manually lowering the volume based on distance. Seems like a custom implementation of PositionalAudio that doesn't use PannerNode shouldn't be too hard to do & would fix the issue.

title: MeshPhysicalMaterial attenuationDistance is null when exporting to JSON
body: **Describe the bug** ```js const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.BoxGeometry(), new THREE.MeshPhysicalMaterial()) console.log(mesh.material.attenuationDistance); // Infinity (correct) const json = JSON.stringify(mesh.toJSON()) const x = new THREE.ObjectLoader().parse(JSON.parse(json)) console.log(x.material.attenuationDistance); // null, but it should be Infinity ``` ***Live example*** - https://jsfiddle.net/we3c562t/5/ (open the console) **Expected behavior** Serializing and deserializing should preserve the material appearance. Probably we should avoid exporting `attenuationDistance` when it's equal to Infinity (the default value). Related issue: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24622 **Screenshots** I noticed this issue by exporting and re-importing a MeshPhysicalMaterial from the editor. title: GLTF Orthographic camera size seems to be handled incorrectly.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Per spec, `camera.orthographic.xmag` and `.ymag` should be half the view window size, I think:

https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/specification/2.0/Specification.adoc#31034-orthographic-projection

```ADoc
==== Orthographic projection

Let

- `r` be half the orthographic width, set by `camera.orthographic.xmag`;
- `t` be half the orthographic height, set by `camera.orthographic.ymag`;
- `f` be the distance to the far clipping plane, set by `camera.orthographic.zfar`;
- `n` be the distance to the near clipping plane, set by `camera.orthographic.znear`.
```

The loader code *looks* right:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/46ccd684a05161dea72119b497057e417eb1ebb6/examples/js/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L3360-L3364

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/3d1c5b8aaad7f64f81322f778a6561d04efa5b77/docs/api/en/cameras/OrthographicCamera.html#L47-L55

But the results don't:

**Screenshots**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37680486/195904636-308a96fc-2cf7-4110-87e7-ab04bdfa8fd3.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37680486/195904652-b7bb9f9a-e689-45fc-b23d-91d8b9fb869a.png)

Each square is one unit.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
- Save and open the GLTF file below.

Note: The Khronos Group reference implementation looks like Three.JS, ~~while Blender looks like Babylon.js~~ (Okay. That's actually not implemented for Blender, and the default just happened to be what I was expecting by hilarious coincidence.). So.... author: will-ca

title: KTX2Loader detaches cached URL response.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Loading the same URL twice with `KTX2Loader` results in an error because the cached URL response (from the shared `Cache` referred to by `FileLoader`) is sent to a web worker and detached.

***Code***

```js
const url = '/sample.ktx';
const loader = new KTX2Loader();
const handleError = (error) => console.log(error);
const t1 = loader.load(url, undefined, undefined, handleError);
const t2 = loader.load(url, undefined, undefined, handleError);
```

The error will be printed out for the second load.

**Expected behavior**

You should get two distinct textures. You can work around it with a cache breaking parameter, but ideally it wouldn't take two downloads.

**Platform:**

I tested this in Chrome on MacOS on r131 but my reading of the code suggests it's still a problem.

Note this is essentially another instance of the problem addressed in #13710.
author: dcervelli

title: WebGLRenderer / WebGLRenderList custom sort method
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

There are many sorting use-cases that aren't possible to implement with only a custom Javascript sort comparison function (via `WebGLRenderer.setTransparentSort` and `WebGLRenderer.setOpaqueSort`). I would suggest an API change to allow the entire `WebGLRenderList.sort` method to be overridden with a callback that accepts parameters for all object lists and the sorting methods (either the default methods, or the custom sorting methods specified in WebGLRenderer).

A quick example use-case is logical grouping. Perhaps objects should be grouped by some condition and then sorted independently as a group by a certain sorting algorithm. Then, all groups should be sorted against each other by some other algorithm, like z-distance to the camera.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I have a PR here with the suggested API changes to WebGLRenderer and WebGLRenderList: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24809

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I have considered using renderOrder, Groups, and WebGLRenderer.setTransparentSort to accomplish my goals, but my specific use case is too complex. A further complication of mine is that I don't want to hijack the renderOrder field of any objects. My framework will be used by other developers, and they should be able to set their own renderOrder value, and my custom sorting algorithm should respect it.

**Additional context**

No additional context.

title: [WebXR] WebXRController doesn't initiate joints when controller connected
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

I brought it up [on the forum](https://discourse.threejs.org/t/create-joints-in-webxr-controllers-hand-space-when-hand-controller-connected/43803) but then I realized it might be too specific to discuss on the forum so adding it as an issue here. 

**Describe the bug**

[WebXRController ](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webxr/WebXRController.js) doesn't populate `hand.joints` when the controller i.e. hand is connected / starts getting tracked. This leads to `hand.joints` being `{}` when accessed inside controller "connected" event. `hand.joints` are populated [only after the first WebXRController `update` call](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webxr/WebXRController.js#L140).

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Add an Event Listener callback for controller `connected` event.
2. `hand.joints` is `{}` inside the callback.

***Code***

```js
const handGroup = renderer.xr.getHand( 0 );
handGroup.addEventListener( 'connected', ( event ) => {
    console.log(handGroup.joints); // returns {}, expected {"joint1":Group, "joint2":Group...}
} );
```

***Live example***

* [glitch-latest-release-r145](https://glitch.com/edit/#!/oil-delicate-relish?path=index.js%3A45%3A25)
Also see comment notes inside the Glitch example above

**Expected behavior**

Current implementation of WebXRController returns {} for `hand.joints` when accessed after controller connected, expected `{"joint1":Group, "joint2":Group...}` . An alternative for getting the joints would be to add it some `update` method, but it requires polling instead of being event-driven, can be considered worse from a performance perspective [(see `render` in the Glitch example)](https://glitch.com/edit/#!/oil-delicate-relish?path=index.js%3A68%3A5)

**Screenshots**

![three js](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19701790/197082244-84405fe7-0c95-4970-8cbc-8e11ff9086b1.png)

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop, VR]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Oculus Browser]
 - Three.js version: [ r145]

 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r145

title: OutlinePass makes 3D Object dark after rendering
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

```js
composer = new EffectComposer( renderer );
renderPass = new RenderPass( scene, camera );
composer.addPass( renderPass );

outlinePass = new OutlinePass( new THREE.Vector2( window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight ), scene, camera );
outlinePass.visibleEdgeColor.set( '#0080FF' );
composer.addPass( outlinePass );

effectFXAA = new ShaderPass( FXAAShader );
effectFXAA.uniforms.resolution.value.set( 1 / window.innerWidth, 1 / window.innerHeight );
composer.addPass( effectFXAA );
```
Here is a part of code to use OutlinePass in a project

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. git clone https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js.git
2. npm install
3. npm run dev
4. open link http://localhost:8080/examples/demo.html
5. click the 3D object on this page

***Code***

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">

<head>
  <title>three.js webgl - post processing - Outline Pass</title>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, user-scalable=no, minimum-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0">
  <link type="text/css" rel="stylesheet" href="main.css">
</head>

<body>
  <!-- Import maps polyfill -->
  <!-- Remove this when import maps will be widely supported -->
  <script async src="https://unpkg.com/es-module-shims@1.3.6/dist/es-module-shims.js"></script>

  <script type="importmap">
			{
				"imports": {
					"three": "../build/three.module.js",
					"three/addons/": "./jsm/"
				}
			}
		</script>

  <script type="module">
import * as THREE from 'three';

import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js';
import { EffectComposer } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/EffectComposer.js';
import { RenderPass } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/RenderPass.js';
import { ShaderPass } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/ShaderPass.js';
import { OutlinePass } from 'three/addons/postprocessing/OutlinePass.js';
import { FXAAShader } from 'three/addons/shaders/FXAAShader.js';
import { GLTFLoader } from 'three/addons/loaders/GLTFLoader.js';
import { DRACOLoader } from 'three/addons/loaders/DRACOLoader.js';

let camera, scene, renderer, controls;
let composer, effectFXAA, outlinePass, renderPass;

const raycaster = new THREE.Raycaster();
const mouse = new THREE.Vector2();

init();

function init() {

	InitRenderer();
	InitScene();
	InitCamera();
	InitLights();
	InitHelper();
	setControl();
	InitData();
	initSelection();
	render();

	renderer.domElement.addEventListener( 'click', onClick.bind( this ) );

}

function fitView( object ) {

	const box = new THREE.Box3();
	box.setFromObject( object );
	const cent = box.getCenter( new THREE.Vector3() );
	const size = box.getSize( new THREE.Vector3() );


	const maxAxis = Math.max( size.x, size.y, size.z );
	let scale = 1;
	if ( maxAxis > 100 ) {

		scale = 30 / maxAxis;
		object.scale.multiplyScalar( scale );

	} else if ( maxAxis < 1 ) {

		scale = 10 / maxAxis;
		object.scale.multiplyScalar( scale );

	}

	const centX = cent.x * scale;
	const centY = cent.y * scale;
	const centZ = cent.z * scale;

	object.translateX( - centX );
	object.translateY( - centY );
	object.translateZ( - centZ );

}

function InitData() {

	const onProgress = function ( xhr ) {

		if ( xhr.lengthComputable ) {

			const percentComplete = xhr.loaded / xhr.total * 100;
			console.log( Math.round( percentComplete, 2 ) + '% downloaded' );

		}

	};

	const onError = function () { };

	const dracoLoader = new DRACOLoader();
	dracoLoader.setDecoderPath( './js/libs/draco/gltf/' );
	const loader = new GLTFLoader();
	loader.setDRACOLoader( dracoLoader );
	loader.load( 'models/gltf/BoomBox.glb', ( { scene: object } ) => {

		fitView( object );
		scene.add( object );
		render();

	}, onProgress, onError );

}

function InitRenderer() {

	renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer( { antialias: true } );
	renderer.setSize( window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight );
	renderer.outputEncoding = THREE.sRGBEncoding;
	renderer.physicallyCorrectLights = false;
	renderer.shadowMap.enabled = true;
	renderer.shadowMap.type = 1;
	renderer.toneMapping = 0;
	renderer.toneMappingExposure = 1;
	// renderer.setClearColor(0xb9d3ff, 1)
	const pmremGenerator = new THREE.PMREMGenerator( renderer );
	pmremGenerator.compileEquirectangularShader();
	document.body.appendChild( renderer.domElement );

}

function InitCamera() {

	camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera( 70, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 1, 2000 );
	camera.position.set( 0, 20, 50 );
	camera.lookAt( scene.position );
	scene.add( camera );

}

function InitScene() {

	scene = new THREE.Scene();
	scene.background = new THREE.Color( 0x333944 );

}

function InitHelper() {

	const helper = new THREE.GridHelper( 500, 30 );
	helper.position.y = 0;
	helper.material.opacity = 0.25;
	helper.material.transparent = true;
	scene.add( helper );

}

function InitLights() {

	const ambientLight = new THREE.AmbientLight( 0xffffff, 1 );
	scene.add( ambientLight );

	const directionalLight = new THREE.DirectionalLight( 0xffffff, 1 );
	directionalLight.position.set( 5, 10, 7.5 );
	scene.add( directionalLight );

}

function setControl() {

	controls = new OrbitControls( camera, renderer.domElement ); //创建控件对象
	controls.addEventListener( 'change', render );

}

function render() {

	if ( composer ) {

		composer.render();

	} else {

		renderer.render( scene, camera );

	}

}

function onClick( event ) {

	// if (event.isPrimary === false) return
	mouse.x = ( event.clientX / window.innerWidth ) * 2 - 1;
	mouse.y = - ( event.clientY / window.innerHeight ) * 2 + 1;
	checkIntersection();
	render();

}

function checkIntersection() {

	raycaster.setFromCamera( mouse, camera );
	const intersects = raycaster.intersectObject( scene, true );
	if ( intersects.length > 0 ) {

		outlinePass.selectedObjects = [ intersects[ 0 ].object ];

	} else {

		outlinePass.selectedObjects = [];

	}

}

function initSelection() {

	composer = new EffectComposer( renderer );
	renderPass = new RenderPass( scene, camera );
	composer.addPass( renderPass );

	outlinePass = new OutlinePass( new THREE.Vector2( window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight ), scene, camera );
	outlinePass.visibleEdgeColor.set( '#0080FF' );
	composer.addPass( outlinePass );

	effectFXAA = new ShaderPass( FXAAShader );
	effectFXAA.uniforms.resolution.value.set( 1 / window.innerWidth, 1 / window.innerHeight );
	composer.addPass( effectFXAA );

}

  </script>
</body>

</html>
```

***Live example***

save the above code as a html file three.js/examples/demo.html

**Expected behavior**

The 3D Object can be displayed in its original colors

author: fengxinming

title: Cross-browser issues; helper functions not particularly helpful
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

This library is supposed to enable cross-browser WebGL solutions while shielding the developer from the inherent complexity of WebGL, but these helper functions don't seem to be very helpful in that regard:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/jsm/capabilities/WebGL.js

Assuming those functions are reliable, developers can detect if WebGL 1.0 is available and if WebGL 2.0 is available. And... then what?

Some library methods require the latter, some don't. Some throw exceptions when 2.0 is unavailable, some log errors to the console. So short of slogging through all of the code, which somewhat defeats the purpose of the library, what is a developer supposed to infer from these results?

For example, if 2.0 is available, then methods A, B and C will work and methods D, E and F work only for specific contexts. How would developers know this without reading the code for each method? Perhaps all of this is documented somewhere, but it shouldn't need to be. In short, users of this library should not need to know much of anything about what the library is doing with WebGL under the hood.

Not sure what to tell you, as the only solution I see would require a major re-design and rewrite. The appropriate cross-browser-enabling pattern that would allow for direct inferences is to detect required features internally, at the outset, and to create only those interfaces that will work in the current context.

Then developers could simply check whether the library features are there or not, without knowing anything about the host features it abstracts away. Inferences don't get much more direct than that and these are the sort that are required for simplified cross-browser scripting.

Instead of something like this:

if (isWebGL2Available) {
   A();
   B();
   C();
}

...calling code would simply check if A, B and C exist (usually at the outset) and proceed (or not) accordingly. For D, E and F, some refactoring would be required, as these will work for some cases and not for others. It's critical that the calling code be able to determine what will work before making the calls, else their script will either blow up or silently (for the user) log a failure.

I see a lot of similar forking inside the library as well, which is obviously subject to change over time and the described pattern allows for such changes without breaking existing projects. It would also simplify the code considerably.

I don't really expect you to do anything about this, as virtually every library seems to suffer from this same affliction and it is obviously very late in the game for this one. Might want to think about a complete overhaul if a sequel is ever considered.
author: david-mark

title: GLTFLoader: No texture.source should be valid?
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

I failed to load a glTF asset 

1. that has `texture` that doesn't have `source` but has `extensions.foo_extension.source`, like the following example, and 
2. that doesn't have `foo_extension` in `extensionsRequired`, and
3. a plugin for handling `foo_extension` is not registered to glTF loader

```
textures: [
  {
    "extensions": {
      "foo_extension": {
        source: 0,
        ...
      }
    }
  }
]
```

The error log is

> Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'uri')

`GLTFParser.loadTexture()` seems to handle `texture` as if `texture.source` is required.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/321106c2bfd447ccfd5fb2a077af366955733291/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js#L3152-L3160

**To Reproduce**

T.B.D.

***Live example***

T.B.D.

**Expected behavior**

Perhaps the loader shouldn't fail to load it? Because in the glTF spec

1. `texture.source` is not required

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#reference-texture

2. `texture.source` description is

> The index of the image used by this texture. When undefined, an extension or other mechanism SHOULD supply an alternate texture source, otherwise behavior is undefined.

in the example, `texture.extensions.foo_extension` supplies a texture source (but the extension is unknown by the glTF loader).

3. 3.8.2 Texture section describs that

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#textures

> When texture.source is undefined, the image SHOULD be provided by an extension or application-specific means, otherwise the texture object is undefined.
> Implementation Note
> Client implementations may render such textures with a predefined placeholder image or being filled with some error color (usually magenta).

If we allow no `texture.source`, `GLTFParser.loadTexture()` will return `null`?

**Alternate solutions**

If we don't allow undefined behavior texture object, the glTF asset should have `foo_extension` in `extensionsRequired`?

The glTF spec mentions that

> All glTF extensions required to load and/or render an asset MUST be listed in the top-level extensionsRequired array

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#specifying-extensions

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop but probably any
 - OS: Windows but probably any
 - Browser: Chrome but probably any
 - Three.js version: dev

/cc @donmccurdy

title: Issues with hand model loading for XR
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

There's a number of issues when actually trying to load custom hand models for XR right now:
- XRHandMeshModel creates its own GLTFLoader that doesn't support Draco / Meshopt, with no way to configure that from the outside
- XRHandMeshModel assumes files end with ".glb", making it hard to use ".gltf" files (e.g. left and right hand could share textures – theoretically possible but uncommon for glb files to reference external textures).
- OculusHandModel doesn't expose the "path" parameter, so custom hands can't be loaded

**To Reproduce**

Try to use a custom hand model with draco compression for Oculus Quest 2.

**Expected behavior**

Parameters are passed through properly, e.g. a loader can be passed into XRHandMeshModel and OculusHandModel. This might make the "path" parameter redundant, too (since it could be set on the loader).

author: ycw

title: TransformControl factor calculation when using Orthographic camera
body:
When using the Orthographic Camera the zoom level of the camera affects the transformation sensitivity way too much compared to Perspective camera. 

I've built an application similar to a house planner in feature set. In it, you can switch between a Perspective or an Orthographic camera while trying to preserve the perceived view of the object when switching. This means having to approximate the zoom level of the Orthographic camera to the distance to target of the Perspective camera. 

While this is working to some extent (a feature to match these would be nice) I'm having problems with TransformControls on a selected object while in orthographic mode.  When having the zoom to match a close up view of an object in order to rotate the object a few degrees you need to move your mouse and entire screen width. This is not the case with perspective mode where the transformation factor is much higher regardless of the distance to target.

I've narrowed down the code inside TransformControls that is responsible for this: 

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/868aee4c3bda29077ba53989cea07948a474782e/examples/jsm/controls/TransformControls.js#L1178-L1188

Because of this, the more you zoom in, on orthographic view it becomes harder (slower) to transform objects and easier (faster) when you zoom out.

There might be a use-case where this makes sense to this extent, I just can't think of it. Is this a bug or is it intended functionality?
It makes sense to be more sensitive when zooming in but not this much. Also it then should also work the same in perspective view.

title: customDistanceMaterial shadowmap issue with gpgpu particles
body:
**Describe the bug**

I am currently exploring GPGPU particle simulations with `THREE.Points`. Because the particle position is set by the vertex shader of the particle I also need to provide a `customDistanceMaterial`. In the vertexshader of the `customDistanceMaterial` I also update the vertexposition according to the positionTexture. The Issue is that the shadowcamera of the `THREE.PointLight` does not seem to pickup the particles in all directions. In my demo, when moving the lightsource around you will notice that particles may appear on other planes of the `PointLight` cubemap but the particles will never be rendered on all planes at the same time which they should do if the `PointLight` is positioned inside the particle orb.
Note: I know the fragment shader is missing the correct light position to correctly calculate the depth, this is another issue on what the best way would be to let threejs set the referencePosition automatically as in [WebGLShadowMap.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9203453841bb9825e3db559a98797d1e6b7085e3/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLShadowMap.js#L305).

**To Reproduce**

* Open live demo and check the shadowmap

***Code***

* [scene repo](https://github.com/kinglouie/fboshadows/tree/master/src/lib/webglscene)
* [particle distance vertex shader](https://github.com/kinglouie/fboshadows/blob/master/src/lib/webglscene/shaders/particlesDistance.vert)

***Live example***

* [live demo of my repo](https://shiny-otter-f0f973.netlify.app/)

**Expected behavior**

Particles shound render on all planes of the `PointLight` shadowmap.

**Screenshots**

If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem (drag and drop the image).

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop and Mobile
 - OS: Windows, ArchLinux, iOS
 - Browser: tested on Firefox, Chrome and mobile Safari
 - Three.js version: r145

**Comment:**
I think the docs around this kind of stuff are a bit sparse and most of the examples I found on google either don't work  with the current three.js version at all or they are only partially working. I would gladly create an example scene demonstrating particles with a `customDistanceMaterial` if you could help me getting it working correctly.

title: Cannot upload a WebGLRenderTarget Texture as a regular texture
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

I am using a `WebGLRenderTarget` to render a scene into a texture. Then I want to apply this texture into an object, and render this object in another scene. I have two distinct `WebGLRenderer`s : the one for the render-to-texture, and one for the regular 3D scene.

When uploading the `Texture` created by the `WebGLRenderTarget`, the WebGL(2) context throws an error :

```
THREE.WebGLState: TypeError: WebGL2RenderingContext.texSubImage2D: Argument 7 is not valid for any of the 7-argument overloads.
```

When debugging the function `texSubImage2D()` : 

```javascript
function texSubImage2D() {

		try {

			gl.texSubImage2D.apply( gl, arguments );

		} catch ( error ) {

			console.error( 'THREE.WebGLState:', error );

		}

	}
```

The 7th argument in `arguments` is the render target `Texture.image`, but it's just a simple JS object with no actual data (since the data is only in the GPU memory I figure) :

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5512096/199688525-bf8c8998-ac1c-48e2-85b9-14d68d36777d.png)

Note : if `Texture.needsUpdate = false`, then the error does not appear, but the texture is not uploaded.

**To Reproduce**

See fiddle

***Code***

See fiddle

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/yrhsq7cL/2/)

**Expected behavior**

We should be able to use a render target texture like any other texture. Probably also the texture should not need to be "uploaded" as its data is already in GL memory.

**Screenshots**

N/A

 - Device: Desktop

title: FBXLoader.js should use the "min" function and not the "max" function when limiting the penumbra value.
body:
**Describe the bug**

`FBXLoader.js` should be use the "min" function instead of the "max" function for the _penumbra_ limiting statement.

***Code***

Here is the suspect code.  The code uses the "max" function when it should use "min" to limit the _penumbra_ to a value less than or equal 1. 

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f54eb5b0bf7baa7132acc26c799bbb16bda136a8/examples/jsm/loaders/FBXLoader.js#L1206-L1217

Also, wouldn't the first statement better as follows?:
```js
        penumbra = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad( lightAttribute.OuterAngle.value  / 360.0 );
	penumbra = Math.min( penumbra, 1 );
```
By dividing the FBX model angle value by 360 you rescale it to 0, 1.

author: Creative-Ataraxia

title: DragControls should allow user to decide whether to recursively raycast draggable objects
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Drag controls currently always supplies a `true` recursive raycasting value when deciding which object should be dragged (eg. when it calls `_raycaster.intersectObjects( _objects, true, _intersections );` . This means currently there's no way to make an object draggable without making it's children draggable.

In some scene graphs the users want to make the ancestor nodes draggable while their children stay non-draggable. For example to drag groups together while the children stay locked in place.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

`DragControls` should allow the user to set a recursive option like `dragControls.recursive = false;` when the user decides whether they would like to supply the ancestors of draggable nodes or they need fine-grained control around which object is draggable.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Perhaps the API could be `dragControls.setRecursive(value);` or `new DragControls(objects, camera, domElement, recursive);` but I think this bloats the API and makes it less simple to use.

**Additional context**

One main use case for this is when the graph is laid out using a layout algorithm (eg. force layout) and we want to drag groups while locking the group children in place without making them draggable.

title: Orbitcontrols removes mouseup event from all child elements
body:
**Describe the bug**

Having a child element inside of the orbitcontrol's domElement will prevent the child element from receiving mouseUp/pointerUp events.

**To Reproduce**
1.Create a div
2.Put canvas in div (your renderer canvas)
2.Put another element in div (I tried it with another canvas)
3.Instantiate threejs.
4.Instantiate orbitcontrols with the DIV as domElement.

now try to listen for mouseup events on the div's second element, it won't work

**What is causing this?**

I figured out that orbitcontrols is deleting the event. This happens in the function [``removePointer``](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/js/controls/OrbitControls.js#L984), to be specific when calling ``pointers.splice( i, 1 );``. You can comment it out and the event will work again.

**Expected behavior**

The mouseup and pointerup event works in the child element, and orbitcontrols does not alter the behavior.

**Platform:**

 - OS: Windows 11
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: r146

title: Issues: Inaccuracy NRRD slices' number
body: **Describe the bug** When we render a NRRD file on threejs, the total slices' number does not match the actual NRRD slices' number. For example, A 3D dimensional for a NRRD file is 256 x 256 x 113, then if we load it on ITK snap software we can get the z position has 113 slices, the x position has 256 slices, and the y position has 256 slices. See fig1. Then if we render the NRRD file on threejs, we can get the result Fig2. The z position at least has 168 slices, but the x and y position only have 238 slices. If threejs render a NRRD file like this, How do we find out how the NRRD slice in threejs corresponds to the actual NRRD slice? For further debugging (Fig3), I found out the threejs are based on `RASDimensions` to extract Slice and display, why do not use `dimensions` to handle this? **Screenshots** Fig 1: the NRRD file is loaded in ITK snap software: Fig 2: Same Nrrd file is rendered in Threejs environment: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80797317/200206800-3394f4bc-f313-4f67-a02d-e6558d4556a5.png) Fig 3: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/80797317/200207752-dbe1c640-3c8e-4127-b7f1-9330274fca63.png) **Platform:** - Device: [Desktop] - OS: [Windows, MacOS,] - Browser: [Chrome] - Three.js version: [dev, r144]
