author: jajetloh

title: Drawing TubeGeometry segments at non-equidistant points
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Using `TubeGeometry.setDrawRange()` to control the amount of a `TubeGeometry` that is rendered to/from a precise location on the tube's path is difficult, as `TubeGeometry` uses `path.getPointAt()` to find equidistant locations along the path to draw vertices.

For example, consider a `TubeGeometry` constructed from a `CurvePath` with 4 segments (5 vertices) with respective lengths (1,2,3,4), and we wish to display any continuous section between two vertices. This is a small example, and workable with some alternatives explained below, but this breaks down in the general case of not-so-nice coordinates and longer paths.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

This can be fixed relatively easily by allowing the use of `path.getPoint()` instead of `path.getPointAt()` in `TubeGeometry`'s segment generation, triggered by some flag when constructing the `TubeGeometry`. If this is the case, we just need to supply `TubeGeometry` with a path whose vertices are the locations we desire, and the flag, and segments are drawn at the given vertices, and `.setDrawRange()` can easily be used to show segments between vertices. We can then provide the path using a `CurvePath` if issue #24924  is fixed.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I've considered two options besides the proposed solution:

1. Choose the `tubularSegments` parameter to be a multiple of the lengths (1,2,3,4) - we can then figure out what values to pass `.setDrawRange()` to show the parts we want
2. Instead use multiple `TubeGeometry` or `CylinderGeometry` instances

Either option is not great for longer paths, as in case 1. we end up with a gigantic number of segments and in case 2. we have to instantiate and manage a large number of geometries.

We also can't just use a map of `t` to `u` values that are then passed to `TubeGeometry.setDrawRange()` as we aren't guaranteed that the generated segments line up with our desired vertices, again unless we have a large value for `tubularSegments`.

**Additional context**

My use case for this is a physics trajectory visualisation, so precise control of the tube vertices shown is required, and `.setDrawRange()` seems to be the most elegant way to deal with this.

This is a relatively easy feature to add, so if there's agreement that this is a good idea, I can submit a PR for this.
author: jajetloh

title: Insufficient float accuracy in Quaternion.setFromUnitVectors
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

After a bunch of rotations, I call `Quaternion.setFromUnitVectors on two normalised vectors, and get the wrong result.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/178028/201024034-6d1777d6-88f7-400b-b260-769e590fdc8a.png)

because the dot product fails the `if ( r < Number.EPSILON ) {` test. 

**To Reproduce**

```js
new Quaternion().setFromUnitVectors(new Vector3(0,0,-1), new Vector3(0,0,1))
Quaternion {isQuaternion: true, _x: 0, _y: 1, _z: 0, _w: 0}

new Quaternion().setFromUnitVectors(new Vector3(0,1.1102230246251563e-16,-0.9999999999999998), new Vector3(0,0,1))
Quaternion {isQuaternion: true, _x: 0.4472135954999578, _y: -0, _z: 0, _w: 0.8944271909999159}
```

***Live example***

https://jsfiddle.net/gromgull/3bfgrj9e/ (console output only) 

**Expected behavior**

My vectors are preeetty close to pointing in the opposite direction. 
I "fixed" this by using my own `setFromUnitVectors` utility function where I compare to EPSILON*2

In this particular case, doing `<= Number.EPSILON` also works, but I wanted some safety margin ;) 

**Platform:**

Any
 - Three.js version: [r145] (but I assume almost any version)

title: FileLoader: Allow custom Content-Length header for file loader total value
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Using Nginx's gzip feature removes the _Content-Length_ header that comes back from the server. This causes the _FileLoader_ to always report 0 as total for every progress event. Therefore, the [onProgress](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e1964f20e4f1d133b2ac0acaec722946af773102/src/loaders/FileLoader.js#L139) callback becomes somewhat useless. In my case it makes it impossible to track progress of loading an OBJ file with the [OBJLoader](https://threejs.org/docs/#examples/en/loaders/OBJLoader)

I know the following line is in the documentation. However, this does not help in my case.
_If the server does not set the Content-Length header; .total will be 0._

**Describe the solution you'd like**

It would be nice to add a secondary header like _X-Content-Length_ or _X-File-Size_ to be also accepted at https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e1964f20e4f1d133b2ac0acaec722946af773102/src/loaders/FileLoader.js#L112

Like:
`const contentLength = response.headers.get( 'Content-Length' ) || response.headers.get( 'X-File-Size' );`

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I am not sure if there is a simpler alternative to this. 

**Additional context**

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

title: Offloading the Hosting and Maintenance of the Non-English Docs
body:
I propose that the Non-English docs be hosted elsewhere -- and maintained by others.

Being responsible for ensuring the English and non-English docs remain in sync is not an efficient use of the three.js maintainers' time.

Furthermore, it is a burden that is only going to increase as more languages are supported and the docs become more complete.

This thread is for discussing how offloading the hosting and maintenance of the Non-English docs may best be accomplished.

It would be helpful if someone would be willing to take responsibility for this task. 😇

title: Objects with alphaClip render opaque shadows when `map` is used instead of `alphaMap`
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Seems that while https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/22410 added support for alpha clipping in shadow maps, it did so only when `alphaMap` is used, not then the alpha channel of `map` is used for transparency. 
This results in, for example, all glTF files loaded at runtime (e.g. with GLTFLoader) not supporting alpha clipping in shadows.

I've seen a few mentions of using `customDepthMaterial` to work around this issue, but in fact this part of the docs was removed when #22410 was merged. Thus I think the missing case for map + clipping may be an oversight / bug here.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to https://threejs.org/editor
2. Add a cube using "map" with alpha channel and a cube with "alphaMap"
3. Add a Directional Light
4. Add a floor plane
5. Enable Cast shadows on the light
6. Enable Cast+Receive Shadows on the cubes and the plane
7. Note that alpha clipping happens on the object but not on the shadows

**Expected behavior**

Alpha clipping in the shadows.

**Screenshots**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/203051737-e61fa9d1-fe07-4b4d-9ae4-293beb8791ce.png)
author: hybridherbst

title: If a material has two maps, they can not be both updated
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

Function refreshUniformsCommon contains a material map switch that effectively updates the first map found on the material, ignoring all others.

**To Reproduce**

An example of LambertMaterial with texture and displacement maps that have their offset changed in animation the loop:

https://discourse.threejs.org/t/lambermaterial-map-displacement-map/44932

***Code***

```js
line 25938 v146:

let uvScaleMap;

if ( material.map ) {

	uvScaleMap = material.map;

} else if ( material.specularMap ) {

	uvScaleMap = material.specularMap;

} else if ( material.displacementMap ) {

	uvScaleMap = material.displacementMap;

} else ...

line 26022:

if ( uvScaleMap !== undefined ) {

        // backwards compatibility
	if ( uvScaleMap.isWebGLRenderTarget ) {

		uvScaleMap = uvScaleMap.texture;

	}

	if ( uvScaleMap.matrixAutoUpdate === true ) {

		uvScaleMap.updateMatrix();

	}
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-1](https://jsfiddle.net/tfoller/zbdq4y0k/21/)
* [jsfiddle-2](https://jsfiddle.net/tfoller/zbdq4y0k/20/)

title: Instanced meshes where some instances are negatively scaled while others are positively scaled.
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

A previous pull request fixed the flipping of faces with negative scales on meshes: [(WIP: Support negative scale factors #12720)](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/12720).

This however does not work for _instanced meshes_ where some instances have negative scale factors while others have positive ones. I also do not know of a way to calculate it manually when the geometry can't be updated (as not all instances will be scaled negatively).

**Describe the solution you'd like**

A way of negatively scaling some instances in an instanced mesh, while keeping some instances positively scaled.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Creating two instanced meshes, one for flipped meshes and one for non-flipped. This however gets tricky to manage if the user wants to update matrices and therefore move them between the two instanced meshes.
author: sezze

title: Incorrect handling of zero-filled accessors when morph animations are present
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

_Requsted to be filed here from the model-viewer repo: https://github.com/google/model-viewer/issues/3946_

**Describe the bug**

The attached file is valid GLB with morph animations. It is modified version of the MorphStressTest glTF sample asset.

In the original sample asset, the scene contains only one mesh with 2 primitives. The "floor" primitive doesn't morph, which is achieved by accessors for all morph targets being zero explictly.

In the modified asset the same is true, but the targets don't state the NORMAL attribute and the POSITION attribute doesn't have a bufferView which means it should be read as all zeros. Both of these are allowed by the spec:
https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#:~:text=Attributes%20present%20in,sets%20of%20attributes.
https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#:~:text=The%20index%20of%20the%20buffer%20view.%20When%20undefined%2C%20the%20accessor%20MUST%20be%20initialized%20with%20zeros

The attached asset has no validation issues and loads and plays animations correctly in Blender and Babylon.js.

[morphTest.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/10057173/morphTest.zip)

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to https://modelviewer.dev/editor/ or https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/
2. Upload the attached glb
3. The model doesn't load correctly despite being a valid asset

**Expected behavior**

The model should load and animate correctly.

**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: Windows
 - Browser: Chrome,  Edge
 - Three.js version: r146
author: Aloalo

title: `Material.setValues()` does not work properly with `._listeners` and `.alphaTest`
body:
**Describe the bug**
`Material.setValues( values )` function does not work properly with `._listeners` and `.alphaTest` properties if `values` is a material - it produces a warning on the first one (if there is a listener on `values`) and does not copy the second (because it is not enumerable in a `for-in` loop).

**Expected behavior**
Either `_listeners` property should be ignored and no warning should be produced and `alphaTest` should be succesfully copied or it should be explicitly noted that `Material.setValues` does not accept materials, only plain objects.

author: ANFADEV

title: Ability to control `texture.anisotropy` from `GLTFLoader`
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

We were often wondering why textures in three.js looked more blurry than in other engines. See for example the files in https://github.com/google/model-viewer/issues/1505. Turns out that while generally texture anisotropy can be set, GLTFLoader currently doesn't expose a way to control it, so glTF viewers generally look "kind of blurry" from steep angles.  
 
I'm happy to work on a PR for this but would like to align on possible solutions first.  

The renderer has `renderer.capabilities.getMaxAnisotropy` which contains the maximum allowed anisotropy, but I think being able to pass in an override for this into the loader would be nice instead of just setting the max (and there's currently no access to the renderer as far as I can see, minus spawning a new one).  

Currently I'm just adding `texture.anisotropy = 4;` in GLTFLoader.loadTextureImage so that it applies to textures loaded via extensions as well.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Some way to override texture anisotropy as used by GLTFLoader.  

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Adding some texture loading extension. Complexity would be that this would still need to work with all other texture loading extensions, so not entirely sure how that would work.  

- Iterating over all materials and all textures in the glTF file and setting anisotropy there. Harder than it should be in my opinion but maybe I'm missing a convenient way to do that.

If there's a good way to set this without changing GLTFLoader I'm all ears :)

author: hybridherbst

title: Default background for transmissive objects
body:
**Problem**

An object with `MeshPhysicalMaterial` and transmission props (`transmission`,  `attenuationColor`, `attenuationDistance`, `thickness`) needs an opaque background to be normally rendered. With a transparent background (and no other opaque objects behind it) it's either invisible or completely transparent, even if it has `attenuationColor` and `thickness`. 

The same problem is [described here.](https://discourse.threejs.org/t/transmission-and-transparent-canvas-workaround/29395)

**Desirable solution**

It would be nice to have something like a `transmissionBackground` prop in the Scene object, that wouldn't be rendered as a normal background, but would be used as a default background for all transmissive objects. 

**Alternatives**

A default background color for transmissive objects (but a texture is preferable of course).

**Additional context**

It seems to be possible. 

If you replace [this line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/146bc1893fd1f8cd4fefeb918977a479752a23c6/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1218)

```js
if ( opaqueObjects.length > 0 ) renderObjects( opaqueObjects, scene, camera );
```

with 

```js
const filteredObjects = opaqueObjects.filter( obj => ! obj.material?.name?.startsWith( 'Background' ) );
if ( filteredObjects.length > 0 ) renderObjects( filteredObjects, scene, camera );
```
Then the scene background will not be rendered, but it will still be used for transmissive objects.

Instead of this: 

![with_background](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/96049300/203970358-bd1d67d8-37f7-405d-9c74-5c6adeca43dc.png)

You will get this: 

![without_background](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/96049300/203970456-98f3faf3-b340-4894-8724-9d23b74eceaa.png)

title: GLTF exporter doesn't correctly handle normalized attributes
body:
**Describe the bug**

The GLTF exporter currently exports normalized attributes as their 0-1 float value instead of their 0-255 integer values. This affects the attribute buffers themselves as well as the gltf sampler min and max fields.

This probably broke because of c6a80f9bb8411575a50990e35b9a169c8d409b41


**To Reproduce**

1. Use the gltf exporter to download a model (for example the [fiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/7abpk50j/56/))
2. Load the model file into a validator like https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/
3. Click errors on the bottom left to view the errors

***Code***



```js
import { GLTFExporter } from 'three/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter';

function exportModel(node){
    let exporter = new GLTFExporter();
    exporter.parse(node, gltf => resolve(gltf), reject, {
  	binary: true
    });
}
```

***Live example***
https://jsfiddle.net/7abpk50j/56/
click download and then load the file into https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/

**Expected behavior**

The model including the vertex colors load correctly.

**Screenshots**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7593843/204064229-92a3cd75-fb54-49ab-be7e-9baf6f7fdc96.png)
**Platform:**

title: Make exporters work with uninitialized textures (`image=null`).
body:
**Describe the bug**

GLTFExporter currently assumes that all textures have image data; 
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/46fb35af1fb86fb014bf1271c7452c113024420b/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter.js#L1088-L1089

This fails when any Texture is passed in that doesn't have image data (e.g. `new Texture()`) because it causes an exception on export instead of exporting "no texture" for that slot.

The same happens in USDZExporter and probably also in ColladaExporter; not sure if there are others.

**Expected behavior**

File can be exported and texture slot stays unassigned.

**Screenshots**

author: MeRahulAhire

title: PropertyBinding: Support more arbitary objects
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

Related: #24537, #24108

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently `PropertyBinding` can bind only some of the object types, and can't do others. For example non-`map` textures can't be bound. Because of this limitation, Three.js animation system can't handle some type of objects.

The glTF `KHR_animation_pointer` extension that allows animation of arbitary objects in glTF support #24108 is blocked by this problem and the implemenation of it is the way more complex. It is a standard `KHR_` prefix extension so it's good if we can support it with simple implementation when it will be ratified.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

`PropertyBinding` supports more arbitary objects.

I guess the major cause is the keyframe track name has to start relative from the 3D object and the name must be up to three level depth like `name1.name2.name3`.

https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/animation/PropertyBinding.parseTrackName
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24537#issuecomment-1226093348

Relaxing the name level depth limitation (allowing nesting) may resolve the problem, like `name1.name2.name3.name4...`? (If I remember correctly, someone (@WestLangley ?) suggested it before).

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Alternative solution may be allow to specify an animation target with the pair of object and property name? (If we do this, we may need "legacy mode" not to break the compatibility.)

```javascript
new KeyframeTrack(
  object.material.roughnessMap, // target object
  'rotation', // property name
  inputAccessor.array,
  outputArray,
  interpolation
);
```

Is there any strong reason why animation target must be specified with the current `name1.name2.name3` name style?

author: BVPlaten

title: Conditional object transforms in TransformControls
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

When transforming an object using the `TransformControls`, an `objectChange` event will be dispatched after the object been transformed. However, it seems impossible when I want to check if such transforms should be performed at first. For example, when dragging an object and it is not allowed to collide with other objects, the developer should check whether the transform result will produce collisions, and then apply (or not apply) the transform to the object being dragged.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I want a callback function called before applying transforms in `TransformControls`. The transform results of `object.matrix` and `object.matrixWorld` are calculated and passed to the function. The developer can check if the matrix is desired.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Some physical libraries like `ammo.js` can simulate object collisions, but they are heavy and a bit complex to use (compared to `three.js`).

**Additional context**

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

author: lo-th

title: Feature: Add the OpenSSF Scorecard GitHub Action
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Open-source supply-chain attacks are [increasing every year][sonatype]. Due to three.js' position as the most popular JS 3D library, it's been included in the Open Source Security Foundation (OpenSSF)'s list of the 100 most important open-source projects.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add the [OpenSSF][ossf] [Scorecard GitHub Action][sc], which performs dozens of automated [checks][checks] to ensure the project's security posture is continuously solid. The Scorecard is a form of project "meta analysis"; it doesn't detect vulnerabilities in your code, but instead makes sure your settings and security features are following the best practices to minimize the risk of vulnerabilities.

For instance, #24332 was based on feedback from the Scorecards system.

(Spoiler alert: three.js has a very solid 7.5/10 score, which puts it at the top 5% of relevant projects)

Would you be interested in a PR to add this Action?

The Action has an associated badge that can be (optionally) added as well.

**Additional context**

I work for Google (an OpenSSF founding member), working full-time to help open-source maintainers improve their projects' security.

![Detail of a Token-Permissions alert, indicating the specific file and remediation steps][img-detail]

[checks]: https://github.com/ossf/scorecard#scorecard-checks
[faq]: https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/main/docs/faq.md#frequently-asked-questions
[ossf]: https://openssf.org/
[sc]: https://github.com/ossf/scorecard
[sonatype]: https://www.sonatype.com/state-of-the-software-supply-chain/introduction

[img-detail]: https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15221358/190184600-ee8d3b39-077e-416a-8711-1b5fb01cf0b3.png
author: pnacht

title: `ShadowMesh` does not work properly when used with `SkinnedMesh`.
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your bug report getting deleted -->

**Describe the bug**

If `ShadowMesh` is used for `SkinnedMesh`, the shadow is not updated when `SkinnedMesh` is animated.

**To Reproduce**

Code that reproduces the bug.
https://noname0310.github.io/three-shadowmesh-test/

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create an animated Skinned Mesh
2. Add ShadowMesh
3. See error

***Code***

https://github.com/noname0310/three-shadowmesh-test/blob/master/src/index.ts
Code that reproduces the bug. It's almost like the [webgl_loader_mmd](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=mm#webgl_loader_mmd) code in examples, and I just added ShadowMesh.

```js
import * as THREE from "three";
import { MMDAnimationHelper } from "three/addons/animation/MMDAnimationHelper";
import { OrbitControls } from "three/addons/controls/OrbitControls";
import { OutlineEffect } from "three/addons/effects/OutlineEffect";
import { MMDLoader } from "three/addons/loaders/MMDLoader";
import { ShadowMesh } from "three/addons/objects/ShadowMesh";

const clock = new THREE.Clock();

// #region init
const container = document.createElement( 'div' );
document.body.appendChild( container );

const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(45, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 1, 2000);
camera.position.z = 30;

//#region scene

const scene = new THREE.Scene();
scene.background = new THREE.Color(0xffffff);

const gridHelper = new THREE.PolarGridHelper(30, 0);
gridHelper.position.y = -10;
scene.add(gridHelper);

const ambient = new THREE.AmbientLight(0x666666);
scene.add(ambient);

const directionalLight = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0x887766);
directionalLight.position.set(-1, 1, 1).normalize();
scene.add(directionalLight);

//#endregion

const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true });
renderer.setPixelRatio(window.devicePixelRatio);
renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
container.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

const effect = new OutlineEffect(renderer);

//#region model

function onProgress(xhr) {
  if (xhr.lengthComputable) {
    const percentComplete = xhr.loaded / xhr.total * 100;
    console.log(Math.round(percentComplete) + "% downloaded");
  }
}

const modelFile = "https://threejs.org/examples/models/mmd/miku/miku_v2.pmd";
const vmdFiles = [ "https://threejs.org/examples/models/mmd/vmds/wavefile_v2.vmd" ];

const helper = new MMDAnimationHelper({
  afterglow: 2.0
});

const loader = new MMDLoader();

let shadowMesh;
const plane = new THREE.Plane(new THREE.Vector3(0, 1, 0), 0.01 + -10);
const lightPosition = new THREE.Vector4(5, 7, -1, 0.001);

loader.loadWithAnimation(modelFile, vmdFiles, (mmd) => {
  const mesh = mmd.mesh;
  mesh.position.y = -10;
  scene.add(mesh);

  shadowMesh = new ShadowMesh(mesh);

  scene.add(shadowMesh);

  helper.add(mesh, {
    animation: mmd.animation,
    physics: false
  });

  const ikHelper = helper.objects.get(mesh).ikSolver.createHelper();
  ikHelper.visible = false;
  scene.add(ikHelper);

}, onProgress, undefined);

//#endregion

const controls = new OrbitControls(camera, renderer.domElement);
controls.minDistance = 10;
controls.maxDistance = 100;

window.addEventListener("resize", onWindowResize);
// #endregion

animate();

function onWindowResize() {
  camera.aspect = window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight;
  camera.updateProjectionMatrix();

  effect.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
}

//

function animate() {
  requestAnimationFrame(animate);
  render();
}

function render() {
  helper.update(clock.getDelta());
  shadowMesh?.update(plane, lightPosition);
  effect.render(scene, camera);
}

```

***Live example***

https://jsfiddle.net/jbkfg23t/26/

**Expected behavior**

The shadow must be updated to match the animation.

**Screenshots**

![2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/48761044/205304308-0e34cd71-7b20-446f-8c4b-1afc25f74506.png)

author: optimus007

title: GPU implementation differences with texture anisotropy and NEAREST filtering
body:
**Describe the bug**

Turns out #25015 has uncovered (not introduced!) a bug/inconsistency in drivers affecting three.js: when texture ansiotropy is != 1, the actual filtering mode is ignored in _some_ implementations (but not others) and results in linear filtering being used. This is problematic for cases where NEAREST filtering was actually the intended result (e.g. pixel art). There doesn't seem to be a cross-platform way to get anisotropic NEAREST filtering; I can understand how that may be undefined.

More references:
- https://bugs.chromium.org/p/angleproject/issues/detail?id=4994
- https://github.com/KhronosGroup/WebGL/pull/3159
- https://github.com/KhronosGroup/Vulkan-Docs/issues/1361#issuecomment-692232030

Some comments from those:
> The only thing we can recommend is avoiding them: use aniso only when it will give a desired quality boost vs plain LINEAR.

>  if you ask for anisotropic filtering but with NEAREST or maxAnisotropy = 1.0, you get what you get

So we'll probably want to avoid that anisotropy is ever used if the filtering is NEAREST.
I'm happy to make a PR: I would probably check here
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/d7b1ca7446ee21f1e1e3e5f15536e53c97a371dd/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L573
if NEAREST is used and skip setting anisotropy in that case.

**To Reproduce**

- Set Texture.DEFAULT_ANISOTROPY to 4 (or set loaded texture's ansio to 4)
- Load this texture with NEAREST filtering:
![pixelArt](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/205449449-17cec856-5f11-411f-9458-dd753ebd5101.png)
- or this glb which contains the same: 
[PixelArt.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/10146851/PixelArt.zip)

**Expected behavior**

Point filtering stays point filtering, even with anisotropy enabled, users shouldn't have to know about hardware implementation bugs.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop]
 - OS: [Windows]
 - Browser: [Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge]
 - Three.js version: [dev]
author: hybridherbst

title: BufferGeometry.calculateBoundingSphere fails due to NaN values
body:
**Describe the bug**
		
BufferAttribute.js has this line:
this.count = array !== undefined ? array.length / itemSize : 0;

Under some circumstances, especially on large meshes, in my case array.length was 321544.  And thus the count can be set to 107181.333 due to floating point errors. 

That has brought me other issues, BufferGeometry.calculateBoundingSphere failed to calculate bounding sphere due to NaN values.

**Inplace fix**

```
    const vbVertices = new Float32BufferAttribute(vertices, 3)
    vbVertices.count = Math.floor( vertices.length / 3 )
    geometry.setAttribute('position', vbVertices)
```


**Platform:**

 - Device: Desktop
 - OS: MacOS
 - Browser: Chrome
 - Three.js version: 141
author: Bangybug

title: EnvMap artifacting with 'mediump' precision on Quest 2 after upgrade from r132->r147
body:
**Describe the bug**

I recently upgraded a project from r132 -> r147. In r147, applying envmap to a MeshStandardMaterial shader set to 'mediump' precision causes cubemap artifacting on Quest 2 hardware.

**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to [this jsfiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/zyxdbkvo/7/)
2. Observe reflections on the cylinder from the envmap.
3. When the Precision option (in the GUI) is set to mediump or lowp, visible banding artifacts are visible.

When I dig in further, it appears that the `vec3 bilinearCubeUV( sampler2D envMap, vec3 direction, float mipInt )`  function in cube_uv_reflection_fragment.js is the cause of this artifacting. If I add a `precision highp float;` to the start of that function, which sets floating point precision to high just for the scope of that function IIUC, the issue goes away.

I'm not sure if this also reproduces on Android devices, as I don't have easy access to one to test.

**Screenshots**
![no_artifacts_at_highp](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/59743491/205474268-6460ea3b-4c7b-440c-84a2-70200b2120a4.jpg)
![banding_artifacts_at_mediump](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/59743491/205474269-ae05d25e-9e6c-46e4-8893-f0ce0b51900b.jpg)

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Quest 2]
 - OS: [Quest VR OS]
 - Browser: [Meta Quest Browser]
 - Three.js version: [r147]
author: davehill00

title: From InstancedMesh to more generic Instances
body:
<!-- Ignoring this template may result in your feature request getting deleted -->

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Three.js has `InstancedMesh` API to allow easy GPU instancing handling.

It extends `Mesh` and the constructor takes `geometry`, `material`, and `count`.

Because of this design, it has limiation. It is hard to make the use of GPU instancing with non-regular `Mesh`, eg. `SkinnedMesh`, `Line`, or `Points`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

We support more generic GPU instancing API. 

Perhaps `Instances( Mesh|SkinnedMesh|Line|Points (or whatever), count )`.

It would be more flexible.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

1. Make `Instanced` API for each object type. eg. `InstancedSkinnedMesh`, `InstancedLine`, `InstancedPoints`. But I don't think it's a good idea because of complexity.
2. Three.js already has `InstancedBufferGeometry/Attribute` and they can be used for non-regular `Mesh`es (?). But it may be harder to use for generic users.

**Additional context**

The idea is from

* https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23835#discussion_r844829963
* https://twitter.com/superhoge/status/1599509848995299329 (This may be the first issue originally raised from AI?)

title: Decide whether class fields can be used in core
body:
Related:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23480
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24237
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25104

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

We should decide whether class fields (public, private, and static) could be currently used in core or is it still too soon.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I personally support using class fields, because they will allow us to shoot three birds with one arrow - we would be able to use static fields for properties like in animation and interpolant (I think?) classes, private fields for properties like `Material.#alphaTest`, and public fields for finally solving the .isClass problem - public fields work best because they do not create a boolean for all instances, IIRC.

author: Hartaj-Singh-Dev

title: Potential improvement of "real-world" performance of updateMatrixWorld()
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

When analyzing peformance of applications built using THREE.js, I often find the per-frame call to updateMatrixWorld() to be a key bottleneck.

I've been looking at the updateMatrixWorld() Benchmark tests at /test/benchmark/benchmarks.html

On my system, this test shows ~250 ops/second for the large graph (depth=9, breadth=3), 29524 nodes.

That suggests that at 50fps, updateMatrixWorld for ~30k nodes should be taking ~20% of my CPU.

My experience is that real-world performance is nothing like as good as this.

For example, a recent scene I was looking at had 18k nodes, and 41.6% of CPU spent in updateMatrixWorld().  That's about 1/3rd of the performance shown by that benchmark.

I've been looking at possible reasons for this, and I believe I have found a significant factor in real-world performance, which is:
- not accounted for in the Benchmark test
- could potentially be addressed with some code changes (I have a prototype that shows a ~50% gain in "real world" conditions).

That specific factor is the topic of this issue

**More detail.**

The current Benchmark tests create a homegeneous scene graph, consisting entirely of vanilla Object3Ds.  That's not realistic: most real-world scene graphs will consist of a range of different sub-classes of Object3D: Groups, Meshes, SkinnedMeshes, InstancedMeshes etc.

This matters because the dynamic nature of JavaScript objects means that performance for access to object properties can be highly variable.  I'm by no means an expert on this, but I have found [this article](https://javascript.plainenglish.io/exploration-of-javascript-object-for-performance-optimization-70b20246ab9e) to be a good primer on the topic.

Under PR #25113, I've added an additional benchmark test with a slight modification.  Instead of creating a homogeneuous population of vanilla Object3Ds, it creates a randomized blend with:
- 5% InstancedMeshes
- 5% SkinnedMeshes
- 50% Meshes
- 40% Groups

I don't make any claims that this is the "correct" mix for a "real-world" benchmark, but I think it is extremely notable that when run with this mix, the benchmark performance drops (on my system) from about 250 ops/second to around 125 ops/second - i.e. drops by ~50% (I'm rounding these numbers because in my experience the values output by these tests vary by +/-10% from one run to the next, regardless of the +/- figure reported by the benchmark tool)



![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16045703/206909092-fc411781-ff2c-4cc0-90c2-41fd6c09fa69.png)


I think this is significant, both for estimating absolute performance of Three.js, and also for assessing gains from fixes in this area - with the current Benchmarks, there is a risk of taking fixes that improve homogeneous performance, but actually make real-world performance worse.

**Why the big difference?**

My understanding (based on the article linked above) is that this is likely to be mostly due to performance reductions when accessing Object3D properties.  To access these properties, the JS engine has to look up a "hidden class" in order to determine the property offsets.  When the object population is homogeneous (or "monomorphic"), this lookup can be cached, and the cache will be hit 100% of the time.  When the object population is heterogeneous (or "polymorphic"), this leads to cache missed, and a significant performance impact.

I think the heterogeneous/polymorphic object population is very probably more representative of "real world" performance than the homogeneous/monomorphic polulation used in the current Benchmark.


Regardless of whether we make any improvements to THREE.js itself, I think it's worth adding a more realistic benchmark here.  I don't claim that the profile offered in #25113 is perfect (I'm sure it's strongly biased to the way I tend to use THREE.js), and would be happy to iterate on the "correct" blend of objects to represent real-world usage under that PR.


**Can we fix this?**

Based on my understanding of the performance difference, I believe we can make some significant improvements here.

I have created a draft PR #25114 that shows a prototype set of changes, that increases the performance of the new benchmark from ~120 ops/sec to ~180 ops/sec, while leaving the previous homogeneous benchmark unchanged at around 250 ops/sec (the slight reduction here seems to be well within the margin of error of these tests).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16045703/206909176-9b802f49-6358-45e3-9fd0-7a9c38dc3f25.png)


You can see the specific code changes in the PR, but in summary:
- I've created a new class Object3DMatrixData, which contains all the properties that needs to be accessed in updateMatrixWorld() - 2 x matrices, some flags, position, quaternion & scale.
- On creation, Object3D instantiates a new instance of Object3DMatrixData, and also sets up a number of properties, getters & setters to map through to these (so that Object3D's external interface is unchanged).
- The implementation of updateMatrixWorld is moved onto the Object3DMatrixData class, with the Object3D.updateMatrixWorld() method mapping through to this

**Why does this work?**

The theory is that this change means that when updateMatrixWorld() iterates through all the children in the scene graph, it will always iterate through a homogeneous/monomorphic set of objects, which are all instances of Object3DMatrixData.

This results in property offsets being accessible via a single hidden class, which will reliably be present in the cache.

The benefits are visible in the ~50% performance gain seen in the "real world" benchmark test.



I am disappointed that this change didn't bring this test up to parity with the homogeneous benchmark test.  I wish I understood this area well enough to imagine why that might be, but I'm not yet at that level... 


**Describe the solution you'd like**

I'd like to see the benchmark tests extended to cover more realistic heterogeneous mixes of Object3D classes.

I think the one test that I have created under #25113 shows the importance of realism in test cases.  I propose further discussion over exactly what the right mix to use for a "real world" benchmark test can occur within #25113.

I'd also like to explore the prospect of a fix along the lines of #25114, as I believe there is potential for a performance gain of around 50% on updateMatrixWorld(), without any major negative side effects.  However there may be downsides to the approach I've taken here that I've not fully considered.

Propose discussion of that should continue in #25114.  Decisions that we reach on the "real world" benchmark in #25113 could potentially have an impact on this, as different benchmark tests may give different indications of the performance gains from #25114 (e.g. if we reject #25113 and just stick with the current benchmarks, #25114 doesn't appear to deliver any benefit at all).

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I am aware that updateMatrixWorld() performance has been a longrunning topic, dating back to at least 2015.  I've skimmed over a bunch of issues & PRs in this area, and I can see that there have been a lot of proposals in this area.

From what I can tell, most of the focus in this area has been on reducing the number of matrix updates that need to be performed.  I may have missed something but it appears that none of these discussions led to any changes actually being merged.
E.g. #21401, #14138

(only actual change I found in this area that was merged was #14044 which optimized matrix.compose).

There is a lot of cotent on this topic, and I may have missed something, but I've not seen any discussion looking at how to optimize the traversal the scene graph by engineering for monomorphic object access (which is what #25114 attempts to do, I believe with some success).

Therefore I think these changes would be orthogonal to (and additive to), any changes that might be made along the lines of #14138 (although it looks like there's no active work on that topic at the moment).

**Other info**

All testing referred to above has been done on Chrome on a Windows PC.  Testing on other browsers / platforms is absolutely something that's needed if we take this set of issues forwards.
author: diarmidmackenzie

title: Benchmarks: Contamination between benchmark tests, dependent on ordering
body:
**Describe the bug**

I've been doing some work with the perf benchmarks here: /test/benchmark/benchmarks.html

One issue I have spotted with these benchmarks is that the data output depends on the order that the tests are run in.
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25113#issuecomment-1346124598

Specifically, when I created a test D with a polymorphic set of Object3D sub-classes, and made it the first test run, I noticed that the performance of tests with monomorphic collections of Object3Ds were compromised (as compared to when they get to run first).  Performance was down by > 60% vs. the non-compromised tests.

A plausible explanation for this is that the V8 engine will observe the polymorphic use of functions, and mark these functions up as non-optimizable (see discussion in #25115 & #25113 for background on polymorphism / monomorphism impact on performance & V8 optimizations).

However, it's rather probematic if benchmark test outputs depend on the ordering of the tests - this can be very misleading.

Ideally we'd reset the V8 optimization state from one benchmark test to the next.  However I can't find any API to do this.

It looks like the tests use benchmarkJS v2.1.0.  I've looked through that repo for discussion of how such an issue can be solved. I found just one relevant comment:
https://github.com/bestiejs/benchmark.js/issues/47#issuecomment-17623458

"we do try to keep each sample as free from previous samples hot code contamination. We do this by keeping variables, properties, values unique in each compiled test sample."

Unfortunately this mechanism doesn't seem to be working.  I don't yet have any understanding why not.  I don't have any background on Benchmark.js or how these tests were set up.  It would be great if someone who does can offer any insights here.

@takahirox?

**To Reproduce**

We don't yet have any polymorphic tests in dev, so I need to point to a code sample on a PR that's not merged yet.

1. take the version of UpdateMatrixWorld.js from PR #25113 
2. Run the updateWorldTransform benchmark tests - here's what I saw for the 1st 2 tests.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16045703/207063824-dd333e8b-3a34-4738-b8d8-9bca3d14301e.png)


3. Swap the order to the tests so that D is run first
4. Re-run the tests, and observe that the benchmark results are very different - what I saw for the 1st 2 tests.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16045703/207063362-bc5d97f9-0174-4fdc-b73b-e2e2da03dc95.png)

There does seem to be some variability from any given run to the next, but it's usually +/-10% - whereas this is a 3-fold drop in the performance benchmark.

Note also that PR #25114 addresses the performance issues associated with polymorphism in updateMatrixWorld().  That PR eliminates the effect in this specific example (but doesn't address the generic issue of contamination between tests).

***Live example***

Sorry, I don't have a live example.

**Expected behavior**

I expect benchmark tests to deliver reliable results from one run to the next, regardless of ordering

**Screenshots**

See above.

author: dangarfield

title: Setting up a texture without uploading initial data
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The problem is mainly related to the performance cost of setting up a big texture which is populated incrementally. Allow me to give some context:

I'm using a custom texture subclass which exposes `texSubImage2D` for that particular texture. The class is inspired by [this code](https://github.com/spite/THREE.UpdatableTexture/blob/master/UpdatableTexture.js), but is adapted to WebGL 2. You can find the custom subclass [here](https://github.com/scalableminds/webknossos/blob/master/frontend/javascripts/libs/UpdatableTexture.ts).

In my use case the updatable texture (typically sized 8192²) is populated in chunks which is why I'm using `texSubImage2D`. This means that the initial texture data is empty. However, with the current approach, I need to allocate a data buffer (e.g., with length == 8192²) so that the texture can be initialized with this data. This is suboptimal from a performance perspective since

1) I need to allocate the data buffer (depending on dtype this can be well over 200 MB) even though I don't need this buffer for the partial updates and
2) threejs uploads the data to the GPU even though it's not really necessary (as it's only zeros). The initial upload roughly takes 1 second for my data load which I'd like to avoid.

For WebGL 1, one could make the texture size 1x1 initially and resize it before the first usage. However, in WebGL 2 this is apparently not possible anymore, since the texture size is immutable.

I found a workaround to the performance problem by passing `{ width: actualWidth, height: actualHeight, data: new Uint32Array(1) }`  to the constructor super call of the Texture class. This avoids the unnecessary memory allocation and texture upload. However, it obviously leads to a WebGL error, since the upload call `texSubImage2D` fails due to the incorrectly sized data array. The rendering works fine afterwards, but the failing WebGL call + error message in the console is not really satisfying to me.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I think, it would be ideal if we could tell ThreeJS somehow that it shouldn't do the initial data upload with `texSubImage2D` or `texImage2D` in `uploadTexture`. The rest of the function (i.e., the whole texture setup) should still happen. For example, `texture.image.suppressInitialTextureUpload = true` could be set on the texture to skip the upload call.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Handling the texture setup without threejs would also be a viable option to me, but I still want to attach the texture to a ThreeJS material so I'm not sure how this could work.
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/17162 could also be of interest, but 1) there's little progress on this regard 2) the lazy initialization wasn't yet discussed there.

title: gl_FrontFacing fails when a DoubleSide object is marked as transparent
body:
**Describe the bug**

I have a fragment shader that uses the OpenGL boolean variable [`gl_FrontFacing`](https://registry.khronos.org/OpenGL-Refpages/gl4/html/gl_FrontFacing.xhtml) to change the color of the fragment for backside faces.

This works fine as long as the material is not `transparent`. This seems to be related to how `WebGLRenderer` handles double-sided transparent objects as two different render calls :

- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L827
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1313


**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a material with `transparent` as `true` and `side` as `DoubleSide`.
2. Use the following fragment shader: 
```glsl
void main() {
    if (gl_FrontFacing) {
        gl_FragColor = vec4(1, 0, 0, 1);
    } else {
        gl_FragColor = vec4(0, 0, 1, 1);
    }
}

```
4. The backside of the object should be blue, but it its red instead. Set `transparent` to `false`, and the backside becomes blue.

***Code***

```js
// code goes here
```

***Live example***

* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/jmef760t/15/)

**Expected behavior**

The backside should stay blue even when `transparent` is `true`.

**Screenshots**

See the jsfiddle.

**Platform:**

 - Device: [Desktop, Mobile]
 - OS: Tested on MacOS X, Windows 10, and Ubuntu 22.04
 - Browser: Chrome, Firefox
 - Three.js version: all ?

author: mrdoob

title: Derivatives should be computed before alpha test
body:
### Description

Hi, 

The computation of tangents for normal mapping relies on computing derivatives in xyz and uv space. Right now in MeshStandardMaterial `alphatest_fragment` comes before those computations. I found that on some devices derivatives on the pixels right next to discarded ones are not computed and set to 0, while on other devices alpha test does not impact the gradient computation. Below I used a custom shader that uses `perturbNormal2Arb` to exaggerate the issue.

![Screenshot from 2022-12-20 17-04-39](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6727524/208909035-267f9781-b012-46b0-9454-fbbf4ef51de4.png)

What solves it: I tried moving the computation of derivatives to before `alphatest_fragment`, it helps. 

![Screenshot from 2022-12-20 17-05-25](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6727524/208909056-917fa441-ebdf-405c-9a30-95362d96c892.png)

Important: It is a hardware/software dependent issue. It only reproduces on Linux with Nvidia drivers. I observe this on two PCs with different driver versions. My current setup is Ubuntu 22.04, Nvidia drivers 520.56.06. The behavior is same in Chrome and Firefox. 

What can be done: 

1) Move computation of derivatives needed in `perturbNormal2Arb` and other places to before `alphatest_fragment`.
2) Do nothing as it is not a common issue, just leave the issue opened for people who will encounter this problem. 

Let me know if you want to go for the 1st option, maybe I could come up with a PR. 

### Reproduction steps

Described above


### Code

-

### Live example
The bug is hardware dependent.

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome, Firefox

Linux

title: Support avif as texture image format
body:
### Description

My Christmas contribution is trying to get AVIF support in GLTF, Three and Babylon.

I Did a PR for the GLTF spec which you can find here
https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/2235

I have also done a PR for three which I'm going to link to from this issue

My thoughts are the following.
Chrome recently gave up on jpeg xl which I personally thought was the superior replacement for gif, png, jpeg, webp.

The next best format after jpeg xl is avif, and since jpeg xl isn't happening I thought I could do a drive trying to improve the file size of gltf files by getting avif support.


### Solution

Add the not yet merged https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/2235 EXT_texture_avif as a supported extension.

### Alternatives

WebP doesn't compress as good as AVIF.

title: Update linter to enforce ES features
body:
### Description

We'll want to update the linter (#23763) to ensure that ES2018 syntax is used consistently in both source and addons (see #25172). Currently it accepts a browserslist rather than an ES version to ensure that experimental browser APIs are used responsibly. This doesn't replace discussion around the use of those features, though.

### Solution

I'd like to replace or add on (preference for the first) a linter plugin that checks against ES features only. This is a common enough thing for me to be comfortable writing one on top of a parser if we can't find something well supported. As this widely affects quality control, this should have great language testing coverage before running wild here.

### Alternatives

Transpilation was mentioned in #24237, but discarded in favor of lighter to no tooling. I'll want to echo that in choice of linter tooling around this issue.

### Additional context

I haven't considered updating browerslist as per https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/121#issuecomment-646956379 since that would give false positives for browser APIs. As this is a moving target, I don't think it's appropriate to update mdcs.
author: CodyJasonBennett

title: ColorKeyframeTrack does not accept THREE.Color
body:
### Description

`values`, the third argument of [ColorKeyframeTrack](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/animation/tracks/ColorKeyframeTrack), is intended to be given either `number[]` or `TypedArray`.
However, if end developers give `THREE.Color[]` by mistake, it causes a flashing light.

I don't know why it causes the flashing light, but I think it's not the point.
If possible, I would want to add a validation code to prevent the behavior.
There is no need to actually accept `THREE.Color`s here I think.

For example, the input `[ THREE.Color(), THRE.Color() ]` will be converted to `[ NaN, NaN ]` by `AnimationUtils.convertArray( values, this.ValueBufferType );`.

The call of `AnimationUtils.convertArray()`: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/309b00afb6dcbc5e6c58e72f10eaa8d2e8888c83/src/animation/KeyframeTrack.js#L20-L21
The implementation of `AnimationUtils.convertArray()`: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/309b00afb6dcbc5e6c58e72f10eaa8d2e8888c83/src/animation/AnimationUtils.js#L19-L33


### Reproduction steps

- Give `THREE.Color[]` to `ColorKeyframeTrack`
- Play the animation
- Flashing lights

I'm 100% sure this is not an intended way to use `ColorKeyframeTrack`, to clarify.


### Code

```js
// ...

const track = new THREE.ColorKeyframeTrack(
  'sphere.material.color',
  [ 0.0, 1.0 ],
  // [ 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1 ], // correct one
  [ new THREE.Color( 1, 1, 0 ), new THREE.Color( 0, 1, 1 ) ], // wrong one, causes flashing light
  THREE.InterpolateLinear,
);

// ...
```


### Live example

**CW: FLASHING LIGHT**
https://glitch.com/edit/#!/sleet-yielding-carrot

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r147

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

Windows
author: 0b5vr

title: VectorKeyframeTrack goes an infinite loop when I give a wrong length of `values`
body:
### Description Related: #25186 In the constructor of `VectorKeyframeTrack`, when `values.length / times.length` is not dividable, it will go the infinite loop. I think it also applies to `ColorKeyframeTrack` and `QuaternionKeyframeTrack`. I think we need validation of the array size of `values` for these keyframe track classes. Where the infinite loop happens: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/309b00afb6dcbc5e6c58e72f10eaa8d2e8888c83/src/math/interpolants/LinearInterpolant.js#L23-L29 The image below is when I give `times = [0, 1]` and `value = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` to `VectorKeyframeTrack`: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7824814/209348309-1be47dea-80c4-4d9f-bf5e-4a65ac9fa21c.png) ### Reproduction steps 1. Give a wrong sized array to `values` of the constructor of `VectorKeyframeTrack` 2. Call `AnimationMixer.update();` 3. Go the infinite loop
Stack trace of the `AnimationMixer.update()` part:
  1. `AnimationMixer.update()`
  2. `AnimationAction._update()`
  3. `Interpolant.evaluate()`
  4. `LinearInterpolant.interpolate_()`
### Code ```js const track = new THREE.VectorKeyframeTrack( 'sphere.position', [ 0.0, 1.0 ], [ 1, 1, 0, 1, 1 ], // have to be a multiple of 3, but it's not THREE.InterpolateLinear, ); ``` ### Live example **CW: It will go the infinite loop and the thread usage hits 100%** https://glitch.com/edit/#!/blossom-burly-whip?path=main.js%3A24%3A2 ### Screenshots _No response_ ### Version r147 ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS Windows

author: fengxinming

title: GLTFLoader: Non-deterministic order of children
body:
### Description

Recent changes to GLTFLoader no longer loads child objects in an predictable order.

Object hierarchies from GLB files have loaded in predictable order for a few versions. That is no longer the case.  

We now need to interrogate the properties of each sub object in an array to correctly assign meshes and bones.  Not sure if this should have been the case and I have been relying on undocumented behaviour by assuming consistent indexes and ordering of components in arrays.

Recent optimisations in loading are great but I've lost more cycles interrogating each node in the hierarchy than I've saved.

### Reproduction steps

1. load a model with GLTFLoader on r147 with 2 children, mesh and bones
2. assign mesh and skeleton using the predictable indexes of the children, in this case 0 = mesh, 1 = skeleton
3. confirm working correctly
4. upgrade to r148 and load the same object using the updated GLTFLoader 
5. note skeleton is now index 0, mesh is index 1 in the children array
6. assign the wrong objects to the wrong values, embrace sadness


### Code

```js
// issue
this.armature = {'some object loaded with r148'}
this.mesh = this.armature.children[1];  // indexes are no longer predictable could be 1 or 0
this.skeleton = this.armature.children[0]; // its a 50/50 guess now

// fix 
this.mesh = expensiveTraversalAndChecksForMesh(this.armature.children); 
this.skeleton = expensiveTraversalAndChecksForSkeleton(this.armature.children);
```


### Live example

* i don't have a live example

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r148

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

title: MeshDepthMaterial with alphaTest not working
body:
### Description

Trying to cast a shadow with `alphaTest` using a `customDepthMaterial` appears not to be working since ~r0.132. I've provided a link to a sandbox example that is working correctly, but selecting a later version of the library causes it to break.

I have found that if the `customDepthMaterial` is also set as the `material` of the mesh, then the `customDepthMaterial` does work (somewhat), although changing this at runtime has different results than doing so before the mesh gets rendered.

### Reproduction steps

1. create a scene with a mesh and shadow-casting light
2. assign a `customDepthMaterial` with a `map` and some value of `alphaTest` to the mesh.


### Code

```js
const loader = new THREE.TextureLoader();
const texture = loader.load("/whatever.png");
const shadowCasterGeometry = new THREE.PlaneGeometry(10, 10);
const shadowCasterMaterial = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({
  color: "#fff",
  side: THREE.DoubleSide,
  map: texture,
  transparent: true
}) as THREE.Material;
const shadowCaster = new THREE.Mesh(shadowCasterGeometry, shadowCasterMaterial);
shadowCaster.customDepthMaterial = new THREE.MeshDepthMaterial(
  {
    map: texture,
    alphaTest: 0.6,
    depthPacking: THREE.RGBADepthPacking,
    transparent: false
  }
);
```


### Live example

* [codesandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/three-depthmaterialbug-jfwox7?file=/src/index.ts)

the fix is to just overwrite onBuild again for MeshDistanceMaterial.

body:
### Description

Working on the webxr_ar_paint.html example. 

Successfully working augmented reality drawing application.

I'm trying to integrate this application with webrtc, but I'm struggling with accessing the camera on the webxr side and full screen issues. 

With WebRTC (tokbox) I actually managed to transfer the drawing to the other side. I even send the webxr emulator screen integrated into the chrome browser on the mac pc to the other side (i.e. the empty room).

But when I try this example on a samsung s20 phone, I get a black screen and the other party sees a black screen instead of seeing the camera.
In another example, I open the camera and draw. When I press the X key, it draws on the canvas in the background and then it goes to the other side. The background appears black.

```
// "use strict";
import {animate, three_init} from "./three_init.js";


three_init()
animate()

// console.log(document.querySelector('div'))
let canvasEl = document.querySelector('canvas')
initializeSession(canvasEl.captureStream(25).getVideoTracks()[0]);
```


### Solution


I can see the need to put the 2D drawing and camera data on a canvas and transfer it to the peers via webrtc.
But with the canvas I get in the drawing example, I can't get the drawing and the camera separately or I can't get them as a single canvas.
I am open to any suggestions.


### Alternatives


As an alternative solution, I thought that something else could be put instead of webxr. If I remember correctly, I read that it could be with ARController in older versions of three.js. 

I am also open to alternative solutions.

My English is not very good. Thank you all for your contributions.

author: r-flash

title: GLTFLoader parser.associations refactor
body:
### Description

`<model-viewer>` has two open issues that trace back to errors in `parser.associations`: https://github.com/google/model-viewer/pull/3287#issuecomment-1075505706, and https://github.com/google/model-viewer/issues/4029

The second turns out to be caused by `ReduceAssociations()` being called for multiple scenes, which was introduced here to handle a memory leak: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/22583. I believe these kinds of errors will keep cropping up unless we can simplify the associations.

### Solution

I think a better approach for avoiding memory leaks would be to make the `associations` into a `WeakMap` instead of a `Map`. Then we could remove `ReduceAssociations()` entirely and not worry about having entries for extra clones. This is a breaking change and I'll have to refactor MV a bit for it, but since `associations` isn't documented I think @takahirox is the main other user of it, so I'm mostly looking for his opinion. @donmccurdy, hopefully you'll appreciate the simplification here.

### Alternatives

Just fix these bugs in place, but I fear the code will get even more convoluted and will continue to be prone to memory leaks. 

### Additional context

_No response_

I suggest to add support for Nodes system to work with JS code similar to GLSL and WGSL. I see multiple ways in which it can be useful:
- It can be useful for doing CPU/GPGPU calculations depending on environment. I.e. you can build a large Nodes program, which then can be executed either by JavaScript, by `WebGPURenderer.compute()`, or `WebGLRenderer.compute()` (I will do a PR adding `WebGLRenderer.compute()` soon).
- It can be used to replicate the removed `SoftwareRenderer` (a CPU-side renderer).

title: MikkTSpaceTangents: Generating tangents for "NormalTangentTest" glTF sample model breaks normals
body:
### Description

When loading the glTF sample [NormalTangentTest](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF-Sample-Models/tree/master/2.0/NormalTangentTest) model and manually generating tangents using the `BufferGeometryUtils.computeMikkTSpaceTangents` function the computed normals change and the model renders incorrectly.

However according to the [spec](https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html) - MikkTSpace tangents should produce the correct result:

> When tangents are not specified, client implementations SHOULD calculate tangents using default [MikkTSpace](https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#mikktspace) algorithms with the specified vertex positions, normals, and texture coordinates associated with the normal texture.

It looks like the `computeMikkTSpaceTangents` function inverts the tangent direction by default here specifically noting alignment for glTF files. However disabling this behavior by passing "false" into the function fixes the issue. Is the tangent inversion correct?

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/982a715fcb7e18a64aade942b761f3dd1dedb9c1/examples/jsm/utils/BufferGeometryUtils.js#L81-L92

Related to https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-gpu-pathtracer/issues/210#issuecomment-1371769273
cc @donmccurdy 

### Reproduction steps

1. Load the sample model
2. Compute tangents using `BufferGeometryUtils.computeMikkTSpaceTangents`
3. Model renders incorrectly

See jsfiddle

author: gkjohnson

title: Octree should allow each leaf to store more than 8 triangles
body:
### Description

The current `Octree` implementation in three.js is often slow and can use up a lot of memory when dealing with geometry containing lots of triangles, especially when some of the triangles are large. `Octree` as a data structure has no problem storing points as each point occupies only one leaf. However, in the case of triangle it can occupy multiple leaves depending on its size. This reduces the efficiency of using `Octree` to store triangles because of the overlaps. A geometry with more than 200k triangles could end up being 100+ times bigger in triangle count in `Octree` due to overlaps, taking more than 20 seconds to build and occupying a few giga bytes of memory, making it practically unusable.

### Solution

The simplest solution is to allow each leaf to store more than 8 triangles. In my test case, I increased the limit from 8 to 32, and it reduced the triangle count in `Octree` by nearly 10 times with no impact on collision detection performance.

### Alternatives

I can't think of any alternative simpler than a static increase in triangle limit.

### Additional context

_No response_

author: andreasplesch

title: Implement direct conversion between quaternions and euler angles in any sequence
body:
### Description

There are two issues with the way Quaternion/Angles conversion are dealt with in three.js:

1. Three.js has two separate methods for the conversion from Euler angles to a quaternion variable:

- A member of the Quaternion class that implements different functions for each non-proper (asymmetric) `order`, like `ZYX`, `ZXY`, etc.
- A function of `Mathutils` that implements different functions for proper (symmetric) sequences, like `ZYZ`, etc.

2. Moreover, the implemented method for converting a quaternion to Euler angles is to first convert it to a rotation Matrix and then to angles, which is not very efficient and only implemented for non-proper sequences.

### Solution

1. For converting Angles to Quaternions, I have two propositions:

- Group everything into `Quaternion.setFromEuler`.
- If it does not make the execution speed higher, then this could all be simply implemented as a multiplication of 3 quaternions constructed from `setFromAxisAngle`.

2. For converting Quaternions to Angles, I propose to implement a whole new method. More info on the **Additional context** section

### Alternatives

I would implement it myself, but I am not a `JavaScript` developer and this would probably be a quick fix for someone familiar with both `JavaScript` and `three.js`.

### Additional context

I recently published an article about a direct formula for the conversion between a quaternion variable to Euler angles in any sequence, which can be read [here](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0276302) (Open Access).

Compared to either having 12 separate formulas (or 24, taking into account both intrinsic and extrinsic rotations) or using the well known quaternion-to-matrix-to-euler method, this has 3 main advantages: 

1. Numerically, it is up to 30 times faster than the previous quaternion-to-matrix-to-euler method (used both originally in `SciPy` and in `transforms3d`, for example).
2. It is a lot simpler to implement, debug and maintain than both methods.
3. It provides simple formulas that can be used for theorerical work.

Because of points 1 and 2, it has already been merged into [Scipy](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17392). Because point 3, it has also been merged into [SymPy](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/24358).

These Python implementations are (probably) very easily translatable to JavaScript.
author: evbernardes

title: Error when changing WebGLRenderTarget.samples from 0
body:
### Description

If we render onto a render target with its `samples` property set to 0 and then change the property to anything non-zero, subsequent rendering fails with the error message `TypeError: WeakMap key must be an object, got r` (or similar, telling us that the key - variable `framebuffer` - is undefined).

If the first render onto the render target happens with non-zero `samples`, we can subsequently switch between zero and non-zero values without triggering the error.


### Reproduction steps

1. Create a new WebGLRenderTarget `rt` and leave the `samples` property at the default 0.
2. Call `renderer.setRenderTarget(rt)` and render.
3. Change the `rt.samples` property to 4.
4. Repeat step 2 and observe the error.


### Code

```js
const scene = new THREE.Scene();
const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera();
const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer();
const renderTarget = new THREE.WebGLRenderTarget();

renderer.setRenderTarget(renderTarget);
renderer.render(scene, camera);

renderTarget.samples = 4;
renderer.setRenderTarget(renderTarget);
renderer.render(scene, camera); // error
```


### Live example

* [jsfiddle-latest-release](https://jsfiddle.net/5c4s3kuz/) (see console output)

title: dynamic cubemap mapping incorrect for transmission > 0 with PBR material
body:
### Description

I am trying to use a dynamic cubemap with a PBR material which has also transmission > 0. When I combine the [transmission example](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=tran#webgl_materials_physical_transmission) with the [dynamic cubemap example](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=cubema#webgl_materials_cubemap_dynamic)

here

[transmission_plus_cubemap - CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/transmission-plus-cubemap-k80cnd)

it does not quite work out. There is dynamic reflection shown on the transmissive sphere but the texture mapping is quite off.

Interestingly, for zero transmission the shading mode changes and the dynamic cubemap is shown correctly.

This may hint that only minor changes may be needed, either to how the cubemap is used or perhaps the texture mapping application.

Please also see the forum thread at https://discourse.threejs.org/t/pbr-transmission-with-dynamic-cubemaps-incorrect/46789 which kindly suggested to file this issue in addition.

### Reproduction steps

1. compose a PBR material with transmission > 0 and envmap set to a cubemap texture
2. render
3. observe incorrect mapping of envmap


### Code

```js

const material = new THREE.MeshPhysicalMaterial({
          color: params.color,
          metalness: params.metalness,
          roughness: params.roughness,
          ior: params.ior,
          alphaMap: texture,
          //envMap: hdrEquirect,
          envMap: cubeRenderTarget.texture,
          //envMap: pmremTarget.texture,
          envMapIntensity: params.envMapIntensity,
          transmission: 1, // params.transmission, // use material.transmission for glass materials
          specularIntensity: params.specularIntensity,
          specularColor: params.specularColor,
          opacity: params.opacity,
          side: THREE.DoubleSide,
          transparent: true
        });


```


### Live example

* [transmission_plus_cubemap - CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/transmission-plus-cubemap-k80cnd)

### Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6171115/212347015-94250070-be62-4cde-a982-a2f958d5d552.png)

The dynamic cubemap used as envmap is circled. It should not be restricted to the small square area but should cover the complete, striped sphere. Setting transmission to 0 shows the correct mapping for the cubemap. Perhaps the cubemap is using the same UVs as the texture internally used for the transmission if there is transmission ?

author: andreasplesch

title: Add a VertexNormals check to Mesh()
body:
### Description

I think I now have my understanding correct: Re: Mesh(geometry, material)

o) If the geometry is BufferGeometry then the VertexNormals are not initially calculated.
o) If the material is MeshNormalMaterial with flatShading:false (default) then the VertexNormals are needed.
o) With this combination, Mesh(geometry, material) will not result in a visible object.
I just went through finding why my object was "non-visible".

### Solution

My suggestion is: 
to add a step to Mesh() which would check if VertexNormals are needed and not present, and if so, to call .calculateVertexNormals().

### Alternatives

Add a note in the BufferGeometry doc that says to call .calculateVertexNormals() if you don't have flatShading:true.

### Additional context

Happy new year -- keep up the good work!

author: hybridherbst

title: GroundProjectedEnv naming is confusing as it's a background, not an environment
body:
### Description

Since #24311 there is a way to make a ground projected environment - which works great! 

But the naming is now somewhat confusing:
- in three, generally "background" is something visible behind the scene and "environment" is used for e.g. IBL and reflections
- see the docs: https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/scenes/Scene.environment vs. Scene.background
- now, "GroundProjectedEnv" has nothing to do with some PMREM/IBL environment - it should probably be a GroundProjectedBackground and not Env (?)
- in the sample, it does use the environment and not the background map, which adds to the confusion when someone actually uses a separate background and environment cubemap 

### Version
latest
author: hybridherbst

title: SkeletonUtils retargetClip is always off by 1 frame.
body:
## [UPDATE] See my new comment below for an improved example.

### Description

Any AnimationClip I use in the `SkeletonUtils` `retargetClip` function will result in an output clip with a shorter duration, that is missing exactly 1 frame.
The difference is hardly visible in animations with hundreds or thousands of frames, but it is still incorrect.

The issue seems to come from the [numFrames calculation](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/996f6c9f5f606d3193b3c5545b88de7eb09cbb55/examples/jsm/utils/SkeletonUtils.js#L214).
`numFrames = Math.round(clip.duration * ( options.fps / 1000 ) * 1000 )`

#### Theoretical example
Lets take a clip with a duration of 3 seconds and 1 fps as example.
- **Input:** frame times: `0 1 2 3`, duration: 3 seconds

`numFrames` is calculated as `3`, which is used in a `< numFrames` for-loop.
- **Output:** frames times: `0 1 2`, duration: 2 seconds

#### Solutions
- If we can assume that any AnimationClip input will start at time 0, we can just add a `+ 1` to the numFrames definition.
(This would get an index out of range for a clip with times `1 2 3`, but i'm not sure that is possible)

- An alternative is to use `numFrames = clip.tracks[0].times.length` 
The function already seems to assume all tracks are the same length.

#### Testing
There don't seem to be any examples or tests that use this function.


### Reproduction steps

1. Load an animationClip and create a skeletonhelper.
2. Make an options object that includes an `fps` property that matches the framerate of the animation.
3. Call `retargetClip` using the variables above as input.
4. The output of retargetClip does not match the duration of the loaded clip.


### Code

```js
  // Any use of the retargetClip function, where the options fps is set.
  // This excerpt is from within the scope of a bvhloader callback, see the live example

  let options = {
    fps: 1 / result.clip.tracks[0].times[1]
  };

  console.log("original duration: " + result.clip.duration);
  console.log("original track0 frames: " + result.clip.tracks[0].times.length);

  let newClip = retargetClip(skeletonHelper, skeletonHelper, result.clip, options);

  console.log("retarget duration: " + newClip.duration);
  console.log("retarget track0 frames: " + newClip.tracks[0].times.length);
```


### Live example

This is basically the bvhLoader example pasted in jsfiddle, with the above code block pasted into the loader callback.
The use of the retarget does not make sense here, as it doesn't load any other model, but this is enough to show the bug.

https://jsfiddle.net/sn58dp40/

Expected console output for the example:
```
"original duration: 4.924997806549072"
"original track0 frames: 592"
"retarget duration: 4.9166646003723145"
"retarget track0 frames: 591"
```

### Version

r148, dev

### Device

Any

### Browser

Any

### OS

Any
