title: Can't find variable: HTMLElement
body:
### Description

I've stumbled into a strange issue, using Three in a Expo (React Native) project causes `HTMLElement` to become undefined.

I don't use of `HTMLElement` since I'm targeting mobile only, but some packages that I'm using do and as soon I'm starting to use Three I'm getting this error:
```
ReferenceError: Can't find variable: HTMLElement
```

For example some packages might run this code `ref instanceof HTMLElement` which normally works fine in a Expo (React Native) project, until you start using Three.

I realize that is not the place to talk about Expo / React Native support, but since the issue is not about some rendering problem with React Native but rather with Three seemingly modifying the global context (`HTMLElement` becoming undefined), I'm hoping that people familiar with Three source code will be able to help me understand what's happening.

Any help would be greatly appreciated. thank you!

### Reproduction steps

1. Create a new Expo project: `npx create-expo-app myapp`
2. Add Three: `npm i three@0.145.0 expo-three expo-gl`
3. Modify App.jsx (or .tsx) to import Three and make a call like `new THREE.Scene();`
4. Run with ExpoGo: `npm start`

=> The app throws an error before it has a chance to render the UI: `Can't find variable: HTMLElement`

Note: the same issue happens with r148

### Code

```js
import { StatusBar } from 'expo-status-bar';
import { StyleSheet, Text, View } from 'react-native';
import * as THREE from 'three';
import { useEffect } from 'react';

export default function App() {
  useEffect(() => {
    new THREE.Scene(); // Comment this line, remove the import and it works!
    console.log(HTMLElement); // Throws "ReferenceError: Can't find variable: HTMLElement"
  });
  return (
    <View style={styles.container}>
      <Text>Open up App.jsx to start working on your app!</Text>
      <StatusBar style="auto" />
    </View>
  );
}

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  container: {
    flex: 1,
    backgroundColor: '#fff',
    alignItems: 'center',
    justifyContent: 'center',
  },
});
```

Note:
`import * as THREE from 'three';` may be replaced by `import { THREE } from 'expo-three';` but the issue will persist.

### Live example

Unfortunately Expo Snack doesn't work with Three so I can't provide a live example.

### Screenshots

_No response_

r145, r148

Mobile

### Browser

Safari

### OS

Android, iOS

author: timmevandermeer

title: GLTFExporter: Missing required padding on integer vertex attributes
body:
### Description

The glTF 2.0 standard states that:

> For performance and compatibility reasons, each element of a vertex attribute MUST be aligned to 4-byte boundaries inside a bufferView (i.e., accessor.byteOffset and bufferView.byteStride MUST be multiples of 4).

However, three.js - under some circumstances - creates glTF files containing a `byteStride` that is not a multiple of 4.

This happens because of the [following line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/03349e98aa1f57a97433c0a3f17697594fdc0997/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter.js#L930):

```
bufferViewDef.byteStride = attribute.itemSize * componentSize;
```

For example, you might decide to use single-byte integers for UV coordinates. So each item might be an unsigned byte (and thus have an `itemSize` of 1) and the `componentSize` be 2, leading to an invalid `byteStride` of 1*2 = 2.

### Reproduction steps

1. Generate an invalid glTF using the example code/jsfiddle below
2. Upload it to the [glTF validator](https://github.khronos.org/glTF-Validator/) to see how it is broken


### Code

```js
const geom = new THREE.BufferGeometry()
geom.setAttribute(
  'position',
  new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Float32Array([0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0]), 3)
)
geom.setAttribute('uv', new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Uint8Array([0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1]), 2))

const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(geom)

const exporter = new GLTFExporter()
exporter.parse(
  mesh,
  data => {
    const file = new File([JSON.stringify(data)], "mesh.gltf")
    const a = document.createElement('a')
    a.href = URL.createObjectURL(file)
    a.download = file.name
    document.body.appendChild(a)
    a.click()
  },
  err => {
  },
  { binary: false }
)
```


### Live example

* [jsfiddle-latest-release - example gltf creation](https://jsfiddle.net/4nkagz1p/)


### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r148

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Firefox

### OS

MacOS

title: `GLTFExporter` is missing support for attributes with `BYTE` and `SHORT` items
body:
### Description

If you try to run the `GLTFExporter` on a mesh whose geometry contains `Int8Array`s or `Int16Array`s, it will crash with the error `THREE.GLTFExporter: Unsupported bufferAttribute component type`.

This doesn't seem too hard to fix - just a few missing `if` statements (I'll submit a PR soon). (Although it will be very rare that an `Int8Array` actually gets exported to a valid glTF file anyway until #25309 is fixed)

### Reproduction steps

1. Create a mesh whose geometry contains an attribute of type `Int8Array` or `Int16Array`
2. Export that mesh with the `GLTFExporter`
3. Enjoy failure

### Code

```js
const geom = new THREE.BufferGeometry()
geom.setAttribute(
  'position',
  new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Float32Array([0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0]), 3)
)
geom.setAttribute('uv', new THREE.BufferAttribute(new Int8Array([0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1]), 2))

const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(geom)

const exporter = new GLTFExporter()
exporter.parse(
  mesh,
  data => {
   console.log('success')
  },
  err => {
   console.log('error', JSON.stringify(err))
  },
  { binary: false }
)
```


### Live example

title: SVGLoader: Shape generation breaks with large stroke width.
body:
### Description

The original issue was reported at the forum: https://discourse.threejs.org/t/glitches-with-svg-loader/46803

SVGs that exceed a certain `stroke-width` are not rendered correctly. As visible in the below screenshot, the resulting shape is rendered with additional triangles on its back side. When decreasing the stroke width (in the below SVG try 200), the issue goes away.

 I suspect there is a problem in [SVGLoader.pointsToStrokeWithBuffers()](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/4a6cf23c5f69ecde00e79171be7235deb39dff79/examples/jsm/loaders/SVGLoader.js#L2412) which generates triangles based on the stroke definition from the SVG.

### Reproduction steps

1. Load the following SVG with the loader to reproduce the glitch.
```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 792" width="600" height="792">
  <path stroke="#64C36E" fill="none" id="link_2_21_ok" d="M30,463.61904761904765C150,463.61904761904765,150,571.0476190476189,270,571.0476190476189" stroke-width="290" stroke-opacity="0.6"/>
</svg>
```

author: Samsy

title: Better 3D object manipulation and even better rollercoaster sample
body:
### Description

The camera and roller coaster in the current [rollercoaster sample](https://threejs.org/examples/?q=coaster#webxr_vr_rollercoaster) does not bank in curves, and thus the sample does not fully demonstrate best practices for orienting an object to follow a curve.

### Solution

I suggest:
a) Implement GetTangent[At], GetNormal[At], and GetBinormal[At] in a way that (when possible) obtains the values from the curve analytically for maximum accuracy (as opposed to using points a short distance further along the curve).
b) Implement a GetFrenetFrame[At] if this will compute and return all three vectors more efficiently.
c) Implement the rollercoaster sample so that the coaster banks as it travels around the curves. Ideally this should do the math to figure out the direction of the local acceleration (that is, gravity plus centripetal), and orient the camera and coaster so that left is aligned with (local acceleration \cross forward vector).
d) If it doesn't already exist, then adding an intuitive function (like "lookAt") that takes in two vectors such as "forwardDirection" and "upDirection" would be helpful. An object that uses the method should end up being fully oriented in the desired direction. For example, setOrientation(tangetToCurve, -normalToCurve) would be perhaps be appropriate for orienting the coaster in a valley.

### Alternatives

I've looked at extrude geometry and observed its use of Frenet frames. But, this code does too much brute math on the various vectors. A more intuitive-to-use method that will automatically orienting things the way you want will be very helpful. It's true that if you are a wizard with Quaternions, you can usually figure out how to get the job done. But 3D manipulation by using quaternions is not intuitive enough, and thus this approach increases development and debug time.

### Additional context
Excellent background information...
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jvPPXbo87ds&ab_channel=FreyaHolm%C3%A9r
_No response_

title: GLTFExporter is always exporting the iridescence extension and never IOR
body: ### Description Importing a valid glTF with the ior extension (renders just fine) and then exporting it results in a glTF without the ior extension and with the iridescence extension (??). It no longer renders fine when imported again: This appears to be because 1) the exporter doesn't support the IOR extension like the importer does, and 2) the iridescence extension is apparently used for *all* `MeshPhysicalMaterial`s. I'll post a PR soon to fix these. Hoping I can sneak into r149. Edit: ah, I see now that the export extensions have not at all kept up with the import extensions. Okay, we'll see how much I can get done quickly... ### Reproduction steps 1. Go to https://modelviewer.dev/editor/ 2. Select the Water Bottles 3. Note the conversion from Spec Gloss has worked (click the "Converted" button to see the extensions include specular and ior). 4. Click "Download Scene" 5. title: CCD IK does not honor bones (joints) rotation limits
body:
### Description

Setting limits for the joints for the CCD IK solver configuration does not seem to have effect.




The first link seems the one affected.

### Reproduction steps

1.Set the limits using the rotationMIN and rotationMAX inside the array passed to the CCD IK solver.
2.The joints do not move in the desired range, some of the them rotate only from 0 to PI / 2 all the time.
3.The first link seems the one affected.


### Code

```js
  const iks = [
    {
      target: 5, // "target"
      effector: 4, // "bone3"
      links: [{ index: 3, limitation: new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 0) },  //Fixed to troubleshoot.  
      { index: 2, limitation: new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, 1) },
      { index: 1, limitation: new THREE.Vector3(0, 1, 0), rotationMax: new THREE.Vector3(0, Math.PI / 2, 0), rotationMin: new THREE.Vector3(0, -Math.PI / 2, 0) }
      ], // "bone2", "bone1", "bone0"
      iteration: 0.5
    }
  ];
  ikSolver = new THREE.CCDIKSolver(mesh, iks);
```


### Live example

* [THREEJS Simple Robot arm with bones  -Example on Github-](https://github.com/JorgeVilchis/ThreeJSBones.git)

To run , just clone it and use local server (e.g.):

`python3 -m http.server`

Then use URL:    http://0.0.0.0:8000/home.html

Code is all in Main.js

### Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7463640/215020566-7db3d9b3-e302-4d1f-b5b3-2c3e9f749d62.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7463640/215020647-4738f0e1-2cd2-4d09-be6d-cbaf0d8bee00.png)

The issue is that the fist joint , for this example, is specified tom move in this range:
rotationMax: new THREE.Vector3(0, Math.PI / 2, 0),   rotationMin: new THREE.Vector3(0, -Math.PI / 2, 0) 

which in total should provide 180 degrees of freedom, but the joint (bone) moves only 90 degrees in on quadrant , then it moves 90 degrees in an opposite quadrant.

title: BufferGeometry.copy(BoxGeometry) error when exporting toJSON
body:
### Description

as suggested by @donmccurdy i'll try to desicribe the issue i experienced from [this](https://discourse.threejs.org/t/object3d-tojson-not-saving-updated-position-attributes/47312/6) post as best possible. 

after creating a simple `BoxGeometry`, modifying its `attributes.position.array` and using `new BufferGeometry().copy(BoxGeometry)` to copy the original geometry i got the following console error when then using `.toJSON` and trying to parse the JSON with `ObjectLoader()`...
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50278392/215095177-49f462aa-9de3-4cfb-8131-d3d03d2b845c.png)

i found that this process will work and the error does not exist after running `delete BufferGeometry.parameters`

it's quite an odd error to try to explain but you can see the attached jsFiddle as a live demonstration of the issue


### Reproduction steps

1. create a new `BoxGeometry(16, 1, 1)`
2. modify `geometry.attributes.position.array`
3. create a new `BufferGeometry()` and `.copy()` the modified BoxGeometry... `new BufferGeometry().copy(BoxGeometry)`
4. run `Group.toJSON()`
5. try to parse the resulting JSON object with `ObjectLoader()`


### Code

```
  originalGeometry = new THREE.BoxGeometry( 30, 3, 3, 30, 1, 1 )

  let box = new THREE.Mesh( originalGeometry, mat )
  
  group.add( box )
  scene.add( group )
  
  modifyGeometry( originalGeometry )
  originalGeometry.center()
  
  let newBufferGeometry = new THREE.BufferGeometry().copy( originalGeometry )
  //delete newBufferGeometry.parameters  // uncommenting this line resolves the error as a workaround
  
  originalGeometry.dispose()
  box.geometry = newBufferGeometry
  
  let jsonOutput = group.toJSON()
  
  scene.remove(group)
  
  let loader = new THREE.ObjectLoader();
  loader.parse(jsonOutput, function( object ) {
  
    scene.add( object )
    
  })
```


### Live example

https://jsfiddle.net/forerun/hdtz2uc9/7/

### Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50278392/215104482-54009ec7-1c6d-4281-8188-36bae789d5d6.png)


### Version

title: Rendering to WebGL3DRenderTarget in Chrome with D3D11 backend does not work correctly
body: ### Description When rendering to a 3D Render Target layer it does not work on Windows 10, Chrome specifically when using the default D3D11 backend with ANGLE. Toggling the `chrome://flags#use-angle` to `OpenGL` fixes the issue. When using D3D11 it actually corrupts the chrome dev UI, as well, and renders to a wrong part of the canvas. This seems to be a browser issue pretty cut and dry but I thought I'd submit here to see if I'm missing a step in rendering to the target layer. This is causing issues rendering to a 3d texture for sdf generation in [this three-mesh-bvh issue](https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-mesh-bvh/issues/497). An aside - the D3D11 backend seems to have a variety of issues (see https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-gpu-pathtracer/issues/325#issuecomment-1407232890). I'm sure there's a good reason but I'm naively wondering why the OpenGL backend isn't the default behavior? ### Reproduction steps 1. Render to a WebGL3DRenderTarget layer 2. Render the layer to screen 3. Layer does not render ### Code See example ### Live example Live example of the modified version of the "webgl2_rendertarget_texture2darray" page: [live page](https://raw.githack.com/gkjohnson/three.js/3d-render-target-repro/examples/webgl2_rendertarget_texture2darray.html) And here's a diff of the minimal changes to the example page: [diff](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/compare/36a8629...gkjohnson:three.js:3d-render-target-repro). ### Screenshots Chrome screenshot using default ANGLE D3D11 backend which actually corrupts the Chrome UI, as well: Using ANGLE OpenGL backend: ### Version dev ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS Windows

title: IFC code is almost a year old

### Description

The IFC files (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/tree/dev/examples/jsm/loaders/ifc) are from Feb 18th and since then there have been many updates 

For a while I downloaded the web-ifc and IFCLoader files and updated the imports myself. But with every update of Three.js and/or IFC.js this would break. Therefore I chose just to rely on the Three.js repository for the loader and all other IFC dependencies.

Here is mentioned that the IFC dependencies will probably move outside of this repository (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25057/files#r1037975491), but I can't see any changes just yet.

Is it possible to update the IFC dependencies for now, while we wait for the new way of importing dependencies for "external" examples?

For me it would be better if there was a stand-alone IFCLoader package on npm, with all the correct dependencies linked. Is that something has been considered before?

title: Docs: Add at least "In construction/WIP" pages for addons that are not documented to make these features more obvious
body:
### Description

I often miss great features that are present in the examples (the latest for me is NURBS) and are not: 
1) documented (not a problem by itself here) 
2) referenced in the documentation at least on a blank page/under construction that I could be seeing when searching in the search bar of the documentation.

### Solution

I would be in favor of at least referencing the features present in the source code, even if they are not documented for two reasons: 

1) it would allow devs to know that it exists when they browse the docs without needing to have a higher level of curiosity and search the repo everywhere for easter eggs. 
2) it would also allow people to see that the documentation is missing, and to work towards making it available by contributing.

For point number 2, the people who will finally stumble upon the "hidden" feature in question, they will have to search in the source code how to use it, to understand how it works. If individually, we take this step of understanding, it would be great to go further and actually update the doc (that was a blank page) so that it is finally complete.

My point is to make it obvious and visible that we need contribution on this specific features.

### Alternatives

It can be cool also to have some kind of metrics somewhere about the documentation coverage. 

### Additional context

This happened to me in multiple occasions:
- Marching cubes
- NURBS
- Water
- ...

title: Docs: Specify output encoding responsibilities when using ShaderMaterial.
body:
### Description

When using ShaderMaterial, the `outputEncoding` property on the renderer appears to just do nothing. 

The [color management](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=color#manual/en/introduction/Color-management) page is helpful, but it does not mention the behavior or responsibilities when using ShaderMaterial.

It also appears any textures consumed in ShaderMaterial are in linear RGB, irregardless of the `encoding` property. Though I may be misunderstanding how this works.

### Reproduction steps

1. Create a ShaderMaterial, have it output anything that would make color space conversion obvious, such as a gradient.
2. Observe that switching between `sRGBEncoding` and `LinearEncoding` on the renderer does nothing to change the output color for this material.


### Code

This writes the top half of a square with gamma corrected output, bottom half no correction.

```frag
varying vec2 vUv;  
void main() {
  float intensity = vUv.x;
  vec3 gradient = vec3(intensity, 1.0 - intensity, 0.0);
  if (vUv.y > 0.5) {
    gl_FragColor = vec4(pow(gradient.rgb, 1.0 / vec3(2.2)), 1.0);
  } else {
    gl_FragColor = vec4(gradient.rgb, 1.0);
  }
}
```


### Live example

https://jsfiddle.net/b896ukLg/1/

### Screenshots

**GIMP Output:**
TOP - Gradient tool blending in linear RGB
BOTTOM - Gradient tool blending in perceptual RGB

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4075314/216758658-81518992-a7ae-4a10-9ed0-7c2580af1286.png)

**ThreeJS Output**
TOP - Gamma 2.2 conversion in shader for gl_FragColor
BOTTOM - Raw output of color

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4075314/216758684-82461cb3-6afe-4063-9611-a8f6450b4f1c.png)

r149

author: Davidyanlong

title: Make raycasting faster by performing a preliminary check based on the bounding sphere
body:
### Description

We noticed in our project that setting the 'near' and 'far' properties on `Raycaster` doesn't result in a speedup contrary to expectations.  

Many of our meshes are 'far away' and we'd like to shoot only at the close ones.

### Solution

Let `r` be the bounding sphere's radius and `R` the distance from ray origin to bounding sphere's center.
An intersection can only occur if the intervals `[near, far]` and `[R - r, R + r]` overlap.

From reading the code and the behaviour of our tests it seems to me that this test is not being performed even though it should be simple and fast.  The bounding sphere is computed anyway:  https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/src/objects/Mesh.js#L161

### Alternatives

Perform the test only optionally if performance is a concern.

### Additional context

We're experimenting with BVHs also but would like to Keep it Simple and Straightforward.

title: OrbitControls stays in incorrect state after pointercancel event
body:
### Description

Opening a page with OrbitControls on an iPad can result in several errors when moving the scene with two fingers closely pinched together. 

Probably it's the same in any case where `pointercancel` would be fired, but this is the scenario which we can consistently reproduce on multiple devices. Surface tables or Android phones don't fire this event (in this case at least).

### Reproduction steps

1. Open a page with OrbitControls (even [the official example](https://threejs.org/docs/#examples/en/controls/OrbitControls) works) on an iPad (tested on multiple devices, the latest is iOS 16.3 Safari)
2. Add a global error listener (`window.addEventListener('error', e=>{...})`)
3. Start panning the scene with two fingers pinched closely together

The error handler will fire several times.


### Code

The error triggered regardless of user code.

As far as I could tell, the problem is that in `OrbitControls.js`, in `onPointerCancel`, the pointer is removed, but `state` is not changed. So if we were in the middle of a panning or zooming gesture for example (`state = TOUCH_DOLLY_PAN`), the next call to `onPointerMove` will eventually end up in `getSecondPointerPosition`, which will throw when trying to read the position of the nonexistent second pointer.

### Live example

* [jsfiddle-dev](https://jsfiddle.net/0s5f6gqa/)


### Screenshots

_No response_

r149

### Device

Mobile

### Browser

Safari

title: ImageLoader fails in Brave due to optimization path
body:
### Description

We've noticed that in Brave, ImageLoader doesn't emit load/error events for some Image elements, even though the network tab shows the image fully loaded and complete.

ThreeJS creates Image elements in a private only scope, unreferenced anywhere else until after the image is loaded.
I suspect that Brave sees this as an opportunity to optimize the users experience since the Image is not referenced anywhere or in the DOM.

Any attempt to debug this issue, eg console logging or storing the Image in global scope immediately fixes the issue.

I'm not sure if this is something we should fix in ThreeJS, but wanted to make sure people find this if they run into similar issues.

I've tweeted Brave about this here: https://twitter.com/hyperfy_io/status/1625479090471059457

### Reproduction steps

Reproduction is not so clear as Brave can choose to "optimize" away an image based on a variety of criteria unknown to us.

We see this in many of our Hyperfy worlds, most recently this one: https://hyperfy.io/love which contains a lot of images. Which image gets evicted is seemingly random.

You can tell when Brave evicts an image when the loading bar never reaches 100% as it gets stuck trying to load an image.

// code goes here
```

author: Kliffh

title: Nodes "chaining" format can cause some problems
body:
### Description

Related issues: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25074 https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25240#issuecomment-1418157810 https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25498

The new nodes chaining format can cause some problems, which aren't very severe but still exist:
- Doesn't allow autocompletion (an IDE doesn't know there exists ShaderNodeObject.add() or ShadedNodeObject.sub() method)
- It can break type checking (types also don't know there exist such methods)
- It can break if a needed file is not imported before the one using. This is solvable in the internal Nodes system (just by exporting some files first) and isn't really a problem in "external" Nodes system -- i.e., for user. But it can break if a compiler/minifier decide to tree-shake a file.

The first two problems can be solved by 1) not adding very much node methods to node chaning and 2) adding such node methods to the TS types of ShaderNodeObject -- but this only resolves these problems for built-in nodes (such that their types can be added to TS) and not user-added nodes.
Possibly, then, user must create a new class that node-chaining user-defined-methods will be added to (user also manages TS types for it) -- so possibly we should add another argument to addNodeElement -- the class that a node must extend so that it can use passed method.

### Solution

This is just an issue for discussing these problems.

### Alternatives

N/A

### Additional context

/ping @sunag @epreston @three-types

title: WebGL Error when using UniformsUtils.merge to merge a WebGLCubeRenderTarget texture
body:
### Description

When using a WebGLCubeRenderTarget texture as an input to a shader, and then passing that through UniformsUtils.merge, you get the following error:

THREE.WebGLState: TypeError: Failed to execute 'texSubImage2D' on 'WebGL2RenderingContext': Overload resolution failed.
    at e.executeFunction (<anonymous>:3:467593)
    at e.executeOriginFunction (<anonymous>:3:467224)
    at WebGL2RenderingContext.texSubImage2D (<anonymous>:3:499508)
    at Object.texSubImage2D (three.module.js?module:formatted:22863:30)
    at uploadCubeTexture (three.module.js?module:formatted:24452:35)
    at WebGLTextures.setTextureCube (three.module.js?module:formatted:23693:13)
    at SingleUniform.setValueT6 [as setValue] (three.module.js?module:formatted:18496:14)
    at WebGLUniforms.upload (three.module.js?module:formatted:19113:19)
    at setProgram (three.module.js?module:formatted:29632:27)
    at WebGLRenderer.renderBufferDirect (three.module.js?module:formatted:28539:25)


From what I can see, UniformUtils.merge clones and does a copy() on the CubeTexture, which flags needsUpdate, which I'm guessing leads to a version increment and eventually the uploadCubeTexture path. Don't think this is an issue with plain WebGLRenderTarget textures though.


### Reproduction steps

1. Create a WebGLCubeRenderTarget
2. Assign the texture to a uniform
3. Use UniformUtils.merge to merge this with anything, even an empty dict {}



### Code

```js
// Goes something like this, check the jsfiddle for full source

const cubeRT = new THREE.WebGLCubeRenderTarget(128);

const brokenUniforms = THREE.UniformsUtils.merge([
    {},
    { cubemap: { value: cubeRT .texture }}]);

```


### Live example

* https://jsfiddle.net/SimonDevYT/ga69wLuy/

author: simondevyoutube

title: USDZExporter - Increase texture max size
body: ### Description When exporting to a USDZ file, the provided texture will be clamped to 1024px (see [ln 132-136](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/29e9f905ff829a6b886ac9c232eb3aee6fab4f8b/examples/jsm/exporters/USDZExporter.js#L132)). **I would like to increase the max-size to 2048px instead, per Apple's recommendation ([source 1](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2018/603/?time=2166), [source 2](https://help.sketchfab.com/hc/en-us/articles/360046421631-glTF-GLB-and-USDZ)).** With the current limitation, my texture gets too blurry when exporting to `usdz`. My use case is displaying a big print (70 x 100 cm) using AR, and with a max-size of 1024px, the resolution is not enough. See the comparison below: Both models are zoomed in to showcase the texture (a use case when displaying the model up close). To the left is the model exported using THREE.js UsdzExporter, to the right is the model exported with Vectary. Both models have the same texture source. As you can see, the texture on the left is much blurrier due to the downscaling. ### Solution Change the max size for the texture when exporting to `usdz`. Change [ln 132](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/exporters/USDZExporter.js#L132) and use `2048` instead of `1024`. This would follow Apple's recommendation and allow users to export high res textures. ### Alternatives I've considered splitting the single plane into 4 planes and the texture into 4 images, but this is just a workaround. I prefer changing the max-size. ### Additional context _No response_

title: Build Size grows 5x with ThreeJs 0.150.0
body:
### Description

I installed **threejs** in a vite project ( **vite ^4.0.0**). 
The threejs version was 0.150.0, the build size was 500kb. 
After changing the the three js version to 0.148.0 the build size reduced to 100kb.

There is a huge difference in build size between these threejs versions. Whats causing this issue?

### Reproduction steps
1. Create a vanilla typescript vite project
2. Install threejs v ^0.150.0 and import it
3. run vite build 
4. remove threejs v ^0.150.0
5. Install threejs v ^0.148.0 and import it
6. run vite build

scope.object.zoom /= factor;

scope.object.zoom = MathUtils.clamp(scope.object.zoom / factor, scope.minDistance, scope.maxDistance);

author: agargaro

title: Cannot override methods when extending WebGLRenderer
body:
### Description



In my case, I have a `CustomRenderer` class that extends `WebGLRenderer`. I want to dispose of other things in the `dispose()` call, so I created an override method that disposes of my resources and also calls `super.dispose()`.

However, in WebGLRenderer, the method doesn't ever get called, and instead the `WebGLRenderer.dispose()` is called directly.

```js
class CustomRenderer extends WebGLRenderer {
  dispose() {
    super.dispose()
    // the code here is never run..
  }
}

const renderer = new CustomRenderer()
renderer.dispose()
```

This is because the `WebGLRenderer.dispose()`, is not a class method per se.


### Reproduction steps

1. Create a class that extends `WebGLRenderer`
2. Create a method that overrides a `WebGLRenderer` method
3. Instance the class and call the method


### Code

```js
import { WebGLRenderer } from 'three';

class CustomRenderer extends WebGLRenderer {
  dispose() {
    super.dispose()
    
    // custom code goes here...
    console.log('1. I should be logged to the console')
  }
}

const renderer = new CustomRenderer()
renderer.dispose()

console.log('2. The log n. 1 should be present above this ^')
```


### Live example

* [jsfiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/8o9hqef7/7/)


### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r150

title: Better Canvas handling for grabbing the canvas contents outside of three.js
body:
### Description

I'm working on [Looking Glass' WebXR Library](https://github.com/Looking-Glass/looking-glass-webxr) which is an Engine Agnostic platform designed to add support for Looking Glass devices to a variety of webgl based frameworks, like three, babylon, playcanvas, vtk etc. 

One of the issues I've run into with three.js lately is that it is rather difficult to capture the main framebuffer of the canvas for things like screenshots while running outside of the main animation loop. 

I've looked for some solutions however they all reference the three.js renderer, directly. I'd like to build a method that allows me to take a screenshot of the main canvas, without having to reference three's internal renderer class. 

My current screenshot method in this branch [here](https://github.com/Looking-Glass/looking-glass-webxr/blob/fbdba5d64ec3606af35d71968bf3f2c89fc5c42e/src/LookingGlassMediaController.ts#L15-L29): works in Babylon.JS currently, but not in three. 

I was wondering if it'd be possible to either revisit the API for this to make it easier to capture the canvas' contents from outside of three.js, or instead, if there's a way I can override this with the base webgl objects. 

I've tried using `gl.readpixels()` but that gives the same results as the more common `Blob` and `ToDataURL()` methods. 

Would appreciate any advice/feedback here! Thanks! 🙏🏻 

### Solution

I'd like to be able to capture the canvas outside of the main three.js render loop. 

### Alternatives

I've tried using the following methods: 

Canvas.toblob

Canvas.toDataURL

gl.readpixels()

All of these return empty images. 

What is a bit funny, is that I *can* just right click the main canvas and click "save image" but I'd like to be able to do that programmatically. 

### Additional context

_No response_

author: haeric

title: Bounding box transform controls
body:
### Description

Three.js has `TransformControls` (similar to Blender), which has 3 modes: `translate`, `rotate` and `scale`. And the problems are that:

* it's not possible to enable more than one or all modes at the same time
* it's not comfortable and intuitive for end users

Live demo: https://threejs.org/examples/#misc_controls_transform.

### Solution

Bounding box transform controls provide manipulation affordances for translate, rotation and scale, all at once, without the need to switch between modes.

![babylonjs](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7892779/222916384-24695447-ba5a-4936-801d-72f201b42668.png)

Here's the video how it works:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7892779/222917713-316209eb-7489-4b7d-bd7d-8ecccce41601.mp4

### Alternatives

Babylon.js provides bounding box gizmo "out-of-the-box".

Live demo: https://playground.babylonjs.com/#8GY6J8#211

Mixed Reality Toolkit (MRTK) for Unity has bounding box gizmo. Unity projects can be exported for WebXR.

Live demo: https://rufus31415.github.io/sandbox/simple-webxr-mrtk/

### Additional context

This feature is really needed for the [new WebXR mode for Meta Quest devices](https://github.com/google/model-viewer/discussions/3969) in the Google's [`<model-viewer>`](https://modelviewer.dev) web component, that is based on Three.js.

title: Docs: Spanish version
body:
## Description

Hello, everyone. Thank you for this fantastic project and your hard work in maintaining it.

I'm in love with Three.js and I'd like to contribute to the project by translating the documentation into Spanish (I've worked on a similar project translating Apple's book _The Swift Programming Language_ into Spanish as well — [swiftbook.es](https://swiftbook.es)). Is it okay to use this issue as a source to track the progress of the translation and anything related to it?

## UPDATE:

This issue will serve as the source of truth to track the progress of the translation — to know who's working on a given section and any other related aspect.

Please feel free to ask for a section so I can update this to mark it as taken. I also suggest you submit any inquiry or doubt through the [Discord channel](https://discord.gg/E2HBJCVB) so we can reach a consensus on the translation of a word, phrase, or grammatical/syntactical construct.

Also, take care of your spelling, it is important that the translation presents good spelling both in accents and in well-written words.

Finally, try to keep a _neutral_ Spanish (assuming such a thing exists 😅), and avoid the use of words that are highly colloquial or exclusive to your region or country, so we can maintain the documentation with a neutral language.

## Translation Progress

To translate a section:

- Check that no one else has claimed the section of your choice in the checklist and comments below. Do not create new issues claiming new work please, let's keep everything here. If you already have an issue opened, please close it after your PR gets merged 🎉. Thanks!

- Comment below with the name of the section you would like to translate. Take only one section at a time.

- Submit a PR, assign it to @mprcodes, and add a link to it to your comment!

### Status Indicators

* ✍🏽 – Translation In Progress
* 👀 – Under Review
* 🎉 – Done

author: steve-flood

title: TransformControls attach BoundingBox or Position Offset
body:
### Description

Some Object3Ds in our environment have origins that are outside the bounds of the mesh. Take, for example, this sphere (mesh) inscribed in a cube (bounding box):
![material-fefnqhga](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32803978/223805933-14c4952d-efb8-4d06-97c6-d15653eabcd4.jpg)
Say I perform TransformControls.attach() to this Object3D, where the origin of Object3D is at point A. The TransformControls renders at point A, the origin of the Object3D, as expected. 

Now take the case where the user has zoomed in (we use OrthographicCamera) and the model is now larger inside the canvas.
![material-fefnqhga (2)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32803978/223806794-65018ac4-eb7d-4047-b03f-e24041e15d1b.jpg)
In this case, as you can see, point A is now outside the field of view of the camera. When a TransformControls.attach() is performed, the gizmo renders outside the field of view of the camera. 

This behavior, although working as expected, has led to complaints from our end users that the TransformControls are not attaching, or they are but requires searching around for them.

Unfortunately, in our use case, the origin of the model cannot be modified to be in the center of the Object3D.

### Solution

An option added to the TransformControls.attach() method that renders the gizmo in the center of the bounding box of the mesh as opposed to the origin of the Object3D.

### Alternatives

The ability to perform a TransformControls attachment point offset. This is a more generalized solution that would also allow for programs to place TransformControls anywhere in scene

- The above would solve our issue by performing a bounding box calculation before attaching TransformControls, calculating the required offset from the Object3D's origin to the bounding box center and apply said offset to the TransformControls

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Renderer context moved to iframe causing isWebGL2 to fail
body: ### Description I'm seeing a very similar issue to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/24634 in iframe contexts and thought I'd raise it again for consideration. I have a WordPress plugin that lets you build Three.js scenes in the wp post editor. There was a recent change to WordPress where the post editor is now wrapped in an iframe. This is causing behavior very similar to the issue linked above where the webgl context breaks and cant render anything with non-basic materials or textures. This change will likely not be reverted so I need to figure out a workaround to keep this plugin alive. I've been documenting my progress debugging this in the WordPress issue: https://github.com/WordPress/gutenberg/issues/47983 Before iframe wrapper: After iframe wrapper: I've attempted to define webgl2 explicitly in my Canvas component (using r3f) but it doesnt seem to stick when the context is moved to inside an iframe. I've documented this issue since I've been trying to solve it within the WordPress repository but I fear there isn't a fix I can implement on that side of things. My plugin code lives here for more context: https://github.com/antpb/three-object-viewer If you are familiar or have a WordPress handy you can reproduce this on a site by installing the Gutenberg plugin and the Three Object Viewer plugin together. The Gutenberg plugin is the development version of the WordPress editor so it has all the new and shiny things like this iframe change. ### Reproduction steps 1. If you have a WordPress site log in and go to the plugin screen 2. click add new 3. search Gutenberg 4. Install and activate Gutenberg (this is the development build of the editor) 5. Go back go plugin screen this time searching for Three Object Viewer 6. Add a post and type `/3D Environment` and select the 3D environment block 7. See webgl errors ### Code ```js // code goes here ``` ### Live example Don't have a live example since this only happens in the logged in editor view. ### Screenshots _No response_ ### Version 0.150.1 ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS MacOS

author: user080975

title: Make PointerLockControls inheritable
body:
### Description

Hi,
In one of my projects I was using the `PointerLockControls`, to easily implement the PointerLockAPI. I expanded the controls to be a kind of first-person movement, by creating another class around the `PointerLockControls` to implement the keyboard-movement. Doing this leads to hard-to-read code because eventlisteners have to be defined in multiple places and so on. I tried to inherit from the `PointerLockControls` but because all of the methods are implemented as properties inside of the constructor, they are not overridable.

### Solution

My solution would be to refactor the `PointerLockControls`-class so that the properties `connect`, `disconnect`, `dispose`, `getObject`, `getDirection`, `moveForward`, `moveRight`, `lock` and `unlock` become actual functions and not only lamdba-function-properties. The eventlisteners `onMouseMove`,`onPointerLockChange` and `onPointerLockError` could become properties of the class. Then they could be usable in the previously mentioned methods.

### Alternatives

1. Inheriting from `PointerLockControls` and rewriting the necessary methods. That is possible but it just doubles the code and kind of defeats the whole purpose of inheritance.
2. Reimplementing the necessary controls, without relying on the examples. Also possible but they are already there, so why not use them?

### Additional context

This is my first feature request (or even issue) for open source code ever. So if my desctiption wasn't verbose enough or is missing some essential parts, please let me know. Also I have yet to master JavaScript, so it's totally possible that I misregarded some features or best practices. If so, please don't hesitate to tell me.

title: Renderer should resize before entering an XR session to facilitate responsive pages
body:
### Description

I have a responsive page and a canvas in this page that resizes as the page size changes. All this works well. The aspect ratio of my 3D scene is maintained as the canvas is resized. 

When I enter XR mode, by using the VRButton class, things change. The canvas now occupies the whole device width and height, which makes sense. However the renderer has not been resized so my original 3D scene, when rendered in VR, gets stretched or squashed.

In the jsFiddle the screen height is divided into two equal parts and the scene, comprising just one sphere, is rendered in the lower half. Proportions are correct. Press the "Enter VR" button and the sphere is no longer spherical. It's so simple and basic I thought I must be doing something fundamentally wrong.

The problem can be avoided, sort of, by passing updateStyle = false to the original renderer.setSize function. But this destroys the responsiveness of the page. I'd like the canvas to continually resize as the screen size is changed. 

Would it not make sense to make a renderer.resize call once an xr session has been granted by the VRButton's onClick handler: 

`navigator.xr.requestSession( 'immersive-vr', sessionInit ).then( onSessionStarted );`

but before the session has been established by the VRButton's onSessionStarted() function: 

`await renderer.xr.setSession( session );`

After the session is established I can't resize since the session "isPresenting" flag is then true, inhibiting resize.

To fix this couldn't a resize call be added to onSessionStarted() function, something like this (but more general to account for the renderer.renderTarget) as in the snippet below?

### Reproduction steps

1. Create any page where the target for the renderer, in my case the canvas, does not take up the whole device  width and height.
2. Render a sphere on the canvas
3. Click the "Enter VR" button


### Code

     
    async function onSessionStarted( session ) {
       **renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);**
       
       session.addEventListener( 'end', onSessionEnded );

       await renderer.xr.setSession( session );
       button.textContent = 'EXIT VR';

       currentSession = session;
    }
 

### Live example

https://jsfiddle.net/fume1k7a/3/



### Screenshots

Before:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/224767559-19293c64-b147-4cb9-a556-828c0dd15905.png)

After:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/224767626-73e406bd-9678-42ba-9c9c-96c6dbe68bbe.png)



### Version

r147

author: AndyMGar

title: Texture from region of canvas
body:
### Description

It would be handy if `THREE.Texture` / `THREE.CanvasTexture` allowed you to create a texture from a subregion of a canvas (I'd imagine this would also be useful for images). I'm attempting to filter very large canvases using GLSL (with dimensions greater than MAX_TEXTURE_SIZE), so the canvases need to be tiled. I'd like to be able to create a set of `THREE.CanvasTexture` instances where each one references the same canvas, but a different subregion of that canvas.

### Solution

This looks to be possible with WebGL 2 using `pixelStorei` with `UNPACK_SKIP_ROWS` and `UNPACK_SKIP_PIXELS` (see https://stackoverflow.com/questions/20930466/webgl-is-there-an-efficient-way-to-upload-only-part-of-an-image-canvas-as-a-tex).

Perhaps these could be additional properties on `THREE.Texture`: `unpackSkipRows`, `unpackSkipPixels`.

### Alternatives

Currently I need to create a separate canvas for each "tile" texture.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Can't remove the old target in OrbitControls
body:
### Description

We currently have 2 movement types we switch between. One is an OrbitController derived from the OrbitControls movement by ThreeJS. The other is a self-written FirstPersonController. We switch between the 2 by a dropdown menu.

But currently, I'm walking into a problem where we still have a target in the camera, and we can't remove it entirely so that we can use our other FirstPersonController movement logic.

### Reproduction steps

1. Use the OrbitControls.
2. Set that bool to false.
3. Enable another movement controller.
4. Then you'll see that the camera still has a target and makes it so we can't use that new movement controller properly.

### Code

```js
Scene.instance.cameraController.orbitController.orbitControls.enabled = false;
Scene.instance.cameraController.orbitController.orbitControls.dispose();
Scene.instance.cameraController.orbitController.orbitControls.target = null; // This isn't possible to do, but is where we think the problem rises.
Scene.instance.cameraController.orbitController.orbitControls.target0 = null; // This isn't possible to do, but is where we think the problem rises.
```


### Live example

There isn't one, currently in a closed Development space.

### Screenshots

Here you can see we're able to use the orbit controls. But as soon as we switch, we can only still zoom to a certain target but aren't able to look around with the camera:
![OrbitControls (2)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/123364333/225600866-b229f1fe-db3c-46d6-a335-b124d1aa1aaa.gif)

This is how the FirstPersonController should act without any targets to look at:
![FirstPersonController (2)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/123364333/225602198-9ca7d811-a88a-4d73-bfb0-244090d80219.gif)


### Version

0.135.0

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

Windows

title: Warning for "multiple three.js instances import" in Edge
body:
### Description

Hi, I'm currently using VSCode, three.js v0.150.1 NPM import, with Vite bundler 4.1.0 and @vitejs/plugin-basic-ssl 1.0.1 for developing. While working fine in every other browser (Firefox, Chrome, etc...) I always get the warning: "WARNING: Multiple instances of Three.js being imported." in Edge, even though I only import three.js and no other three-dependency libraries. My first thought was, that maybe Vite does smth crazy, but the warning only appears in Edge browser. I'm using the sample code from the README.md. Happens on Glitch example too (link is attached).

It's not a major issue, but I thought it's worth mentioning it here.

br,
Max

### Reproduction steps

1. Setup a Vite project with `npm create vite@latest`
2. Enter project folder
3. `npm install` to install Vite and dependencies
4. `npm install three` to get three.js
5. Replace js code from main.js with sample code from README.md
6. (optional) install Vite basic SSL with `npm i @vitejs/plugin-basic-ssl --save-dev`
7. (optional) create vite.config.js in root and copy content from https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-basic-ssl
8. run `npx vite` from terminal in VSCode

OR

1. Create new Glitch site
2. Import three.js via import map
3. create a js script, import three and e.g. create a scene
4. running the site in Edge will show the multiple instances warning.

Will run fine in most browsers, but show the warning "WARNING: Multiple instances of Three.js being imported." in Edge.


### Code

```javascript
import * as THREE from 'three';
const scene = new THREE.Scene();
```

### Live example

* [https://edge-multiple-instances-warning.glitch.me/](https://edge-multiple-instances-warning.glitch.me/)

### Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23502690/225606936-d367f7e3-598e-4687-9f24-8fbed4dcc907.png)

title: r151 throws error when THREE-CustomShaderMaterial is based on materials that have a map
body:
### Description

Reference issue: https://github.com/FarazzShaikh/THREE-CustomShaderMaterial/issues/35
Because `#define USE_UV` no longer happens (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25740), this code https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/uv_pars_fragment.glsl.js#L4 never happens and `vUv` is now not defined. Any shaders or logic that previously relied on it will now break, e.g: https://github.com/FarazzShaikh/THREE-CustomShaderMaterial/blob/main/package/src/shaders.ts#L26


### Reproduction steps

1. Create base material that has a texture specified as its map
2. Create a CustomShaderMaterial using that material as the base material
3. Above works on 0.150.x, breaks on 0.151.x


### Code

```js
// roughly:
const material = new MeshStandardMaterial({ map: texture });
const extendedMaterial = new CustomShaderMaterial({ baseMaterial: material });
```


### Live example

title: SkeletonUtils `.retarget()` and `.retargetClip()` error with documented `SkeletonHelper` params
body:
### Description [SkeletonUtils Docs](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=skeleton#examples/en/utils/SkeletonUtils) indicate that `.retarget()` and `.retargetClip()` expect two `SkeletonHelper` as the source and target.
When I call `retarget()` with these arguments, I get an error: ``` ❌ SkeletonUtils.js:31 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'bones') ``` The traceback points here: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/a55464eacb39e9a781a91d7848eb7401621d1045/examples/jsm/utils/SkeletonUtils.js#L31-L32 `source.isObject3D` reads `true` for a SkeletonHelper, so it then tries to read `source.skeleton.bones` which is invalid for SkeletonHelper. Instead, bones are stored on `SkeletonHelper.bones`. I've tried to piece together what type(s) retarget is meant to accept. Taking a look at that `getBones` function: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/a55464eacb39e9a781a91d7848eb7401621d1045/examples/jsm/utils/SkeletonUtils.js#L485-L489 My guess is that retarget is written to accept: * `SkinnedMesh` (according to `source.skeleton.bones`) * `Bone[]` (according to `Array.isArray` in `getBones`) * `Skeleton` (according to `skeleton.bones` in `getBones`) I tried passing my `SkeletonHelper.bones` array instead, but that didn't seem to perform retargeting. Then I tried passing a `SkinnedMesh` and it sort of worked, but the retargeted pose was wrong. Then I tried passing a `Skeleton` and that worked as expected. I notice in the Fiddle shared in #25288, they had to explicitly assign a skeleton to `skeletonHelper.skeleton` . I get the impression that `retarget()` and `retargetClip()` are generally outdated, either in implementation or documentation. As @sunag mentioned in #25589 this could use an official example. I'd also like there to be some explanation of the `options` object. ### Reproduction steps 1. Load a humanoid animation and create a SkeletonHelper `helperSource` for it 2. Load a humanoid character and create a SkeletonHelper `helperTarget` for it 3. Call `SkeletonUtils.retarget(helperTarget, helperSource, {})` ### Code ```js const gltfSource = await loadGLTF(urlSource) const helperSource = new THREE.SkeletonHelper(gltfSource.scene) const gltfTarget = await loadGLTF(urlTarget) const helperTarget = new THREE.SkeletonHelper(gltfTarget.scene) SkeletonUtils.retarget(helperTarget, helperSource, {}) ``` ### Live example https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/h2473jf0/4/ ### Screenshots _No response_ ### Version r151 ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS MacOS

title: JavaScript wrapper for Three.js module
body:
### Description

From r160 Three.js will remove the plain vanilla JavaScript build of Three.js, and the module build must be used instead.

However, rewriting existing JavaScript applications to JavaScript modules can be a lot of work. 

### Solution

I therefore propose that a plain JavaScript wrapper be implemented to support existing users of Three.js who want to continue using Three.js in the classic way.

### Alternatives

Rewriting the game to JavaScript modules is too much work at the moment. I am also considering a legacy JavaScript fork of Three.js :)

- Alternative: support legacy JavaScript compatibility forever.

### Additional context

I am currently making www.fciv.net which is a 3D fork of Freeciv: https://github.com/fciv-net/fciv-net

title: GLTFLoader: Order of nodes in hierarchy is non-deterministic in some cases when compression is used
body:
### Description

When authoring models that use transparency for real-time use, often the order of nodes in the hierarchy is the only option for artists to control transparency sorting. However, it looks like when compression (draco) is used, the hierarchy resulting from a file is non-deterministic - sometimes node A comes before node B and sometimes after, potentially altering the resulting appearance.

The attached file exhibits this behaviour in three-based viewers, e.g.
- https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/
- https://three.needle.tools/examples/?q=multiple#webgl_loader_multiple

[TransparencyOrder.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/11215443/TransparencyOrder.zip)

Video of the issue: dropping the same file results in different results on different attempts. For some reason, after a few attempts the result "stabilizes" until the page is reloaded.
https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2693840/231571409-6cb75554-bd70-4419-ae6a-ea30b7b71646.mp4

Looking at the resulting scene hierarchy, in some cases "Interior" comes after "Exterior" but sometimes the order is reverted. This seems to only happen when compression is used.

Curiously, I can't get this to reproduce in https://modelviewer.dev/editor/; @elalish are you using custom transparency sorting?

### Reproduction steps

1. Open https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/ or other viewers that use default three.js GLTFLoader + rendering
2. Drop the file attached above
3. Drop the file _again_ - in some cases you'll see an appearance change
4. Drop the file _again_ - appearance will likely change back
5. Refresh the page and repeat 2-4; in some cases one version will already be present at the start, in others subsequent loading will show it

problem's when you want both renderer in same site

author: stevexbritton

title: Wrong filters applied when switching shadow map types
body:
### Description

Related PR:
[https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24223](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24223) _("Ensures VSM can not just be used when enabling it at application start but also **if someone switched from another shadow mapping type** like PCF to VSM.")_.

Problem: 
The `mapPass` bug was fixed with that PR, but the `shadow.map.texture` filters are not switched. 

### Reproduction steps

In my little demo app below, VSM shadows are correct  (even after switching forth and back) if and only if the type was already set to VSM initially. They are wrong if the initial type was something else.

[In this code line](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLShadowMap.js#L136) it appears that the default filter (`LinearFilter`) is meant to be applied to VSM shadow map textures, and `NearestFilter` for the other types.

But these filters are only applied initially (`if (shadow.map === null`), so switching the shadow type doesn't have an effect on the filters.

### Code


My suggestion would be to not only check for null, but also if there has been a recent switch from or to VSM (disposing the previous map before creating a new one):

```js
...

let _previousType = this.type;

this.render = function ( lights, scene, camera ) {

  ...

  const switchedToVSM = _previousType !== VSMShadowMap && this.type === VSMShadowMap;
  const switchedFromVSM = _previousType === VSMShadowMap && this.type !== VSMShadowMap;

  if ( shadow.map === null || switchedToVSM || switchedFromVSM ) {

    const pars = ( this.type !== VSMShadowMap ) ? { minFilter: NearestFilter, magFilter: NearestFilter } : {};

    shadow.map?.dispose();
    shadow.map = new WebGLRenderTarget( _shadowMapSize.x, _shadowMapSize.y, pars );
    shadow.map.texture.name = light.name + '.shadowMap';

    shadow.camera.updateProjectionMatrix();

  }

  _previousType = this.type;

  ...
```



BTW: 

Is the different treatment of the shadow types even necessary? To me, the non-VSM shadows look the same, no matter whether they have `NearestFilter`s applied or not. And since VSM shadows don't work with `NearestFilter`s, why not just omit the `NearestFilter`s at all? - But it may very well be that I overlooked something(?)

### Live example

Here is a small demo app showing the reported issue (comment/uncomment code line 21 and select VSM in the app to see the difference):

[jsfiddle demo](https://jsfiddle.net/a6vnsbym/)

### Screenshots

Here are two screenshots from the fiddle, showing a VSM shadow with a directional light.

1. Correct VSM shadow (if VSM was applied initially - even after switching forth and back between different types):

![vsm-correct](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/130747909/232009748-3b8b95d7-f48e-4367-b52c-803e747bdb53.jpg)

2. Wrong VSM shadow (if a different shadow type was applied initially):

![vsm-wrong](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/130747909/232009788-1f8b388d-4fad-41bf-9593-82e6bcf099df.jpg)


### Version

r151

### Device

_No response_

### Browser

_No response_

### OS

_No response_

title: GroundProjecedEnv Module Not Found
body:
### Description I've just installed a new SvelteKit app and installed Three and Threlte libraries. Once I've set up everything, I tested it in my dev server. Following the video example by [Joy of Code](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=89LYeHOncVk&t=2090s), with the goal to import a .glb file and render it to screen. Once I run the dev server, I get an error saying, "Cannot find module 'three/examples/jsm/objects/GroundProjectedEnv' from my local working directory. After reading that, I looked in my local installed pkg for ThreeJS and found no file exists. After that, I found this pull request [Examples: Rename GroundProjectedEnv to GroundProjectedSkybox. #25645](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25645). I changed that local file `GroundProjectedSkyBox` to the name it was searching for and everything started working as normal. Also changed the exported classes to reflect that name. This is my first issue with a public repository like this, so I hope I did my best here to provide information or hope this is worth the issue. But I felt like it was a good first chance to try this, if there is any more information needed I can provide that. ### Reproduction steps 1. npm create svelte 2. npm i 3. npm i @threlte/core @threlte/extras three 4. npm i -D @types/three 5. followed Joy of Code Example 6. Added a GLTF component to render my ThreeJS.glb file I made with Adobe Dimension. 7. npm run dev 8. After compile, it breaks and produces that error mentioned above. ### Code ```svelte

For a given gltf model, gltf imports are working absolutely fine on slower computers, but texture loading fails reliably in Chrome on faster computers (for the same model). 

### Reproduction steps

I purchased this gltf model from Sketchfab for testing purposes: https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/mountain-dragon-animations-347cae9f87104374bc3ac554d9d3fd6e.

Since the problem may be related to specific file sizes, resp. file sizes ratio, here are the model's (sorted) file sizes:

```
 15 KB   emissive map
256 KB   gltf 
  9 MB   diffuse map
 11 MB   normal map
 13 MB   bin
 21 MB   ao/metallic/roughness map
```

I've converted the gltf folder to a glb file using this tool: https://github.com/CesiumGS/gltf-pipeline.

The behavior summarized above is exactly the same, no matter whether I use https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/ (screenshots 1, 3, 5), https://threejs.org/editor/ (`autosave` disabled, screenshots 2, 4, 6), or my own model viewer, built with threejs, where I first discovered this problem:

1. On my faster computers (a workstation and a fairly fast consumer computer), loading the `ao/metallic/roughness map` fails (almost) always (screenshots 1 and 2).
2. Sometimes the `normal map` doesn't load either on these computers (screenshots 3 and 4). 
3. However, when I set GPU throttling to `6x slowdown` in the Performance tab, texture loading works without any problems (screenshots 5 and 6). - BTW: Network throttling or disabling the cache has no effect.
4. The problem never occurs on any of my slower computers, and the problem never occurs when using Firefox, even on my faster computers (maybe since FF seems to be slower in this regard anyway?).
5. I also made the same tests with the original model folder (not converted to glb). The only difference is that on my faster devices slowing down the GPU speed artificially (see 3.) doesn't help (but 1., 2. and 4. behave exactly like above).

I used several Windows 10/11 computers with the latest Chrome (112.0.5615.138) for my tests.

Since my test model is not available for free on Sketchfab, I guess, I'm not allowed to share it here publicly.

title: Docs: Broken formatting on some method signatures
body:
### Description Unfortunately, applying Prettier to the HTML documentation has changed the HTML in ways that appear the same on the page, but are *not* the same for the purposes of the Regular Expressions in [`page.js`](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/docs/page.js). I'm overall in favor of using Prettier, it's a great way to keep the HTML clean, but we may need to adjust `page.js` to handle these newlines (or, eventually, find another way of formatting our API docs). ### Reproduction steps View any method documentation with a longer signature, like Color.fromBufferAttribute, on the `dev` branch. ### Code ```html

[method:this fromBufferAttribute]( [param:BufferAttribute attribute], [param:Integer index] )

[method:this fromBufferAttribute]( [param:BufferAttribute attribute], [param:Integer index] )

``` ### Live example n/a ### Screenshots Note the return value (`: this`) is on the wrong side of the parameter list: ![Screenshot 2023-04-24 at 11 06 42 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1848368/234164881-b326594f-36bc-47cf-abd8-b70f0fa3b0e7.png) ### Version r152dev ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS MacOS
author: donmccurdy

title: HTMLMesh: hover on button elements in VR
body:
### Description

Style :hover on button elements doesn't work in VR. That's because `window.getComputedStyle( element )` doesn't get updated once you are in a immersive-vr session.

### Solution

Save the hover styles before entering VR, so we can apply the correct style on mouseenter/mouseleave by adding a hover class on the button.
For this to work, you need to have some css rules like that :
```
button:hover,
button.hover {
  background-color: red
}
```

### Alternatives

You can modify on the fly all the loaded stylesheets to add the `button.hover` variant like it has been done on other aframe ui components, but I find that very intrusive.

### Additional context

I create this issue for visibility. I did some months ago a PR on aframe-htmlmesh https://github.com/AdaRoseCannon/aframe-htmlmesh/pull/9 to implement this. This works great, but could be generalized to other elements than button, and use something different than using data attributes to keep a list of css properties with their value. I'm not planning to work on it right now. If you have suggestions, you can comment on the PR.
author: vincentfretin

title: Quaternion Serialisation Changed
body:
### Description

Quaternions are now serialised in a way that is indistinguishable from Vector4.  

They have changed from being an object to an array in exports.  There is no way to know which data type is required / used when loading exported data or inspecting log dumps.

This breaks all of our tooling.

### Reproduction steps

1.  Attempt to serialise a quaternion
2.  Inspect the output.



### Code

They used to be serialised by default as:

```js
"rotation": Quaternion {
  "_w": 1,
  "_x": 0,
  "_y": 0,
  "_z": 0,
  "isQuaternion": true,
},
```

Now they are serialised as

```js
"rotation": [
  0,
  0,
  0,
  1,
],
```

### Live example

Provided above.

author: RemusMar

title: WebGLBackground: `renderer.autoClear` has no effect when an XR session is active
body:
### Description

Since r152 the `renderer.autoClear` flag is effectively ignored when in an XR session. This seems to be a result of changes made as part of #25781. The code in `WebGLBackground` now force clears if there is an XR session.

Relevant code: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLBackground.js#L47-L66
(note that the switch case is exhaustive as per WebXR spec, and all cases set `forceClear`)

This causes a problem when the frame is rendered using multiple `renderer.render()` calls. Setting `renderer.autoClear = false` no longer suffices in that case. It can be worked around by setting the individual `autoClearColor`, `autoClearDepth` and `autoClearStencil`, as these are still honoured, but I believe the behaviour differing between non-XR and XR is a bug (or at least not intuitive).

### Reproduction steps

1. Disable `autoClear`
2. Render a frame with more than one `render()` call (e.g. `clear()`, `render(object1)`, `render(object2)`)
3. Start an XR session and see only the result of the last `render()` call


### Code

```js
renderer.autoClear = false;

renderer.clear();
renderer.render(object1, camera);
renderer.render(object2, camera);
```


### Live example

Example: https://jsfiddle.net/br1nygdk/2/

The example shows two spheres; one cyan and one magenta. When clicking 'Enter VR' only the magenta sphere is visible, whereas both should be visible. Setting `autoClearColor` to false works around the issue.

(Note: it's not possible to enter VR in the fiddle on a Quest, but the immersive-web-emulator can be used as well)

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r152

### Device

Headset

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

Android
author: mrxz

title: CSM constructor cannot take null parameters
body:
### Description

The CSM constructor in the cascaded shadowmap addon is written as if a null (or undefined) parameter object is a valid use case:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/csm/CSM.js#L27

However, in practice this will just lead to a null dereference a few lines later in the code:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/jsm/csm/CSM.js#L69

Either it should allow a null (or undefined) parameter, or it should fail early and not pretend that it works at first, thus obfuscating the actual problem.

I don't know which of the two matches the API design philosophy in this project.

### Reproduction steps

`new CSM()`
